structures.desc: Forming past simple questions with did + base form and was/were, including short answers.

rules.t: For most verbs, form questions with Did + subject + base form. Did you enjoy the film?
rules.td: Bei den meisten Verben bildet man Fragen mit Did + Subjekt + Grundform. Did you enjoy the film?

ex.en: Did you like the party?
ex.de: Hat dir die Party gefallen?

ex.en: Did she buy a new jacket?
ex.de: Hat sie eine neue Jacke gekauft?

rules.t: For 'be', put was/were before the subject. Do NOT use 'did' with was/were.
rules.td: Bei 'be' stellt man was/were vor das Subjekt. Man verwendet NICHT 'did' mit was/were.

ex.en: Was he happy?
ex.de: War er glücklich?

ex.en: Were they at school?
ex.de: Waren sie in der Schule?

rules.t: Short answers use the auxiliary: Yes, I did. / No, she didn't. / Yes, he was. / No, they weren't.
rules.td: Kurzantworten verwenden das Hilfsverb: Yes, I did. / No, she didn't. / Yes, he was. / No, they weren't.

ex.en: Did you go? — Yes, I did.
ex.de: Bist du gegangen? — Ja.

ex.en: Was she embarrassed? — No, she wasn't.
ex.de: War sie verlegen? — Nein, war sie nicht.

errs.d: Using past form after 'did' instead of base form
errs.w: Did you went to the cinema?
errs.c: Did you go to the cinema?

structures.desc: Additional irregular past simple forms: buy-bought, know-knew, make-made, write-wrote, expanding the M1 set.

rules.t: These irregular verbs do not follow the -ed pattern. Their past forms must be learnt: buy→bought, know→knew, make→made, write→wrote.
rules.td: Diese unregelmäßigen Verben folgen nicht dem -ed-Muster. Ihre Vergangenheitsformen muss man lernen: buy→bought, know→knew, make→made, write→wrote.

ex.en: She bought a new dress yesterday.
ex.de: Sie hat gestern ein neues Kleid gekauft.

ex.en: I knew the answer.
ex.de: Ich wusste die Antwort.

rules.t: In negative sentences and questions, use the base form (not the past form): didn't buy, didn't know.
rules.td: In verneinten Sätzen und Fragen verwendet man die Grundform (nicht die Vergangenheitsform): didn't buy, didn't know.

ex.en: He didn't make any mistakes.
ex.de: Er hat keine Fehler gemacht.

ex.en: Did she write an email?
ex.de: Hat sie eine E-Mail geschrieben?

structures.desc: Using should/shouldn't to give advice and make suggestions. No 3rd person -s, no 'to' after should.

rules.t: should/shouldn't + base form (infinitive without 'to'). The form is the same for all persons.
rules.td: should/shouldn't + Grundform (Infinitiv ohne 'to'). Die Form ist für alle Personen gleich.

ex.en: You should wear a helmet.
ex.de: Du solltest einen Helm tragen.

ex.en: He should study more.
ex.de: Er sollte mehr lernen.

rules.t: Negative: shouldn't + base form. Meaning: it is not a good idea.
rules.td: Verneinung: shouldn't + Grundform. Bedeutung: Es ist keine gute Idee.

ex.en: You shouldn't eat so many sweets.
ex.de: Du solltest nicht so viele Süsigkeiten essen.

ex.en: She shouldn't stay out late.
ex.de: Sie sollte nicht so lange draußen bleiben.

rules.t: Questions: Should + subject + base form?
rules.td: Fragen: Should + Subjekt + Grundform?

ex.en: Should I phone my parents?
ex.de: Soll ich meine Eltern anrufen?

ex.en: What should we do?
ex.de: Was sollen wir tun?

negative: You shouldn't eat so many sweets.

errs.w: You should to wear a costume.

structures.desc: Comparing two things: short adjectives + -er, long adjectives with 'more', and irregular forms (better, worse).

rules.t: One-syllable adjectives: add -er (+ than). Spelling: CVC → double final consonant (big→bigger); -e → just add -r (nice→nicer).
rules.td: Einsilbige Adjektive: -er anhängen (+ than). Rechtschreibung: KVK → Verdopplung (big→bigger); -e → nur -r (nice→nicer).

ex.en: A cheetah is faster than a lion.
ex.de: Ein Gepard ist schneller als ein Löwe.

ex.en: This box is bigger than that one.
ex.de: Diese Schachtel ist größer als jene.

rules.t: Adjectives with three or more syllables use 'more' + adjective + than.
rules.td: Adjektive mit drei oder mehr Silben verwenden 'more' + Adjektiv + than.

ex.en: A snake is more dangerous than a mouse.
ex.de: Eine Schlange ist gefährlicher als eine Maus.

ex.en: This book is more interesting than that one.
ex.de: Dieses Buch ist interessanter als jenes.

rules.t: Two-syllable adjectives ending in -y: change -y to -ier (happy→happier, heavy→heavier).
rules.td: Zweisilbige Adjektive auf -y: -y wird zu -ier (happy→happier, heavy→heavier).

ex.en: She is happier than before.
ex.de: Sie ist glücklicher als vorher.

ex.en: This bag is heavier than that one.
ex.de: Diese Tasche ist schwerer als jene.

rules.t: Irregular comparatives: good→better, bad→worse. These must be learnt by heart.
rules.td: Unregelmäßige Komparativformen: good→better, bad→worse. Diese muss man auswendig lernen.

ex.en: She is better at maths than me.
ex.de: Sie ist besser in Mathe als ich.

ex.en: The weather is worse today.
ex.de: Das Wetter ist heute schlechter.

affirmative: A cheetah is faster than a lion.
This is more dangerous than that.
questions: Is a cheetah faster than a lion?

errs.w: A lion is more fast than a turtle.
errs.c: A lion is faster than a turtle.

errs.w: She is more bigger than her sister.
errs.c: She is bigger than her sister.

errs.d: Forgetting to double the final consonant
errs.w: The elephant is biger than the horse.
errs.c: The elephant is bigger than the horse.

structures.desc: Expressing the highest degree: the + -est for short adjectives, the most for long adjectives, irregular forms (the best, the worst).

rules.t: One-syllable adjectives: the + adjective-est. Spelling rules apply (big→the biggest, nice→the nicest).
rules.td: Einsilbige Adjektive: the + Adjektiv-est. Rechtschreibregeln beachten (big→the biggest, nice→the nicest).

ex.en: The cheetah is the fastest animal.
ex.de: Der Gepard ist das schnellste Tier.

ex.en: Mount Everest is the highest mountain.
ex.de: Der Mount Everest ist der höchste Berg.

rules.t: Adjectives with three or more syllables: the most + adjective.
rules.td: Adjektive mit drei oder mehr Silben: the most + Adjektiv.

ex.en: It's the most dangerous animal in the world.
ex.de: Es ist das gefährlichste Tier der Welt.

ex.en: This is the most beautiful place.
ex.de: Das ist der schönste Ort.

rules.t: Irregular superlatives: good→the best, bad→the worst.
rules.td: Unregelmäßige Superlativformen: good→the best, bad→the worst.

ex.en: She is the best student in the class.
ex.de: Sie ist die beste Schülerin der Klasse.

ex.en: That was the worst film ever.
ex.de: Das war der schlechteste Film aller Zeiten.

affirmative: The cheetah is the fastest animal.
This is the most dangerous snake.

errs.w: He is fastest runner in the school.
errs.c: He is the fastest runner in the school.

errs.w: The blue whale is the most biggest animal.
errs.c: The blue whale is the biggest animal.

structures.desc: Expressing equality (as...as) and inequality (not as...as) between two things.

rules.t: as + adjective (base form) + as = two things are equal.
rules.td: as + Adjektiv (Grundform) + as = zwei Dinge sind gleich.

ex.en: A rhino is as dangerous as a hippo.
ex.de: Ein Nashorn ist so gefährlich wie ein Nilpferd.

ex.en: Tom is as tall as his brother.
ex.de: Tom ist so gross wie sein Bruder.

rules.t: not as + adjective + as = the first thing has LESS of the quality than the second.
rules.td: not as + Adjektiv + as = das erste Ding hat WENIGER von der Eigenschaft als das zweite.

ex.en: A leopard is not as fast as a cheetah.
ex.de: Ein Leopard ist nicht so schnell wie ein Gepard.

ex.en: This hotel isn't as expensive as that one.
ex.de: Dieses Hotel ist nicht so teuer wie jenes.

affirmative: She is as tall as her sister.
negative: He isn't as fast as a cheetah.
This isn't as good as that.
questions: Is she as tall as her brother?

errs.w: She is so fast as a cheetah.
errs.c: She is as fast as a cheetah.

errs.d: Using comparative form instead of base form between as...as
errs.w: He is as taller as his dad.
errs.c: He is as tall as his dad.

errs.w: She is as fast like a cheetah.
errs.c: She is as fast as a cheetah.

structures.desc: Expanded prepositions (opposite, beside, round the corner) combined with direction-giving language (go straight, turn left, cross).

rules.t: New location prepositions: opposite (directly across from), beside (next to), round the corner, on the corner of.
rules.td: Neue Ortspräpositionen: opposite (gegenüber), beside (neben), round the corner (um die Ecke), on the corner of (an der Ecke von).

ex.en: The cinema is opposite the bank.
ex.de: Das Kino ist gegenüber der Bank.

ex.en: The shop is round the corner.
ex.de: Das Geschäft ist um die Ecke.

rules.t: Direction language uses imperatives + prepositions: Go straight ahead, Turn left/right, Go past the..., Cross the bridge, Walk as far as the church.
rules.td: Wegbeschreibungen verwenden Imperative + Präpositionen: Go straight ahead, Turn left/right, Go past the..., Cross the bridge, Walk as far as the church.

ex.en: Go straight ahead and turn left at the traffic lights.
ex.de: Geh geradeaus und biege bei der Ampel links ab.

ex.en: Go past the post office and it's on your right.
ex.de: Geh an der Post vorbei und es ist auf deiner rechten Seite.

rules.t: Polite phrases for asking directions: Excuse me, how do I get to...? / Excuse me, I'm trying to find...
rules.td: Höfliche Ausdrücke beim Wegfragen: Excuse me, how do I get to...? / Excuse me, I'm trying to find...

ex.en: Excuse me, how do I get to the station?
ex.de: Entschuldigung, wie komme ich zum Bahnhof?

ex.en: Excuse me, I'm trying to find the supermarket.
ex.de: Entschuldigung, ich suche den Supermarkt.

questions: How do I get to the station?

structures.desc: Expressing necessity (have to) and absence of necessity (don't have to). 3rd person: has to / doesn't have to. CRITICAL: don't have to is NOT mustn't.

rules.t: have to / has to + base form = it is necessary. 3rd person singular: has to.
rules.td: have to / has to + Grundform = es ist notwendig. 3. Person Einzahl: has to.

ex.en: You have to wear a helmet.
ex.de: Du musst einen Helm tragen.

ex.en: She has to get up early.
ex.de: Sie muss früh aufstehen.

rules.t: don't have to / doesn't have to = it is NOT necessary (but you can if you want). This is NOT the same as mustn't!
rules.td: don't have to / doesn't have to = es ist NICHT notwendig (aber du darfst). Das ist NICHT dasselbe wie mustn't!

ex.en: You don't have to bring food. (= not necessary)
ex.de: Du musst kein Essen mitbringen. (= nicht notwendig)

ex.en: He doesn't have to wear a uniform.
ex.de: Er muss keine Uniform tragen.

rules.t: Questions: Do/Does + subject + have to + base form?
rules.td: Fragen: Do/Does + Subjekt + have to + Grundform?

ex.en: Do you have to wear a uniform?
ex.de: Musst du eine Uniform tragen?

ex.en: Does she have to get up early?
ex.de: Muss sie früh aufstehen?

affirmative: She has to wear a helmet.
negative: You don't have to bring food.
He doesn't have to wash up.
questions: Do I have to bring food?
Does she have to wear a uniform?

errs.c: You have to wear a helmet.

structures.desc: Expressing possibility and uncertainty with might/might not. No 3rd person -s, no 'to'. Same form for all persons.

rules.t: might + base form (no 'to', no -s). Same form for all persons: I might, he might, they might.
rules.td: might + Grundform (kein 'to', kein -s). Gleiche Form für alle Personen: I might, he might, they might.

ex.en: I might go to the cinema tonight.
ex.de: Ich gehe vielleicht heute Abend ins Kino.

ex.en: She might come later.
ex.de: Sie kommt vielleicht später.

rules.t: might not + base form = something is possibly not going to happen. 'might not' is more common than 'mightn't' at A2.
rules.td: might not + Grundform = etwas wird möglicherweise nicht passieren.

ex.en: I might not finish in time.
ex.de: Ich werde es vielleicht nicht rechtzeitig schaffen.

ex.en: It might not rain tomorrow.
ex.de: Es regnet morgen vielleicht nicht.

errs.w: I might to go to the cinema.
errs.c: I might go to the cinema.

errs.w: I might went to the party.
errs.c: I might go to the party.

structures.nd: Zeitangaben in der Vergangenheit und Erzählstruktur
structures.desc: Using time markers (then, after that, finally, suddenly) and time expressions to sequence past narratives.

rules.t: Use time markers to structure narratives in order: First, ... Then, ... After that, ... Finally, ...
rules.td: Verwende Zeitwörter, um Erzählungen zu ordnen: First, ... Then, ... After that, ... Finally, ...

ex.en: First, we went to the museum. Then, we had lunch.
ex.de: Zuerst gingen wir ins Museum. Dann assen wir zu Mittag.

ex.en: After that, we walked home. Finally, we watched TV.
ex.de: Danach gingen wir nach Hause. Schließlich sahen wir fern.

rules.t: Common time expressions for past narratives: yesterday, last week, two days ago, in 2013, one day, suddenly, half an hour ago.
rules.td: Häufige Zeitangaben für Vergangenheitserzählungen: yesterday, last week, two days ago, in 2013, one day, suddenly, half an hour ago.

ex.en: Suddenly, a strange light appeared.
ex.de: Plötzlich erschien ein seltsames Licht.

ex.en: One day, an astronaut found something amazing.
ex.de: Eines Tages fand ein Astronaut etwas Erstaunliches.

rules.t: Time markers usually go at the beginning of a sentence, followed by a comma. Time expressions can go at the beginning or end.
rules.td: Zeitwörter stehen meist am Satzanfang, gefolgt von einem Komma. Zeitangaben können am Anfang oder Ende stehen.

ex.en: Then, he ran to the door.
ex.de: Dann rannte er zur Tür.

ex.en: He ran to the door two hours ago.
ex.de: Er rannte vor zwei Stunden zur Tür.

errs.d: Incorrect order of time markers in narrative
errs.w: Finally, we left. Then, we arrived.
errs.c: First, we left. Then, we arrived.

errs.d: Mixing tenses within a past narrative
errs.w: First, we went to the park. Then we play football.
errs.c: First, we went to the park. Then we played football.

errs.d: V2 word order after time marker (L1 transfer)

structures.desc: Using some in positive sentences and offers, any in negative sentences and questions.

rules.t: Use 'some' in positive sentences: There are some apples. I'd like some water.
rules.td: Verwende 'some' in positiven Sätzen: There are some apples. I'd like some water.

ex.en: There are some tomatoes in the fridge.
ex.de: Es sind ein paar Tomaten im Kühlschrank.

ex.en: I need some help.
ex.de: Ich brauche etwas Hilfe.

rules.t: Use 'any' in negative sentences and questions: There aren't any onions. Are there any mushrooms?
rules.td: Verwende 'any' in verneinten Sätzen und Fragen: There aren't any onions. Are there any mushrooms?

ex.en: I haven't got any money.
ex.de: Ich habe kein Geld.

ex.en: Are there any seats left?
ex.de: Sind noch Plätze frei?

rules.t: EXCEPTION: Use 'some' in offers and requests (not 'any'): Would you like some tea?
rules.td: AUSNAHME: Verwende 'some' bei Angeboten und Bitten (nicht 'any'): Would you like some tea?

ex.en: Would you like some cake?
ex.de: Möchtest du etwas Kuchen?

ex.en: Can I have some water, please?
ex.de: Kann ich bitte etwas Wasser haben?

affirmative: There are some books on the table.

structures.desc: Using one (singular) and ones (plural) as pronoun substitutes to avoid repeating a noun.

rules.t: 'one' replaces a singular countable noun to avoid repetition.
rules.td: 'one' ersetzt ein zäliges Nomen im Singular, um Wiederholung zu vermeiden.

ex.en: Which pizza do you want? — The big one.
ex.de: Welche Pizza möchtest du? — Die grosse.

ex.en: I don't like this hat. I prefer the blue one.
ex.de: Ich mag diesen Hut nicht. Ich bevorzuge den blauen.

rules.t: 'ones' replaces a plural countable noun.
rules.td: 'ones' ersetzt ein zäliges Nomen im Plural.

ex.en: Which shoes do you want? — The red ones.
ex.de: Welche Schuhe möchtest du? — Die roten.

ex.en: These cakes are nice, but I prefer those ones.
ex.de: Diese Kuchen sind gut, aber ich bevorzuge jene.

errs.d: Using 'one' for plural nouns or 'ones' for singular
errs.w: I like the red one. (referring to plural shoes)

structures.desc: Expressing enjoyment of activities using like/likes + verb-ing (gerund).

rules.t: like + verb-ing to talk about activities you enjoy: I like reading. She likes dancing.
rules.td: like + verb-ing um über Aktivitäten zu sprechen, die man gerne macht: I like reading. She likes dancing.

ex.en: I like swimming in the lake.
ex.de: Ich schwimme gerne im See.

ex.en: She likes playing the guitar.
ex.de: Sie spielt gerne Gitarre.

rules.t: Spelling rules for -ing: run→running (double consonant), dance→dancing (drop -e), swim→swimming, write→writing.
rules.td: Rechtschreibregeln für -ing: run→running (Verdopplung), dance→dancing (-e fällt weg), swim→swimming, write→writing.

ex.en: He likes running in the park.
ex.de: Er läuft gerne im Park.

ex.en: We don't like getting up early.
ex.de: Wir stehen nicht gerne früh auf.

rules.t: Negative: don't like / doesn't like + verb-ing.
rules.td: Verneinung: don't like / doesn't like + verb-ing.

ex.en: I don't like cooking.
ex.de: Ich koche nicht gerne.

ex.en: She doesn't like going shopping.
ex.de: Sie geht nicht gerne einkaufen.

negative: She doesn't like getting up early.

errs.d: Using base form instead of -ing after like

structures.desc: must for strong obligation, mustn't for prohibition. CRITICAL: mustn't (nicht dürfen) is NOT the same as don't have to (nicht müssen).

rules.t: must + base form = strong obligation. No -s for 3rd person, no 'to'. Same form for all persons.
rules.td: must + Grundform = starke Verpflichtung. Kein -s in der 3. Person, kein 'to'. Gleiche Form für alle Personen.

ex.en: You must wear a seatbelt.
ex.de: Du musst einen Sicherheitsgurt tragen.

ex.en: She must be home by eight.
ex.de: Sie muss um acht zu Hause sein.

rules.t: mustn't + base form = prohibition (it is forbidden). mustn't = nicht duerfen in German.
rules.td: mustn't + Grundform = Verbot (es ist verboten). mustn't = nicht dürfen.

ex.en: You mustn't use your phone in class.
ex.de: Du darfst dein Handy im Unterricht nicht verwenden.

ex.en: He mustn't be late.
ex.de: Er darf nicht zu spät kommen.

rules.t: CRITICAL DISTINCTION: mustn't = forbidden (nicht duerfen). don't have to = not necessary (nicht muessen). These are NOT interchangeable!
rules.td: WICHTIGE UNTERSCHEIDUNG: mustn't = verboten (nicht dürfen). don't have to = nicht notwendig (nicht müssen). Diese sind NICHT austauschbar!

ex.en: You mustn't run in the corridor. (= forbidden!)
ex.de: Du darfst nicht im Gang laufen. (= verboten!)

ex.en: You don't have to bring lunch. (= not necessary)
ex.de: Du musst kein Mittagessen mitbringen. (= nicht notwendig)

negative: You mustn't run in the corridor.

errs.w: You must to go home now.

errs.d: Confusing mustn't with don't have to
errs.w: You mustn't bring lunch. (meaning: not necessary)
errs.c: You don't have to bring lunch.

structures.desc: Expanded possessive 's including singular possessive ('s), plural possessive (s'), and irregular plural possessive (children's).

rules.t: Singular nouns: add 's — the boy's book, Tom's bike, my sister's room.
rules.td: Nomen im Singular: 's anhängen — the boy's book, Tom's bike, my sister's room.

ex.en: This is Tom's bike.
ex.de: Das ist Toms Fahrrad.

ex.en: My sister's room is very tidy.
ex.de: Das Zimmer meiner Schwester ist sehr ordentlich.

rules.t: Regular plural nouns (ending in -s): add only an apostrophe — the boys' room, my parents' car.
rules.td: Regelmäßige Pluralnomen (auf -s endend): nur Apostroph — the boys' room, my parents' car.

ex.en: The boys' room is messy.
ex.de: Das Zimmer der Jungen ist unordentlich.

ex.en: My parents' car is new.
ex.de: Das Auto meiner Eltern ist neu.

rules.t: Irregular plural nouns (not ending in -s): add 's — the children's toys, the women's team.
rules.td: Unregelmäßige Pluralnomen (nicht auf -s endend): 's anhängen — the children's toys, the women's team.

ex.en: The children's toys are everywhere.
ex.de: Das Spielzeug der Kinder ist überall.

ex.en: The women's team won the match.
ex.de: Die Frauenmannschaft hat das Spiel gewonnen.

questions: Whose bike is this? — It's Tom's.

errs.d: Wrong apostrophe placement for plural possessive

structures.desc: Possessive pronouns (mine, yours, his, hers, ours, theirs) as standalone forms, contrasted with possessive adjectives.

rules.t: Possessive pronouns stand alone (no noun after them): mine, yours, his, hers, ours, theirs.
rules.td: Possessivpronomen stehen allein (kein Nomen danach): mine, yours, his, hers, ours, theirs.

ex.en: This bag is mine.
ex.de: Diese Tasche gehört mir.

ex.en: Is this yours?
ex.de: Ist das deins?

rules.t: Possessive adjective + noun vs possessive pronoun alone: my book → mine, your pen → yours, her bag → hers.
rules.td: Possessives Adjektiv + Nomen vs Possessivpronomen allein: my book → mine, your pen → yours, her bag → hers.

ex.en: That is my book. → That book is mine.
ex.de: Das ist mein Buch. → Das Buch gehört mir.

ex.en: Is this her phone? → Is this phone hers?
ex.de: Ist das ihr Handy? → Gehört dieses Handy ihr?

errs.d: Using possessive adjective instead of pronoun
Using possessive pronoun before a noun
Wrong form for 'hers' (adding apostrophe or using 'her')
errs.w: This is her's. / This is her.

structures.desc: who for subject questions (no auxiliary needed) and whose for possession questions.

rules.t: who as subject = no auxiliary needed. 'Who plays football?' (NOT 'Who does play football?').
rules.td: who als Subjekt = kein Hilfsverb nötig. 'Who plays football?' (NICHT 'Who does play football?').

ex.en: Who discovered America?
ex.de: Wer hat Amerika entdeckt?

ex.en: Who lives next door?
ex.de: Wer wohnt nebenan?

rules.t: whose asks about possession: Whose + noun + be? or Whose + be + this?
rules.td: whose fragt nach dem Besitz: Whose + Nomen + be? oder Whose + be + this?

ex.en: Whose book is this?
ex.de: Wessen Buch ist das?

ex.en: Whose are these shoes?
ex.de: Wem gehören diese Schuhe?

errs.d: Adding unnecessary auxiliary with who-subject questions

structures.desc: have/has + past participle for recent events with present relevance. Includes 'just' for very recent completion.

rules.t: have/has + past participle (3rd form). I/you/we/they have ('ve); he/she/it has ('s).
rules.td: have/has + Partizip Perfekt (3. Form). I/you/we/they have ('ve); he/she/it has ('s).

ex.en: I've broken my arm.
ex.de: Ich habe mir den Arm gebrochen.

ex.en: She has hurt her knee.
ex.de: Sie hat sich das Knie verletzt.

rules.t: 'just' goes between have/has and the past participle. Meaning: very recently completed.
rules.td: 'just' steht zwischen have/has und dem Partizip Perfekt. Bedeutung: gerade eben abgeschlossen.

ex.en: I've just finished my homework.
ex.de: Ich habe gerade meine Hausaufgabe fertig gemacht.

ex.en: She has just arrived.
ex.de: Sie ist gerade angekommen.

rules.t: Negative: haven't/hasn't + past participle. Questions: Have/Has + subject + past participle?
rules.td: Verneinung: haven't/hasn't + Partizip Perfekt. Fragen: Have/Has + Subjekt + Partizip Perfekt?

ex.en: I haven't eaten yet.
ex.de: Ich habe noch nicht gegessen.

ex.en: Have you seen the doctor?
ex.de: Warst du beim Arzt?

rules.t: Present perfect = result relevant NOW (no specific past time). Past simple = completed past with specific time (yesterday, last week).
rules.td: Present Perfect = Ergebnis jetzt relevant (keine bestimmte Vergangenheitszeit). Past Simple = abgeschlossene Vergangenheit mit Zeitangabe (yesterday, last week).

ex.en: I've broken my arm. (result visible now)
ex.de: Ich habe mir den Arm gebrochen. (Ergebnis jetzt sichtbar)

ex.en: I broke my arm last week. (specific time)
ex.de: Ich habe mir letzte Woche den Arm gebrochen. (bestimmte Zeit)

errs.d: Using past simple form instead of past participle
Using present perfect with specific past time markers
Wrong auxiliary (have instead of has or vice versa)

structures.desc: will/won't + base form for spontaneous decisions, offers, promises, and predictions. Contrasted with going to for planned actions.

rules.t: will ('ll) + base form. Same form for all persons. No -s, no 'to'.
rules.td: will ('ll) + Grundform. Gleiche Form für alle Personen. Kein -s, kein 'to'.

ex.en: I'll help you carry those bags.
ex.de: Ich helfe dir, die Taschen zu tragen.

ex.en: I think it'll rain later.
ex.de: Ich glaube, es wird später regnen.

rules.t: won't (will not) + base form for negative predictions, refusals, promises not to do something.
rules.td: won't (will not) + Grundform für negative Vorhersagen, Ablehnungen, Versprechen etwas nicht zu tun.

ex.en: It won't rain tomorrow.
ex.de: Es wird morgen nicht regnen.

ex.en: I won't be late, I promise.
ex.de: Ich werde nicht zu spät kommen, versprochen.

rules.t: will = spontaneous decisions, offers, predictions. going to = planned intentions decided BEFORE speaking.
rules.td: will = spontane Entscheidungen, Angebote, Vorhersagen. going to = geplante Absichten, die VOR dem Sprechen beschlossen wurden.

ex.en: I'm going to visit London next summer. (planned)
ex.de: Ich werde nauchsten Sommer London besuchen. (geplant)

ex.en: Oh, you need help? I'll carry your bags! (spontaneous)
ex.de: Oh, du brauchst Hilfe? Ich trage deine Taschen! (spontan)

errs.d: Using will for clearly planned future
errs.w: I will visit London next summer. (already planned)
errs.c: I'm going to visit London next summer.

structures.desc: Forming adverbs from adjectives with -ly (slowly, carefully) and irregular adverbs (well, fast). Position: usually after the verb or object.

rules.t: Most adverbs of manner: adjective + -ly. Two-syllable adjectives ending in -y: change -y to -ily (happy→happily).
rules.td: Die meisten Adverbien der Art und Weise: Adjektiv + -ly. Zweisilbige Adjektive auf -y: -y wird zu -ily (happy→happily).

ex.en: She drives carefully.
ex.de: Sie fährt vorsichtig.

ex.en: He spoke quietly.
ex.de: Er sprach leise.

rules.t: Irregular adverbs: good→well, fast→fast (NO -ly!). These must be learnt by heart.
rules.td: Unregelmäßige Adverbien: good→well, fast→fast (KEIN -ly!). Diese muss man auswendig lernen.

ex.en: She sings well.
ex.de: Sie singt gut.

ex.en: He runs fast.
ex.de: Er läuft schnell.

rules.t: Adverbs of manner usually go AFTER the verb or after the object: She sings beautifully. He ate his lunch quickly.
rules.td: Adverbien der Art und Weise stehen meist NACH dem Verb oder nach dem Objekt: She sings beautifully. He ate his lunch quickly.

ex.en: She played the piano beautifully.
ex.de: Sie spielte wunderschön Klavier.

ex.en: He read the book quickly.
ex.de: Er las das Buch schnell.

errs.d: Using adjective instead of adverb after a verb

structures.n: Present Perfect: already, yet, ever, never
structures.nd: Present Perfect: already, yet, ever, never (Signalwörter)
structures.desc: Present perfect signal words: already (completed, positive), yet (not completed / asking, negative/questions), ever (lifetime experience, questions), never (no experience, statements).

rules.t: already = action completed. Position: between have/has and past participle. Used in positive sentences.
rules.td: already = Handlung abgeschlossen. Position: zwischen have/has und Partizip Perfekt. In positiven Sätzen verwendet.

ex.en: I've already done my homework.
ex.de: Ich habe meine Hausaufgabe schon gemacht.

ex.en: She has already left.
ex.de: Sie ist schon gegangen.

rules.t: yet = still waiting. Position: at the END of the sentence. Used in negatives and questions.
rules.td: yet = noch (nicht). Position: am ENDE des Satzes. In verneinten Sätzen und Fragen verwendet.

ex.en: I haven't finished yet.
ex.de: Ich habe noch nicht fertig gemacht.

ex.en: Have you eaten yet?
ex.de: Hast du schon gegessen?

rules.t: ever = at any time in your life. Position: between have/has and past participle. Used in questions.
rules.td: ever = jemals in deinem Leben. Position: zwischen have/has und Partizip Perfekt. In Fragen verwendet.

ex.en: Have you ever been to London?
ex.de: Warst du jemals in London?

ex.en: Has she ever played tennis?
ex.de: Hat sie jemals Tennis gespielt?

rules.t: never = at no time / not ever. Position: between have/has and past participle. Used in positive sentence structure (no 'not'!).
rules.td: never = niemals. Position: zwischen have/has und Partizip Perfekt. In positiver Satzstruktur (kein 'not'!).

ex.en: I've never been to London.
ex.de: Ich war noch nie in London.

ex.en: She has never tried sushi.
ex.de: Sie hat noch nie Sushi probiert.

questions: Have you ever been to London?

errs.w: I haven't never been to London.

errs.d: Using 'ever' in positive statements instead of questions
errs.w: I have ever been to London.

structures.n: So do I / Neither do I
structures.nd: So do I / Neither do I (Übereinstimmung)
structures.desc: Expressing positive agreement (So do I / So have I) and negative agreement (Neither do I / Neither have I) with inverted word order.

rules.t: Positive agreement: So + auxiliary + I. Match the auxiliary of the original statement: present simple → do/does; have got/present perfect → have/has.
rules.td: Positive Übereinstimmung: So + Hilfsverb + I. Das Hilfsverb muss zum Originalsatz passen: Present Simple → do/does; have got/Present Perfect → have/has.

ex.en: "I like pizza." — "So do I."
ex.de: "Ich mag Pizza." — "Ich auch."

ex.en: "I've been to London." — "So have I."
ex.de: "Ich war in London." — "Ich auch."

rules.t: Negative agreement: Neither + auxiliary + I. Use 'neither' when agreeing with a negative statement.
rules.td: Negative Übereinstimmung: Neither + Hilfsverb + I. Verwende 'neither' bei Zustimmung zu einer verneinten Aussage.

ex.en: "I don't like spiders." — "Neither do I."
ex.de: "Ich mag keine Spinnen." — "Ich auch nicht."

ex.en: "I haven't got a pet." — "Neither have I."
ex.de: "Ich habe kein Haustier." — "Ich auch nicht."

rules.t: The auxiliary must match: do/does with present simple, have/has with present perfect and have got, can with can, will with will.
rules.td: Das Hilfsverb muss passen: do/does bei Present Simple, have/has bei Present Perfect und have got, can bei can, will bei will.

ex.en: "She likes music." — "So does he."
ex.de: "Sie mag Musik." — "Er auch."

ex.en: "I can swim." — "So can I."
ex.de: "Ich kann schwimmen." — "Ich auch."

errs.d: Missing inversion (So I do instead of So do I)
Using 'So' with negative statements or 'Neither' with positive

items.p: ___ you enjoy the school trip? — Yes, I did.
items.h: To ask about actions in the past, start with Did.
items.hd: Um nach Handlungen in der Vergangenheit zu fragen, beginne mit Did.
items.e: 'Enjoy' is an action verb, so the past question needs Did + subject + base form.
items.ed: 'Enjoy' ist ein Tätigkeitswort, daher braucht die Vergangenheitsfrage Did + Subjekt + Grundform.

items.p: ___ she at the swimming pool yesterday?
items.h: 'She' goes with was (not were).
items.hd: 'She' steht mit was (nicht were).
items.e: 'She' is 3rd person singular, so we use Was she...? for past questions with 'be'.
items.ed: 'She' ist 3. Person Einzahl, daher verwenden wir Was she...? für Vergangenheitsfragen mit 'be'.

items.p: Did Tom ___ his homework last night?
items.h: After 'did', always use the base form of the verb.
items.hd: Nach 'did' verwendet man immer die Grundform des Verbs.
items.e: In past simple questions: Did + subject + base form. 'Did' already shows it's past, so we don't need 'finished'.
items.ed: Bei Fragen im Simple Past: Did + Subjekt + Grundform. 'Did' zeigt bereits die Vergangenheit, daher brauchen wir nicht 'finished'.

items.p: ___ the children happy at the party?
items.h: 'Children' is plural — which form of 'be' in the past goes with plural?
items.hd: 'Children' ist Mehrzahl — welche Vergangenheitsform von 'be' passt zur Mehrzahl?
items.e: 'Children' is plural, so we need Were they...? not Was they...?
items.ed: 'Children' ist Mehrzahl, daher brauchen wir Were they...? nicht Was they...?

items.p: Did your brother ___ to the football match on Saturday?
items.h: 'Did' already marks the past. What form comes after it?
items.hd: 'Did' zeigt bereits die Vergangenheit. Welche Form folgt danach?
items.e: After 'did', we always use the base form: Did he go? (not went, goes, or gone).
items.ed: Nach 'did' verwenden wir immer die Grundform: Did he go? (nicht went, goes oder gone).

items.p: Where ___ you last weekend? — I was at my grandma's house.
items.h: 'You' always goes with 'were' in the past.
items.hd: 'You' steht im Past immer mit 'were'.
items.e: For questions with 'be' in the past: Where were you? 'You' always takes 'were'.
items.ed: Bei Fragen mit 'be' in der Vergangenheit: Where were you? 'You' steht immer mit 'were'.

items.p: Which question is correct?
items.c: Did she visit her friend yesterday?
a: Did she visit her friend yesterday?
ds: Did she visited her friend yesterday?
She did visit her friend yesterday?
Was she visit her friend yesterday?
items.h: Past questions with action verbs: Did + subject + base form.
items.hd: Fragen in der Vergangenheit mit Tätigkeitswörtern: Did + Subjekt + Grundform.
items.e: The correct pattern is Did + subject + base form: Did she visit? 'Did' shows the past, so 'visit' stays in base form.
items.ed: Das richtige Muster ist Did + Subjekt + Grundform: Did she visit? 'Did' zeigt die Vergangenheit, daher bleibt 'visit' in der Grundform.

items.p: Which past question about a film is correct?
items.h: Is there an action verb here, or is it 'be'?
items.hd: Gibt es hier ein Tätigkeitswort, oder ist es 'be'?
items.e: 'Good' is an adjective, not an action verb. We need was/were, not did. The film is singular → Was the film good?
items.ed: 'Good' ist ein Adjektiv, kein Tätigkeitswort. Wir brauchen was/were, nicht did. Der Film ist Einzahl → Was the film good?

items.p: Choose the correct short answer: Were you and Emma at the park? — No, ___.
items.h: The short answer must match the auxiliary in the question.
items.hd: Die Kurzantwort muss zum Hilfsverb in der Frage passen.
items.e: The question uses 'were', so the short answer is: No, we weren't. Match the auxiliary!
items.ed: Die Frage verwendet 'were', daher lautet die Kurzantwort: No, we weren't. Das Hilfsverb muss passen!

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Did you went to the cinema last Friday?
items.c: Did you go to the cinema last Friday?
a: Did you go to the cinema last Friday?
Did you go to the cinema last Friday
items.h: What form of the verb comes after 'did'?
items.hd: Welche Form des Verbs kommt nach 'did'?
items.e: After 'did', use the base form: Did you go (not went). 'Did' already marks the past.
items.ed: Nach 'did' verwendet man die Grundform: Did you go (nicht went). 'Did' zeigt bereits die Vergangenheit.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Did he was tired after the match?
items.c: Was he tired after the match?
a: Was he tired after the match?
Was he tired after the match
items.h: Do we use 'did' with was/were?
items.hd: Verwendet man 'did' mit was/were?
items.e: Don't use 'did' with was/were. For 'be' questions, just move was/were to the front: Was he tired?
items.ed: Man verwendet 'did' nicht mit was/were. Bei 'be'-Fragen stellt man was/were einfach an den Anfang: Was he tired?

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: You liked the new teacher?
items.c: Did you like the new teacher?
a: Did you like the new teacher?
Did you like the new teacher
items.h: In English, questions need an auxiliary verb at the start.
items.hd: Im Englischen brauchen Fragen ein Hilfsverb am Anfang.
items.e: In English, we can't make a question just with rising intonation. We need: Did you like the new teacher?
items.ed: Im Englischen kann man eine Frage nicht nur mit steigender Betonung bilden. Wir brauchen: Did you like the new teacher?

items.p: Your friend was away from school yesterday. Ask her about the trip: '___ you ___ the school trip?'
a: Did you enjoy the school trip?
items.h: To ask about actions in the past, start with Did + subject + base form.
items.hd: Um nach Handlungen in der Vergangenheit zu fragen, beginne mit Did + Subjekt + Grundform.
items.e: 'Enjoy' is an action verb, so the past question needs Did + subject + base form (enjoy, not enjoyed).
items.ed: 'Enjoy' ist ein Tätigkeitswort, daher braucht die Vergangenheitsfrage Did + Subjekt + Grundform (enjoy, nicht enjoyed).

items.p: You want to know where your friend was after school. Ask: '___ they at the zoo yesterday?'
a: Were they at the zoo yesterday?
Were they at the zoo yesterday
items.h: For 'be' in the past, just move was/were before the subject. 'They' needs 'were'.
items.hd: Bei 'be' in der Vergangenheit stellt man was/were einfach vor das Subjekt. 'They' braucht 'were'.
items.e: They were → Were they? Just move 'were' to the front. No 'did' needed for 'be'.
items.ed: They were → Were they? Man stellt 'were' einfach an den Anfang. Kein 'did' nötig bei 'be'.

items.p: You're curious about your pen pal's holiday. Ask where he went: 'Where ___?'
items.h: Wh-word + did + subject + base form.
items.hd: Fragewort + did + Subjekt + Grundform.
items.e: Wh-questions in past simple: Where + did + he + go (base form, not went).
items.ed: W-Fragen im Simple Past: Where + did + he + go (Grundform, nicht went).

items.p: 🇩🇪 Hast du den Film gesehen?
items.h: In English, past questions use Did + subject + base form.
items.hd: Im Englischen verwenden Vergangenheitsfragen Did + Subjekt + Grundform.
items.e: German uses Perfekt (Hast du gesehen?), but English uses Did + base form: Did you see?
items.ed: Deutsch verwendet das Perfekt (Hast du gesehen?), aber Englisch verwendet Did + Grundform: Did you see?

items.p: 🇩🇪 War dein Bruder gestern krank?
items.h: 'Be' in the past → was/were. No 'did' needed.
items.hd: 'Be' in der Vergangenheit → was/were. Kein 'did' nötig.
items.e: 'Brother' is singular → Was your brother ill? Don't use 'did' with adjectives or 'be'.
items.ed: 'Brother' ist Einzahl → Was your brother ill? Verwende 'did' nicht mit Adjektiven oder 'be'.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: you / did / for / what / have / lunch / ?
items.c: What did you have for lunch?
a: What did you have for lunch?
What did you have for lunch
items.h: Wh-word + did + subject + base form.
items.hd: Fragewort + did + Subjekt + Grundform.
items.e: Wh-questions follow this order: What + did + you + have + for lunch?
items.ed: W-Fragen folgen dieser Reihenfolge: What + did + you + have + for lunch?

items.p:
Match the question beginnings with the correct endings:
1. Did you play
2. Was she
3. Were they
4. Did he eat

a. at the party last night?
b. football after school?
c. his breakfast this morning?
d. happy about the result?
items.h: Think about which verb form fits each subject and auxiliary.
items.hd: Überlege, welche Verbform zu jedem Subjekt und Hilfsverb passt.
items.e: Did + base form for action verbs (play, eat). Was/Were for 'be' (happy, at the party).
items.ed: Did + Grundform für Tätigkeitswörter (play, eat). Was/Were für 'be' (happy, at the party).

items.p: Your friend tells you about the weekend. Which sentence is a correct past simple question?
items.c: Did your brother play football on Saturday?
a: Did your brother play football on Saturday?
ds: Does your brother played football on Saturday?
Did your brother played football on Saturday?
Your brother did play football on Saturday?
items.h: Past simple questions: Did + subject + base form. Only 'did' carries the past meaning.
items.hd: Past-simple-Fragen: Did + Subjekt + Grundform. Nur 'did' trägt die Vergangenheitsbedeutung.
items.e: The correct past simple question form is: Did + subject + base form. 'Did your brother play...' — no -ed on the main verb.
items.ed: Die korrekte Past-simple-Frageform ist: Did + Subjekt + Grundform. 'Did your brother play...' — kein -ed am Hauptverb.

items.p: My mum ___ (buy) a new jacket yesterday.
items.h: Buy is irregular. Its past form is NOT buy + ed.
items.hd: Buy ist unregelmäßig. Die Vergangenheitsform ist NICHT buy + ed.
items.e: Buy → bought (irregular). Do not add -ed to irregular verbs.
items.ed: Buy → bought (unregelmäßig). Hänge kein -ed an unregelmäßige Verben.

items.p: She ___ (write) a letter to her pen pal last week.
items.h: Write is irregular. Think: write → wr___e.
items.hd: Write ist unregelmäßig. Denke: write → wr___e.
items.e: Write → wrote (irregular). The past form changes the vowel, not by adding -ed.
items.ed: Write → wrote (unregelmäßig). Die Vergangenheitsform ändert den Vokal, nicht durch Anhängen von -ed.

items.p: I ___ (know) the answer, but I was too shy to say it.
items.h: Know is irregular. Its past form starts with kn-.
items.hd: Know ist unregelmäßig. Die Vergangenheitsform beginnt mit kn-.
items.e: Know → knew (irregular). Don't add -ed to irregular verbs.
items.ed: Know → knew (unregelmäßig). Hänge kein -ed an unregelmäßige Verben.

items.p: They ___ (make) a big sandcastle at the beach.
items.h: Make is irregular. Think: make → m___e.
items.hd: Make ist unregelmäßig. Denke: make → m___e.
items.e: Make → made (irregular). The past form changes completely — no -ed.
items.ed: Make → made (unregelmäßig). Die Vergangenheitsform ändert sich komplett — kein -ed.

items.p: He didn't ___ (know) where the school was.
items.h: After didn't, always use the base form.
items.hd: Nach didn't verwendet man immer die Grundform.
items.e: After didn't, use the base form: didn't know (not didn't knew). 'Didn't' already shows the past.
items.ed: Nach didn't verwendet man die Grundform: didn't know (nicht didn't knew). 'Didn't' zeigt bereits die Vergangenheit.

items.p: We didn't ___ (buy) anything at the school fair.
items.h: After didn't, which form of the verb do we use?
items.hd: Welche Form des Verbs verwenden wir nach didn't?
items.e: After didn't, always use the base form: didn't buy (not didn't bought).
items.ed: Nach didn't verwendet man immer die Grundform: didn't buy (nicht didn't bought).

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: She wrote an email to her teacher.
a: She wrote an email to her teacher.
ds: She writed an email to her teacher.
She write an email to her teacher.
She wroten an email to her teacher.
items.h: Write is irregular. What is the correct past form?
items.hd: Write ist unregelmäßig. Wie lautet die richtige Vergangenheitsform?
items.e: Write → wrote. It's an irregular past form that must be memorised.
items.ed: Write → wrote. Es ist eine unregelmäßige Vergangenheitsform, die man auswendig lernen muss.

items.p: Which negative sentence is correct?
items.c: I didn't make any mistakes in the test.
a: I didn't make any mistakes in the test.
ds: I didn't made any mistakes in the test.
I didn't maked any mistakes in the test.
I not made any mistakes in the test.
items.h: What verb form comes after didn't?
items.hd: Welche Verbform kommt nach didn't?
items.e: After didn't, use the base form: didn't make (not made or maked).
items.ed: Nach didn't verwendet man die Grundform: didn't make (nicht made oder maked).

items.p: Choose the correct sentence:
items.c: My grandpa knew a lot about animals.
a: My grandpa knew a lot about animals.
ds: My grandpa knowed a lot about animals.
My grandpa know a lot about animals.
My grandpa knew a lot about animal.
items.h: Know is an irregular verb. What's its past form?
items.hd: Know ist ein unregelmäßiges Verb. Wie lautet die Vergangenheitsform?
items.e: Know → knew (irregular past form). 'Knowed' does not exist.
items.ed: Know → knew (unregelmäßige Vergangenheitsform). 'Knowed' gibt es nicht.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She buyed new trainers for PE.
items.c: She bought new trainers for PE.
a: She bought new trainers for PE.
She bought new trainers for PE
items.h: Buy is irregular — it doesn't take -ed.
items.hd: Buy ist unregelmäßig — es bekommt kein -ed.
items.e: Buy → bought (not buyed). Irregular verbs don't follow the -ed pattern.
items.ed: Buy → bought (nicht buyed). Unregelmäßige Verben folgen nicht dem -ed-Muster.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I didn't knew the answer to question five.
items.c: I didn't know the answer to question five.
a: I didn't know the answer to question five.
I didn't know the answer to question five
items.h: After didn't, what form of the verb do we use?
items.hd: Welche Form des Verbs verwenden wir nach didn't?
items.e: After didn't, use the base form: didn't know (not didn't knew).
items.ed: Nach didn't verwendet man die Grundform: didn't know (nicht didn't knew).

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Tom writed a really funny story in English class.
items.c: Tom wrote a really funny story in English class.
a: Tom wrote a really funny story in English class.
Tom wrote a really funny story in English class
items.h: Write is irregular — check the past form.
items.hd: Write ist unregelmäßig — überprüfe die Vergangenheitsform.
items.e: Write → wrote (not writed). Irregular verbs have their own past forms.
items.ed: Write → wrote (nicht writed). Unregelmäßige Verben haben eigene Vergangenheitsformen.

items.p: You're telling your mum about lunch at school. Complete: 'Yesterday I ___ (buy) a sandwich at the canteen.'
items.h: 'Buy' is irregular. What is the past form of 'buy'?
items.hd: 'Buy' ist unregelmäßig. Was ist die Vergangenheitsform von 'buy'?
items.e: Buy → bought (irregular). Don't add -ed to irregular verbs.
items.ed: Buy → bought (unregelmäßig). Hänge kein -ed an unregelmäßige Verben.

items.p: Your sister wrote a poem for homework but it's gone now. Tell your dad: 'She ___ (not write) a new poem — she lost the old one.'
items.h: Negative past: didn't + base form. The base form of 'wrote' is 'write'.
items.hd: Verneintes Past: didn't + Grundform. Die Grundform von 'wrote' ist 'write'.
items.e: Negative past simple: didn't + base form. She didn't write (not 'didn't wrote').
items.ed: Verneinetes Simple Past: didn't + Grundform. She didn't write (nicht 'didn't wrote').

items.p: It's Monday morning. Tell your friend about yesterday: 'We ___ (make) pancakes on Sunday morning.'
items.h: 'Make' is irregular. What is the past form of 'make'?
items.hd: 'Make' ist unregelmäßig. Was ist die Vergangenheitsform von 'make'?
items.e: Make → made (irregular). Don't add -ed — it's not 'maked'.
items.ed: Make → made (unregelmäßig). Hänge kein -ed an — es heißt nicht 'maked'.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ich habe gestern ein neues Buch gekauft.
items.c: I bought a new book yesterday.
a: I bought a new book yesterday.
I bought a new book yesterday
Yesterday I bought a new book.
Yesterday I bought a new book
items.h: German uses Perfekt (habe gekauft), but English uses past simple here.
items.hd: Deutsch verwendet das Perfekt (habe gekauft), aber Englisch verwendet hier das Simple Past.
items.e: German 'habe gekauft' = English 'bought' (past simple). Buy → bought.
items.ed: Deutsch 'habe gekauft' = Englisch 'bought' (Simple Past). Buy → bought.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Er hat keinen Kuchen gemacht.
a: He did not make a cake.
He did not make a cake
items.h: Negative past: didn't + base form. Don't use the past form after didn't.
items.hd: Vergangenheit verneint: didn't + Grundform. Verwende nicht die Vergangenheitsform nach didn't.
items.e: German 'hat nicht gemacht' = English 'didn't make' (didn't + base form, not 'didn't made').
items.ed: Deutsch 'hat nicht gemacht' = Englisch 'didn't make' (didn't + Grundform, nicht 'didn't made').

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: wrote / she / a / long / email / yesterday

a: She wrote a long email yesterday.
She wrote a long email yesterday
Yesterday she wrote a long email.
Yesterday she wrote a long email
items.h: Start with the subject, then the verb in its past form.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Subjekt, dann das Verb in seiner Vergangenheitsform.
items.e: Subject + past verb + object + time: She wrote a long email yesterday.
items.ed: Subjekt + Verb im Past + Objekt + Zeit: She wrote a long email yesterday.

items.p:
Match each verb with its past form:

2. know

b. wrote

items.h: These are all irregular — none of them use -ed.
items.hd: Diese sind alle unregelmäßig — keine davon verwendet -ed.
items.e: buy→bought, know→knew, make→made, write→wrote. All irregular past forms.
items.ed: buy→bought, know→knew, make→made, write→wrote. Alles unregelmäßige Vergangenheitsformen.

items.p: Your class made posters for the school project last week. Which sentence correctly describes what happened?
items.c: They made a big poster for the school project.
a: They made a big poster for the school project.
ds: They maked a big poster for the school project.
They make a big poster for the school project.
They have made a big poster for the school project.
items.h: The event happened last week. Which past form of 'make' is correct?
items.hd: Das Ereignis war letzte Woche. Welche Vergangenheitsform von 'make' ist richtig?
items.e: 'Make' is irregular: make → made. With a past time marker ('last week'), use past simple.
items.ed: 'Make' ist unregelmäßig: make → made. Mit einer Vergangenheitszeitangabe ('last week') verwende Simple Past.

items.p: You ___ eat more fruit. It's healthy!
items.h: Modal verbs like 'should' are followed directly by the base form — no 'to'.
items.hd: Modalverben wie 'should' werden direkt von der Grundform gefolgt — kein 'to'.
items.e: Should + base form (no 'to'): You should eat more fruit.
items.ed: Should + Grundform (kein 'to'): You should eat more fruit.

items.p: It's raining. You ___ take an umbrella.
items.h: Should is the same for all persons — no changes needed.
items.hd: Should ist für alle Personen gleich — keine Änderungen nötig.
items.e: Should never changes form. No 'to', no -s, no auxiliary before it.
items.ed: Should ändert seine Form nie. Kein 'to', kein -s, kein Hilfsverb davor.

items.p: She ___ (should / not) stay up so late on school nights.
items.h: To make should negative, add 'not' after it: should + not = shouldn't.
items.hd: Um should zu verneinen, füge 'not' dahinter ein: should + not = shouldn't.
items.e: Should + not = shouldn't. Don't use 'don't should' — modals make their own negatives.
items.ed: Should + not = shouldn't. Verwende nicht 'don't should' — Modalverben bilden ihre eigene Verneinung.

items.p: He ___ (should) drink more water when he plays football.
items.h: Does 'should' change for he/she/it?
items.hd: Ändert sich 'should' bei he/she/it?
items.e: Should stays the same for ALL persons: I should, he should, they should. No -s for 3rd person.
items.ed: Should bleibt für ALLE Personen gleich: I should, he should, they should. Kein -s bei der 3. Person.

items.p: You look tired. You ___ go to bed early tonight.
items.h: Should is followed directly by the base form of the verb.
items.hd: Auf should folgt direkt die Grundform des Verbs.
items.e: Should + base form: You should go (not 'should to go').
items.ed: Should + Grundform: You should go (nicht 'should to go').

items.p: Your little sister is scared of dogs. You ___ (should / not) take her to the dog park.
items.h: Should makes its own negative: should + not = shouldn't. No 'don't' needed.
items.hd: Should bildet seine eigene Verneinung: should + not = shouldn't. Kein 'don't' nötig.
items.e: Shouldn't + base form. Never use 'don't/doesn't should' — modals negate themselves.
items.ed: Shouldn't + Grundform. Verwende nie 'don't/doesn't should' — Modalverben verneinen sich selbst.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: You should wear a helmet when you ride your bike.
a: You should wear a helmet when you ride your bike.
ds: You should to wear a helmet when you ride your bike.
You shoulds wear a helmet when you ride your bike.
You don't should wear a helmet when you ride your bike.
items.h: Should + base form. No 'to', no -s, no 'don't'.
items.hd: Should + Grundform. Kein 'to', kein -s, kein 'don't'.
items.e: Should + base form: You should wear. No 'to' after should, no -s ending.
items.ed: Should + Grundform: You should wear. Kein 'to' nach should, keine -s-Endung.

items.p: Your friend is always late for school. Which advice is correct?
ds: He should to get up earlier.
He don't should get up earlier.
items.h: Modal verbs don't change for he/she/it.
items.hd: Modalverben ändern sich nicht bei he/she/it.
items.e: He should get up — no -s on 'should', no 'to' after it. Modal verbs are always the same form.
items.ed: He should get up — kein -s bei 'should', kein 'to' danach. Modalverben haben immer die gleiche Form.

items.p: Which question is correct?
items.c: Should I bring my laptop to school?
a: Should I bring my laptop to school?
ds: Do I should bring my laptop to school?
Should I to bring my laptop to school?
Am I should bring my laptop to school?
items.h: Should questions: Should + subject + base form?
items.hd: Should-Fragen: Should + Subjekt + Grundform?
items.e: Modal questions put the modal first: Should I bring? No 'do' or 'to' needed.
items.ed: Bei Modalfragen steht das Modalverb an erster Stelle: Should I bring? Kein 'do' oder 'to' nötig.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: You should to study for the test tomorrow.
items.c: You should study for the test tomorrow.
a: You should study for the test tomorrow.
You should study for the test tomorrow
items.h: Does 'should' need 'to' after it?
items.hd: Braucht 'should' ein 'to' danach?
items.e: Should + base form (no 'to'): You should study, not 'should to study'.
items.ed: Should + Grundform (kein 'to'): You should study, nicht 'should to study'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She don't should eat so much chocolate.
items.c: She shouldn't eat so much chocolate.
a: She shouldn't eat so much chocolate.
She shouldn't eat so much chocolate
She should not eat so much chocolate.
She should not eat so much chocolate
items.h: How do we make 'should' negative?
items.hd: Wie verneint man 'should'?
items.e: Should makes its own negative: shouldn't (not 'don't should'). Modals negate directly.
items.ed: Should bildet seine eigene Verneinung: shouldn't (nicht 'don't should'). Modalverben verneinen sich direkt.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: He shoulds help his mum with the dishes.
items.c: He should help his mum with the dishes.
a: He should help his mum with the dishes.
He should help his mum with the dishes
items.h: Do modal verbs take -s for he/she/it?
items.hd: Bekommen Modalverben ein -s bei he/she/it?
items.e: Modal verbs never take -s: He should (not shoulds). The form is the same for all persons.
items.ed: Modalverben bekommen nie ein -s: He should (nicht shoulds). Die Form ist für alle Personen gleich.

items.p: Your friend has a bad headache and doesn't know what to do. Give advice: 'You ___ take some medicine and rest.'
items.h: You want to give friendly advice. Which modal verb is used for advice?
items.hd: Du möchtest einen freundlichen Rat geben. Welches Modalverb wird für Ratschläge verwendet?
items.e: 'Should' is used to give advice. You should take some medicine = I think it's a good idea.
items.ed: 'Should' wird für Ratschläge verwendet. You should take some medicine = Ich denke, das ist eine gute Idee.

items.p: Your little brother is playing on his phone during dinner. Tell him: 'You ___ play on your phone at the table!'
items.h: You want to tell him it's a bad idea. Use the negative of 'should'.
items.hd: Du willst ihm sagen, dass es keine gute Idee ist. Verwende die Verneinung von 'should'.
items.e: 'Shouldn't' gives negative advice — it means 'it's not a good idea'. You shouldn't play on your phone at the table.
items.ed: 'Shouldn't' gibt einen negativen Rat — es bedeutet 'es ist keine gute Idee'. You shouldn't play on your phone at the table.

items.p: You have a problem at school and want advice. Ask your mum: '___ I ___ my teacher about it?'
a: Should I tell my teacher about it?
Should I tell my teacher about it
items.h: To ask for advice, put 'should' before the subject.
items.hd: Um nach einem Rat zu fragen, stelle 'should' vor das Subjekt.
items.e: Should I tell...? — Put 'should' before 'I' to make a question asking for advice.
items.ed: Should I tell...? — Stelle 'should' vor 'I', um eine Ratschlag-Frage zu bilden.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Du solltest mehr Wasser trinken.
a: You ought to drink more water.
You ought to drink more water
items.h: Should + base form (no 'to').
items.hd: Should + Grundform (kein 'to').
items.e: 'Solltest' = should. You should drink (not 'should to drink').
items.ed: 'Solltest' = should. You should drink (nicht 'should to drink').

items.p: 🇩🇪 Er sollte nicht so spät aufbleiben.
items.c: He shouldn't stay up so late.
a: He shouldn't stay up so late.
He shouldn't stay up so late
He should not stay up so late.
He should not stay up so late
items.h: 'Sollte nicht' = shouldn't. No 'to' after shouldn't.
items.hd: 'Sollte nicht' = shouldn't. Kein 'to' nach shouldn't.
items.e: 'Sollte nicht' = shouldn't + base form: He shouldn't stay up.
items.ed: 'Sollte nicht' = shouldn't + Grundform: He shouldn't stay up.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: should / we / to / walk / school
items.h: Subject + should + base form + rest of sentence.
items.hd: Subjekt + should + Grundform + Rest des Satzes.
items.e: We should walk to school. Subject first, then should + base form.
items.ed: We should walk to school. Zuerst das Subjekt, dann should + Grundform.

items.p:
Match the situation with the correct advice:
1. I have a toothache.
2. She is always tired.
3. We have a test tomorrow.
4. He eats too many sweets.
5. It's very cold outside.

a. He should eat less sugar.
b. You should go to the dentist.
c. You should wear a warm jacket.
d. She should go to bed earlier.
e. We should study tonight.
items.h: Match each problem with the best advice using 'should'.
items.hd: Ordne jedes Problem dem besten Ratschlag mit 'should' zu.
items.e: Each situation matches with logical advice: toothache→dentist, tired→bed earlier, test→study, sweets→less sugar, cold→warm jacket.
items.ed: Jede Situation passt zu einem logischen Ratschlag: Zahnschmerzen→Zahnarzt, müde→früher schlafen, Test→lernen, Süßigkeiten→weniger Zucker, kalt→warme Jacke.

items.p: You should ask your teacher. → Ask about: what to do. Start with 'What'.
items.h: Wh-word + should + subject + base form?
items.hd: Fragewort + should + Subjekt + Grundform?
items.e: What should I do? — Put 'should' after the wh-word and before the subject.
items.ed: What should I do? — Stelle 'should' nach dem Fragewort und vor das Subjekt.

items.p: A cheetah is ___ (fast) than a horse.
items.h: Short adjectives (1 syllable) add -er for comparisons.
items.hd: Kurze Adjektive (1 Silbe) bekommen -er beim Vergleichen.
items.e: Fast is a short adjective → faster (not 'more fast'). One syllable = -er.
items.ed: Fast ist ein kurzes Adjektiv → faster (nicht 'more fast'). Eine Silbe = -er.

items.p: My sister is ___ (tall) than me.
items.h: Tall has one syllable. What ending do we add?
items.hd: Tall hat eine Silbe. Welche Endung hängen wir an?
items.e: Tall → taller. Short adjectives take -er, not 'more'.
items.ed: Tall → taller. Kurze Adjektive bekommen -er, nicht 'more'.

items.p: This bag is ___ (heavy) than that one.
items.h: Adjectives ending in -y: change -y to -ier.
items.hd: Adjektive auf -y: ändere -y zu -ier.
items.e: Heavy ends in -y → change to -ier: heavier (not 'more heavy' or 'heavyer').
items.ed: Heavy endet auf -y → ändere zu -ier: heavier (nicht 'more heavy' oder 'heavyer').

items.p: The elephant is ___ (big) than the horse.
items.h: Big is short (CVC pattern). Double the final consonant before -er.
items.hd: Big ist kurz (KVK-Muster). Verdopple den letzten Konsonanten vor -er.
items.e: Big → bigger (double the -g). CVC adjectives double the final consonant: big → bigger.
items.ed: Big → bigger (Verdopplung des -g). KVK-Adjektive verdoppeln den letzten Konsonanten: big → bigger.

items.p: Science is ___ (interesting) than geography, I think.
items.h: Long adjectives (3+ syllables) use 'more' + adjective.
items.hd: Lange Adjektive (3+ Silben) verwenden 'more' + Adjektiv.
items.e: Interesting has 4 syllables → more interesting (not interestinger).
items.ed: Interesting hat 4 Silben → more interesting (nicht interestinger).

items.p: My brother is ___ (good) at maths than I am.
items.h: Good is irregular. Its comparative form is NOT good + er.
items.hd: Good ist unregelmäßig. Die Vergleichsform ist NICHT good + er.
items.e: Good → better (irregular). 'Gooder' and 'more good' don't exist.
items.ed: Good → better (unregelmäßig). 'Gooder' und 'more good' gibt es nicht.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
ds: Dogs are more friendly than cats.
Dogs are more friendlier than cats.
items.h: Friendly ends in -y. How do -y adjectives form the comparative?
items.hd: Friendly endet auf -y. Wie bilden Adjektive auf -y den Komparativ?
items.e: Friendly → friendlier (change -y to -ier). Some two-syllable adjectives ending in -y use -ier.
items.ed: Friendly → friendlier (ändere -y zu -ier). Manche zweisilbigen Adjektive auf -y verwenden -ier.

items.p: Which sentence about the weather is correct?
items.c: The weather is worse today than yesterday.
a: The weather is worse today than yesterday.
ds: The weather is worser today than yesterday.
The weather is more bad today than yesterday.
The weather is more worse today than yesterday.
items.h: Bad is an irregular adjective. Its comparative is a completely different word.
items.hd: Bad ist ein unregelmäßiges Adjektiv. Sein Komparativ ist ein ganz anderes Wort.
items.e: Bad → worse (irregular). Not 'badder', 'more bad', or 'worser'.
items.ed: Bad → worse (unregelmäßig). Nicht 'badder', 'more bad' oder 'worser'.

items.p: Which sentence about a school exercise is correct?
items.c: This exercise is more difficult than the last one.
a: This exercise is more difficult than the last one.
ds: This exercise is difficulter than the last one.
This exercise is more difficulter than the last one.
This exercise is most difficult than the last one.
items.h: Think carefully about the grammar rule and choose the best answer.
items.hd: Überlege dir die Grammatikregel und wähle die beste Antwort.
items.e: Difficult has 3 syllables → more difficult (not difficulter). Long adjectives use 'more'.
items.ed: Difficult hat 3 Silben → more difficult (nicht difficulter). Lange Adjektive verwenden 'more'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: A lion is more fast than a turtle.
items.c: A lion is faster than a turtle.
a: A lion is faster than a turtle.
A lion is faster than a turtle
items.h: Fast has one syllable. Does it use 'more' or -er?
items.hd: Fast hat eine Silbe. Verwendet es 'more' oder -er?
items.e: Fast is a short adjective → faster (not 'more fast'). One syllable = add -er.
items.ed: Fast ist ein kurzes Adjektiv → faster (nicht 'more fast'). Eine Silbe = -er anhängen.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She is more bigger than her sister.
items.c: She is bigger than her sister.
a: She is bigger than her sister.
She is bigger than her sister
items.h: Can you use 'more' AND -er at the same time?
items.hd: Kann man 'more' UND -er gleichzeitig verwenden?
items.e: Never use 'more' + -er together. Big → bigger (not 'more bigger'). Choose one method only.
items.ed: Verwende nie 'more' + -er zusammen. Big → bigger (nicht 'more bigger'). Wähle nur eine Methode.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: He is gooder at football than his brother.
items.c: He is better at football than his brother.
a: He is better at football than his brother.
He is better at football than his brother
items.h: Good is an irregular adjective. It has a special comparative form.
items.hd: Good ist ein unregelmäßiges Adjektiv. Es hat eine besondere Vergleichsform.
items.e: Good → better (not gooder). This is an irregular comparative that must be learnt.
items.ed: Good → better (nicht gooder). Das ist ein unregelmäßiger Komparativ, den man lernen muss.

items.p: You're comparing two friends in PE class. Tom is tall, Ben is short. Tell your teacher: 'Tom is ___ Ben.'
items.h: Short adjective + -er + than. 'Tall' → 'taller than'.
items.hd: Kurzes Adjektiv + -er + than. 'Tall' → 'taller than'.
items.e: Comparative of short adjectives: tall → taller + than. Tom is taller than Ben.
items.ed: Komparativ kurzer Adjektive: tall → taller + than. Tom is taller than Ben.

items.p: You're talking about school subjects with a friend. You find English easy but maths hard. Say: 'Maths is ___ English.'
items.h: Long adjectives use 'more' + adjective + 'than'.
items.hd: Lange Adjektive verwenden 'more' + Adjektiv + 'than'.
items.e: Comparative of long adjectives: more + difficult + than. Maths is more difficult than English.
items.ed: Komparativ langer Adjektive: more + difficult + than. Maths is more difficult than English.

items.p: You're comparing your grades with your friend. Your English grade is bad, your maths grade is good. Say: 'My English grade is ___ my maths grade.'
items.h: 'Bad' is irregular: bad → worse (not 'badder' or 'more bad').
items.hd: 'Bad' ist unregelmäßig: bad → worse (nicht 'badder' oder 'more bad').
items.e: Irregular comparative: bad → worse. My English grade is worse than my maths grade.
items.ed: Unregelmäßiger Komparativ: bad → worse. My English grade is worse than my maths grade.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Mein Hund ist größer als deine Katze.
items.c: My dog is bigger than your cat.
a: My dog is bigger than your cat.
My dog is bigger than your cat
My dog is larger than your cat.
My dog is larger than your cat
items.h: Big is short (CVC) → double the last consonant + -er.
items.hd: Big ist kurz (KVK) → verdopple den letzten Konsonanten + -er.
items.e: Big → bigger (double the -g). German uses -er too (größer), but watch the English spelling.
items.ed: Big → bigger (Verdopplung des -g). Deutsch verwendet auch -er (größer), aber achte auf die englische Schreibweise.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Dieses Buch ist interessanter als der Film.
items.c: This book is more interesting than the film.
a: This book is more interesting than the film.
This book is more interesting than the film
This book is more interesting than the movie.
This book is more interesting than the movie
items.h: German uses -er for all comparatives, but English uses 'more' for long adjectives.
items.hd: Deutsch verwendet -er für alle Komparative, aber Englisch verwendet 'more' für lange Adjektive.
items.e: German 'interessanter' = English 'more interesting' (not interestinger). Long adjectives need 'more' in English.
items.ed: Deutsch 'interessanter' = Englisch 'more interesting' (nicht interestinger). Lange Adjektive brauchen 'more' im Englischen.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: is / a / cat / smaller / a / dog / than
items.c: A cat is smaller than a dog.
a: A cat is smaller than a dog.
A cat is smaller than a dog
items.h: Subject + is + comparative + than + object.
items.hd: Subjekt + is + Komparativ + than + Objekt.
items.e: A cat is smaller than a dog. Pattern: noun + is + adjective-er + than + noun.
items.ed: A cat is smaller than a dog. Muster: Nomen + is + Adjektiv-er + than + Nomen.

items.p:
Match each adjective with its correct comparative form:
1. good
2. big
3. happy
4. dangerous
5. bad

a. bigger
b. worse
c. happier
d. more dangerous
e. better
items.h: Think: short adj → -er, -y adj → -ier, long adj → more, irregular → special form.
items.hd: Denke: kurzes Adj → -er, -y Adj → -ier, langes Adj → more, unregelmäßig → Sonderform.
items.e: good→better, big→bigger, happy→happier, dangerous→more dangerous, bad→worse.
items.ed: good→better, big→bigger, happy→happier, dangerous→more dangerous, bad→worse.

items.p: You're describing your pet to a friend. Which sentence uses the comparative correctly?
items.c: My dog is bigger than my cat.
a: My dog is bigger than my cat.
ds: My dog is more bigger than my cat.
My dog is biger than my cat.
My dog is big than my cat.
items.h: Short adjectives form the comparative with -er + than. Watch the spelling of 'big'!
items.hd: Kurze Adjektive bilden den Komparativ mit -er + than. Achte auf die Schreibung von 'big'!
items.e: Big → bigger (double the consonant) + than. Don't use 'more' with short adjectives.
items.ed: Big → bigger (Konsonant verdoppeln) + than. Verwende 'more' nicht mit kurzen Adjektiven.

items.p: Mount Everest is ___ mountain in the world.
items.h: Short adjectives use 'the + adjective + -est'.
items.hd: Kurze Adjektive bilden den Superlativ mit 'the + Adjektiv + -est'.
items.e: 'High' is a short adjective, so we say 'the highest'. Superlatives always need 'the'.
items.ed: 'High' ist ein kurzes Adjektiv, deshalb sagen wir 'the highest'. Superlative brauchen immer 'the'.

items.p: This is ___ book I have ever read.
items.h: 'Good' has an irregular superlative form.
items.hd: 'Good' hat eine unregelmäßige Superlativform.
items.e: 'Good' is irregular: good → better → the best. We never say 'goodest' or 'most good'.
items.ed: 'Good' ist unregelmäßig: good → better → the best. Wir sagen nie 'goodest' oder 'most good'.

items.p: English is ___ subject at our school.
items.h: Long adjectives (3+ syllables) use 'the most + adjective'.
items.hd: Lange Adjektive (3+ Silben) bilden den Superlativ mit 'the most + Adjektiv'.
items.e: 'Popular' has three syllables, so we use 'the most popular', not 'the popularest'.
items.ed: 'Popular' hat drei Silben, deshalb sagen wir 'the most popular', nicht 'the popularest'.

items.p: My sister is ___ person in our family.
items.h: Two-syllable adjectives ending in -y change to -iest.
items.hd: Zweisilbige Adjektive auf -y ändern sich zu -iest.
items.e: For adjectives ending in -y, change -y to -i and add -est: funny → the funniest.
items.ed: Bei Adjektiven auf -y wird -y zu -i und man fügt -est an: funny → the funniest.

items.p: Winter is ___ season in Austria.
items.h: 'Cold' is a short adjective. Superlatives always need 'the'.
items.hd: 'Cold' ist ein kurzes Adjektiv. Superlative brauchen immer 'the'.
items.e: 'Cold' is a short adjective → the coldest. Don't forget 'the' before a superlative!
items.ed: 'Cold' ist ein kurzes Adjektiv → the coldest. Vergiss 'the' vor dem Superlativ nicht!

items.p: That was ___ film I have ever seen.
items.h: 'Bad' has an irregular superlative form, just like 'good'.
items.hd: 'Bad' hat eine unregelmäßige Superlativform, genau wie 'good'.
items.e: 'Bad' is irregular: bad → worse → the worst. Never say 'baddest' or 'most bad'.
items.ed: 'Bad' ist unregelmäßig: bad → worse → the worst. Sag nie 'baddest' oder 'most bad'.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: She is the tallest girl in our class.
a: She is the tallest girl in our class.
ds: She is tallest girl in our class.
She is the most tallest girl in our class.
She is the taller girl in our class.
items.h: Superlatives always need 'the' and only ONE superlative form.
items.hd: Superlative brauchen immer 'the' und nur EINE Superlativform.
items.e: Short adjectives form the superlative with 'the + -est'. 'Tall' → 'the tallest'. Never combine 'most' and '-est'.
items.ed: Kurze Adjektive bilden den Superlativ mit 'the + -est'. 'Tall' → 'the tallest'. Kombiniere nie 'most' und '-est'.

items.p: Which sentence uses the superlative correctly?
items.c: This is the most expensive phone in the shop.
a: This is the most expensive phone in the shop.
ds: This is the expensivest phone in the shop.
This is most expensive phone in the shop.
This is the more expensive phone in the shop.
items.h: Long adjectives use 'the most + adjective'.
items.hd: Lange Adjektive verwenden 'the most + Adjektiv'.
items.e: 'Expensive' is a long adjective (4 syllables), so we use 'the most expensive'.
items.ed: 'Expensive' ist ein langes Adjektiv (4 Silben), deshalb verwenden wir 'the most expensive'.

items.p: Choose the correct option: My dog is ___ pet in the neighbourhood.
items.h: What happens to adjectives ending in -y?
items.hd: Was passiert mit Adjektiven auf -y?
items.e: 'Friendly' ends in -y → change -y to -i and add -est: the friendliest.
items.ed: 'Friendly' endet auf -y → ändere -y zu -i und füge -est an: the friendliest.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: He / is / the / fastest / runner / in / the / school
items.c: He is the fastest runner in the school.
a: He is the fastest runner in the school.
He is the fastest runner in the school
items.h: What word is always needed before a superlative?
items.hd: Welches Wort braucht man immer vor einem Superlativ?
items.e: Superlatives always need 'the': the fastest, the biggest, the best.
items.ed: Superlative brauchen immer 'the': the fastest, the biggest, the best.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: This is the most biggest cake I have ever seen.
items.c: This is the biggest cake I have ever seen.
a: This is the biggest cake I have ever seen.
This is the biggest cake I have ever seen
items.h: Can you use 'most' AND '-est' together?
items.hd: Kann man 'most' UND '-est' zusammen verwenden?
items.e: Never combine 'most' and '-est'. Use only one: the biggest (not 'the most biggest').
items.ed: Kombiniere nie 'most' und '-est'. Verwende nur eines: the biggest (nicht 'the most biggest').

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She is the goodest singer in the choir.
items.c: She is the best singer in the choir.
a: She is the best singer in the choir.
She is the best singer in the choir
items.h: 'Good' does not follow the regular -est rule.
items.hd: 'Good' folgt nicht der regulären -est-Regel.
items.e: 'Good' is irregular: good → better → the best. 'Goodest' does not exist.
items.ed: 'Good' ist unregelmäßig: good → better → the best. 'Goodest' gibt es nicht.

items.p: Your class is talking about heights. Tom is the tallest. Tell a new student: 'Tom is ___ boy in the class.'
a: the tallest boy in the class
items.h: Superlative of short adjectives: the + adjective + -est.
items.hd: Superlativ kurzer Adjektive: the + Adjektiv + -est.
items.e: Superlative: tall → the tallest. Always use 'the' before the superlative.
items.ed: Superlativ: tall → the tallest. Verwende immer 'the' vor dem Superlativ.

items.p: Your family visited a museum at the weekend. You loved it! Tell your friend: 'It is ___ place in our town.'
a: the most interesting place in our town
items.h: Long adjectives use 'the most' + adjective for the superlative.
items.hd: Lange Adjektive verwenden 'the most' + Adjektiv für den Superlativ.
items.e: Superlative of long adjectives: the most + interesting. It is the most interesting place.
items.ed: Superlativ langer Adjektive: the most + interesting. It is the most interesting place.

items.p: You're complaining about homework. This exercise was terrible. Say: 'It is ___ exercise in the book!'
a: the worst exercise in the book
items.h: 'Bad' is irregular: bad → worst (not 'the baddest' or 'the most bad').
items.hd: 'Bad' ist unregelmäßig: bad → worst (nicht 'the baddest' oder 'the most bad').
items.e: Irregular superlative: bad → the worst. It is the worst exercise.
items.ed: Unregelmäßiger Superlativ: bad → the worst. It is the worst exercise.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Das ist das größte Haus in der Straße.
items.c: That is the biggest house in the street.
a: That is the biggest house in the street.
That is the biggest house in the street
This is the biggest house in the street.
This is the biggest house in the street
That's the biggest house in the street.
That's the biggest house in the street
It is the biggest house in the street.
It's the biggest house in the street.
items.h: How do you say 'das größte' in English? Remember: big → bigger → ...
items.hd: Wie sagt man 'das größte' auf Englisch? Denk daran: big → bigger → ...
items.e: 'Das größte' = 'the biggest'. Big doubles the final consonant: big → bigger → the biggest.
items.ed: 'Das größte' = 'the biggest'. Big verdoppelt den Endkonsonanten: big → bigger → the biggest.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Mathe ist das schwierigste Fach.
items.c: Maths is the most difficult subject.
a: Maths is the most difficult subject.
Maths is the most difficult subject
Math is the most difficult subject.
Math is the most difficult subject
items.h: 'Schwierig' = 'difficult'. How many syllables does 'difficult' have?
items.hd: 'Schwierig' = 'difficult'. Wie viele Silben hat 'difficult'?
items.e: 'Difficult' has three syllables → the most difficult. You can also say 'the hardest' (short adjective).
items.ed: 'Difficult' hat drei Silben → the most difficult. Man kann auch 'the hardest' sagen (kurzes Adjektiv).

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: youngest / is / the / she / in / player / the team
items.c: She is the youngest player in the team.
a: She is the youngest player in the team.
She is the youngest player in the team
items.h: Start with the subject, then the verb 'is', then the superlative.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Subjekt, dann das Verb 'is', dann der Superlativ.
items.e: The correct order is: Subject + is + the + superlative + noun + prepositional phrase.
items.ed: Die richtige Reihenfolge ist: Subjekt + is + the + Superlativ + Nomen + Präpositionalphrase.

items.p: Match each adjective with its correct superlative form: 1) good  2) bad  3) happy  4) beautiful  5) big
items.h: Two of these adjectives are irregular. The others follow spelling rules.
items.hd: Zwei dieser Adjektive sind unregelmäßig. Die anderen folgen Rechtschreibregeln.
items.e: good → the best (irregular), bad → the worst (irregular), happy → the happiest (-y → -iest), beautiful → the most beautiful (long adj), big → the biggest (double consonant).
items.ed: good → the best (unregelmäßig), bad → the worst (unregelmäßig), happy → the happiest (-y → -iest), beautiful → the most beautiful (langes Adj.), big → the biggest (Konsonant verdoppeln).

items.p: Your PE teacher asks who is the fastest runner. Which sentence is correct?
items.c: Anna is the fastest runner in our class.
a: Anna is the fastest runner in our class.
ds: Anna is the most fastest runner in our class.
Anna is the faster runner in our class.
Anna is fastest runner in our class.
items.h: Superlative: the + adjective + -est. Don't forget 'the'!
items.hd: Superlativ: the + Adjektiv + -est. Vergiss 'the' nicht!
items.e: Superlative of short adjectives: the + fastest. Anna is the fastest runner in our class.
items.ed: Superlativ kurzer Adjektive: the + fastest. Anna is the fastest runner in our class.

items.p: My bike is ___ your bike. They are the same speed.
items.h: Use 'as + base adjective + as' to say two things are equal.
items.hd: Verwende 'as + Grundform des Adjektivs + as', um zu sagen, dass zwei Dinge gleich sind.
items.e: We use 'as + adjective (base form) + as' for equal comparisons: as fast as.
items.ed: Wir verwenden 'as + Adjektiv (Grundform) + as' für Gleichheitsvergleiche: as fast as.

items.p: Tom is ___ his brother. They are the same height.
items.h: 'As...as' always uses the base form of the adjective.
items.hd: 'As...as' verwendet immer die Grundform des Adjektivs.
items.e: When two things are equal, use 'as + adjective (base form) + as': as tall as.
items.ed: Wenn zwei Dinge gleich sind, verwende 'as + Adjektiv (Grundform) + as': as tall as.

items.p: This pizza is ___ that one. I like both!
items.h: Even irregular adjectives use the base form in 'as...as'.
items.hd: Auch unregelmäßige Adjektive verwenden die Grundform in 'as...as'.
items.e: In 'as...as' we always use the base form: as good as (not 'as better as').
items.ed: In 'as...as' verwenden wir immer die Grundform: as good as (nicht 'as better as').

items.p: The test was ___ I expected. It was easier!
items.h: The negative form is 'not as + adjective + as'.
items.hd: Die verneinte Form ist 'not as + Adjektiv + as'.
items.e: 'Not as...as' means something is less than something else: not as hard as = easier.
items.ed: 'Not as...as' bedeutet, dass etwas weniger ist als etwas anderes: not as hard as = einfacher.

items.p: My cat is ___ your dog. They both weigh 5 kg.
items.h: Use the base form of the adjective, not the comparative.
items.hd: Verwende die Grundform des Adjektivs, nicht den Komparativ.
items.e: 'As...as' always takes the base form: as heavy as (not 'as heavier as').
items.ed: 'As...as' nimmt immer die Grundform: as heavy as (nicht 'as heavier as').
