items.p: This homework isn't ___ yesterday's homework. Yesterday's was more difficult.
items.h: Even with long adjectives, 'as...as' uses the base form — no 'more'!
items.hd: Auch bei langen Adjektiven verwendet 'as...as' die Grundform — kein 'more'!
items.e: In 'as...as', always use the base form: as difficult as (never 'as more difficult as').
items.ed: In 'as...as' immer die Grundform verwenden: as difficult as (nie 'as more difficult as').

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: She is as clever as her sister.
a: She is as clever as her sister.
ds: She is so clever as her sister.
She is as cleverer as her sister.
She is as clever like her sister.
items.h: The correct pattern is 'as + base adjective + as'.
items.hd: Das richtige Muster ist 'as + Grundform + as'.
items.e: The correct structure is 'as + adjective (base form) + as': as clever as.
items.ed: Die richtige Struktur ist 'as + Adjektiv (Grundform) + as': as clever as.

items.p: Which sentence uses 'not as...as' correctly?
items.c: The blue car is not as expensive as the red one.
a: The blue car is not as expensive as the red one.
ds: The blue car is not so expensive as the red one.
The blue car is as not expensive as the red one.
The blue car is not as more expensive as the red one.
items.h: The negative form is 'not as + base adjective + as'.
items.hd: Die verneinte Form ist 'not as + Grundform + as'.
items.e: 'Not as...as' uses the base form: not as expensive as. 'Not' comes before the first 'as'.
items.ed: 'Not as...as' verwendet die Grundform: not as expensive as. 'Not' steht vor dem ersten 'as'.

items.p: Choose the correct option: The maths test was ___ the English test.
items.h: Where does 'not' go in 'as...as'?
items.hd: Wo steht 'not' in 'as...as'?
items.e: 'Not' comes before the first 'as': not as easy as. Always use the base form.
items.ed: 'Not' steht vor dem ersten 'as': not as easy as. Verwende immer die Grundform.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She is so tall as her mum.
items.c: She is as tall as her mum.
a: She is as tall as her mum.
She is as tall as her mum
items.h: In English, we don't use 'so...as' for comparisons. What do we use instead?
items.hd: Im Englischen verwenden wir nicht 'so...as' für Vergleiche. Was verwenden wir stattdessen?
items.e: In English we say 'as...as', not 'so...as'. German uses 'so...wie', but English uses 'as...as'.
items.ed: Im Englischen sagen wir 'as...as', nicht 'so...as'. Deutsch verwendet 'so...wie', aber Englisch verwendet 'as...as'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: This bag is as cheaper as that one.
items.c: This bag is as cheap as that one.
a: This bag is as cheap as that one.
This bag is as cheap as that one
items.h: Which form of the adjective do we use between 'as...as'?
items.hd: Welche Form des Adjektivs verwenden wir zwischen 'as...as'?
items.e: 'As...as' always uses the base form of the adjective: as cheap as (not 'as cheaper as').
items.ed: 'As...as' verwendet immer die Grundform des Adjektivs: as cheap as (nicht 'as cheaper as').

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: My phone is as good like yours.
items.c: My phone is as good as yours.
a: My phone is as good as yours.
My phone is as good as yours
items.h: What word should come after the adjective in 'as...as'?
items.hd: Welches Wort sollte nach dem Adjektiv in 'as...as' kommen?
items.e: The structure is 'as + adjective + as' (not 'like'). In English we say 'as good as', not 'as good like'.
items.ed: Die Struktur ist 'as + Adjektiv + as' (nicht 'like'). Im Englischen sagen wir 'as good as', nicht 'as good like'.

items.p: You and your friend are racing. The dog is slower than the cat. Describe it differently: 'The dog is ___.'
items.c: not as fast as the cat
a: not as fast as the cat
not as fast as the cat.
items.h: 'Not as...as' means 'less than'. Think of the opposite adjective.
items.hd: 'Not as...as' bedeutet 'weniger als'. Denk an das Gegenteil des Adjektivs.
items.e: 'Slower than' = 'not as fast as'. Use 'not as + adjective + as' for inequality.
items.ed: 'Slower than' = 'not as fast as'. Verwende 'not as + Adjektiv + as' für Ungleichheit.

items.p: Lisa and Anna measured their height. They're both 150 cm! Tell your mum: 'Lisa is ___.'
items.h: 'As...as' shows two things are equal. Both girls are 150 cm.
items.hd: 'As...as' zeigt, dass zwei Dinge gleich sind. Beide Mädchen sind 150 cm.
items.e: 'As tall as' shows equality. Lisa is as tall as Anna — they're the same height.
items.ed: 'As tall as' zeigt Gleichheit. Lisa is as tall as Anna — sie sind gleich groß.

items.p: You're comparing bedrooms with your friend. Your room is smaller. Say: 'My room is ___.'
items.c: not as big as your room
a: not as big as your room
not as big as your room.
not as large as your room
items.h: 'Not as...as' means 'less than'. Think of the opposite of 'smaller'.
items.hd: 'Not as...as' bedeutet 'weniger als'. Denk an das Gegenteil von 'smaller'.
items.e: 'Smaller than' = 'not as big as'. Use the positive adjective between 'not as...as'.
items.ed: 'Smaller than' = 'not as big as'. Verwende das positive Adjektiv zwischen 'not as...as'.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Mein Bruder ist so groß wie mein Vater.
items.c: My brother is as tall as my father.
a: My brother is as tall as my father.
My brother is as tall as my father
My brother is as tall as my dad.
My brother is as tall as my dad
items.h: German 'so...wie' = English 'as...as'. Don't use 'so' in English!
items.hd: Deutsch 'so...wie' = Englisch 'as...as'. Verwende kein 'so' im Englischen!
items.e: German 'so groß wie' = English 'as tall as'. The pattern 'so...wie' becomes 'as...as'.
items.ed: Deutsch 'so groß wie' = Englisch 'as tall as'. Das Muster 'so...wie' wird zu 'as...as'.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Das Buch ist nicht so spannend wie der Film.
items.c: The book is not as exciting as the film.
a: The book is not as exciting as the film.
The book is not as exciting as the film
The book isn't as exciting as the film.
The book isn't as exciting as the film
The book is not as exciting as the movie.
The book isn't as exciting as the movie.
The book isn't as exciting as the movie
items.h: 'Nicht so...wie' = 'not as...as'. Use the base form of the adjective.
items.hd: 'Nicht so...wie' = 'not as...as'. Verwende die Grundform des Adjektivs.
items.e: 'Nicht so spannend wie' = 'not as exciting as'. German 'nicht so...wie' = English 'not as...as'.
items.ed: 'Nicht so spannend wie' = 'not as exciting as'. Deutsch 'nicht so...wie' = Englisch 'not as...as'.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: as / is / my / old / as / your / cat / cat
items.c: My cat is as old as your cat.
a: My cat is as old as your cat.
My cat is as old as your cat
items.h: Start with 'My cat is', then build the comparison.
items.hd: Beginne mit 'My cat is', dann bau den Vergleich.
items.e: The pattern is: Subject + is + as + adjective + as + object: My cat is as old as your cat.
items.ed: Das Muster ist: Subjekt + is + as + Adjektiv + as + Objekt: My cat is as old as your cat.

items.p: Match the beginnings with the correct endings: 1) She is as old  2) He isn't as strong  3) The test was as easy  4) This pizza isn't as tasty
ds: b: as the one we had yesterday.
items.h: Look for the matching noun or pronoun in each ending.
items.hd: Suche nach dem passenden Nomen oder Pronomen in jeder Endung.
items.e: 1) as old as her brother (c), 2) as strong as his dad (a), 3) as easy as I thought (d), 4) as tasty as yours (b).
items.ed: 1) as old as her brother (c), 2) as strong as his dad (a), 3) as easy as I thought (d), 4) as tasty as yours (b).

items.p: Tom is as old as Lucy. Ask about: Lucy's age compared to Tom's.
items.c: Is Lucy as old as Tom?
a: Is Lucy as old as Tom?
Is Lucy as old as Tom
items.h: Turn 'Lucy is as old as Tom' into a yes/no question.
items.hd: Mache aus 'Lucy is as old as Tom' eine Ja/Nein-Frage.
items.e: To form a question with 'as...as', move 'is' to the front: Is Lucy as old as Tom?
items.ed: Um eine Frage mit 'as...as' zu bilden, stelle 'is' an den Anfang: Is Lucy as old as Tom?

items.p: The bank is ___ the supermarket. You can see it from there.
items.h: 'Opposite' is used WITHOUT 'of' in English.
items.hd: 'Opposite' wird im Englischen OHNE 'of' verwendet.
items.e: In English we say 'opposite the supermarket' (not 'opposite of'). German uses 'gegenüber von', but English drops the 'of'.
items.ed: Im Englischen sagen wir 'opposite the supermarket' (nicht 'opposite of'). Deutsch verwendet 'gegenüber von', aber Englisch lässt 'of' weg.

items.p: The bookshop is ___ the café. They are next to each other.
items.h: 'Beside' means 'next to' and doesn't need 'of'.
items.hd: 'Beside' bedeutet 'neben' und braucht kein 'of'.
items.e: 'Beside' = next to. Don't confuse 'beside' (= neben) with 'besides' (= außerdem).
items.ed: 'Beside' = neben. Verwechsle nicht 'beside' (= neben) mit 'besides' (= außerdem).

items.p: Don't turn here. Go ___ for 100 metres and then turn right at the traffic lights.
items.h: The direction word for 'keep going in the same direction' is 'straight'.
items.hd: Das Richtungswort für 'immer geradeaus weiter' ist 'straight'.
items.e: 'Go straight' means continue in the same direction. 'Straight' is already an adverb — don't add -ly.
items.ed: 'Go straight' bedeutet, in der gleichen Richtung weitergehen. 'Straight' ist bereits ein Adverb — füge kein -ly hinzu.

items.p: The pharmacy is ___ the corner from the post office.
items.h: If you need to walk past the corner to find it, use 'round' the corner.
items.hd: Wenn man um die Ecke gehen muss, um es zu finden, verwendet man 'round the corner'.
items.e: 'Round the corner' (or 'around the corner') means you have to go past the corner to find it.
items.ed: 'Round the corner' (oder 'around the corner') bedeutet, dass man um die Ecke gehen muss.

items.p: Turn ___ at the church and the school is on your right.
items.h: We say 'turn left' or 'turn right' — no extra words needed.
items.hd: Wir sagen 'turn left' oder 'turn right' — keine zusätzlichen Wörter nötig.
items.e: 'Turn left' and 'turn right' don't need 'in', 'to', or 'on': just 'turn left'.
items.ed: 'Turn left' und 'turn right' brauchen kein 'in', 'to' oder 'on': einfach 'turn left'.

items.p: Go ___ the park and the library is on the other side.
items.h: 'Go past' means walk by something without stopping.
items.hd: 'Go past' bedeutet, an etwas vorbeizugehen.
items.e: 'Go past' (preposition) means walk by. Don't confuse with 'passed' (past tense of 'pass').
items.ed: 'Go past' (Präposition) bedeutet vorbeigehen. Verwechsle es nicht mit 'passed' (Vergangenheit von 'pass').

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: The cinema is opposite the train station.
a: The cinema is opposite the train station.
ds: The cinema is opposite of the train station.
The cinema is opposite to the train station.
The cinema is opposite from the train station.
items.h: 'Opposite' is followed directly by the noun — no extra preposition.
items.hd: 'Opposite' steht direkt vor dem Nomen — keine zusätzliche Präposition.
items.e: In English, 'opposite' is used directly before the noun: opposite the station (not 'opposite of/to/from').
items.ed: Im Englischen steht 'opposite' direkt vor dem Nomen: opposite the station (nicht 'opposite of/to/from').

items.p: Which direction instruction is correct?
items.c: Go straight and turn right at the corner.
a: Go straight and turn right at the corner.
ds: Go straight and turn in the right at the corner.
Go straightly and turn right at the corner.
Go straight and turn on right at the corner.
items.h: Think carefully about the grammar rule and choose the best answer.
items.hd: Überlege dir die Grammatikregel und wähle die beste Antwort.
items.e: 'Go straight' (no -ly) and 'turn right' (no 'in the' or 'on') are correct.
items.ed: 'Go straight' (kein -ly) und 'turn right' (kein 'in the' oder 'on') sind richtig.

items.p: Choose the correct option: The bakery is ___ the shoe shop.
items.h: 'Beside' = next to. 'Besides' = in addition to. Which one means 'next to'?
items.hd: 'Beside' = neben. 'Besides' = außerdem. Welches bedeutet 'neben'?
items.e: 'Beside' means 'next to'. 'Besides' means 'in addition to' — they are different words!
items.ed: 'Beside' bedeutet 'neben'. 'Besides' bedeutet 'außerdem' — das sind verschiedene Wörter!

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: The school is opposite of the hospital.
items.c: The school is opposite the hospital.
a: The school is opposite the hospital.
The school is opposite the hospital
items.h: Does 'opposite' need 'of' after it in English?
items.hd: Braucht 'opposite' ein 'of' danach im Englischen?
items.e: In English we say 'opposite the hospital' without 'of'. German uses 'gegenüber von', but English drops the 'of'.
items.ed: Im Englischen sagen wir 'opposite the hospital' ohne 'of'. Deutsch verwendet 'gegenüber von', aber Englisch lässt 'of' weg.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Turn in the left at the church.
items.h: Do you need 'in the' before 'left' when giving directions?
items.hd: Braucht man 'in the' vor 'left' bei Wegbeschreibungen?
items.e: We say 'turn left' (not 'turn in the left'). No extra words are needed between 'turn' and 'left'.
items.ed: Wir sagen 'turn left' (nicht 'turn in the left'). Keine zusätzlichen Wörter zwischen 'turn' und 'left'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Go straightly down the road and the park is on your left.
items.c: Go straight down the road and the park is on your left.
a: Go straight down the road and the park is on your left.
Go straight down the road and the park is on your left
items.h: Is 'straightly' a real English word? Think about how 'straight' works.
items.hd: Ist 'straightly' ein echtes englisches Wort? Denke darüber nach, wie 'straight' funktioniert.
items.e: 'Straight' is already an adverb — 'straightly' does not exist. Say 'go straight'.
items.ed: 'Straight' ist bereits ein Adverb — 'straightly' gibt es nicht. Sag 'go straight'.

items.p: A tourist asks you for help on the street. Say it more politely: 'Turn left here.' → 'Could you ___?'
items.h: Add 'please' to make a polite request with 'Could you...?'
items.hd: Füge 'please' hinzu, um eine höfliche Bitte mit 'Could you...?' zu machen.
items.e: 'Could you turn left here, please?' is a polite way to give directions.
items.ed: 'Could you turn left here, please?' ist eine höfliche Art, Wegbeschreibungen zu geben.

items.p: You're explaining where your house is to a friend. The park is across the road from the library. Say: 'The park is ___ the library.'
items.h: 'Across the road from' and 'opposite' mean the same thing.
items.hd: 'Auf der anderen Straßenseite von' und 'opposite' bedeuten dasselbe.
items.e: 'Opposite' means directly across from something. The park is opposite the library.
items.ed: 'Opposite' bedeutet direkt gegenüber von etwas. The park is opposite the library.

items.p: You're giving directions to the new student. Tell them how to find the gym: '___ the cinema and keep going straight.'
items.h: 'Walk by' and 'go past' mean the same. Continue without stopping.
items.hd: 'Walk by' und 'go past' bedeuten dasselbe. Weitergehen ohne anzuhalten.
items.e: 'Go past' means to walk by something without stopping. Go past the cinema = walk by the cinema.
items.ed: 'Go past' bedeutet, an etwas vorbeizugehen. Go past the cinema = am Kino vorbeigehen.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Die Bäckerei ist gegenüber der Schule.
items.c: The bakery is opposite the school.
a: The bakery is opposite the school.
The bakery is opposite the school
The bakery is across from the school.
The bakery is across from the school
items.h: 'Gegenüber' = 'opposite'. Remember: no 'of' in English!
items.hd: 'Gegenüber' = 'opposite'. Denk daran: kein 'of' im Englischen!
items.e: 'Gegenüber der Schule' = 'opposite the school'. Don't add 'of' — that's a German pattern.
items.ed: 'Gegenüber der Schule' = 'opposite the school'. Füge kein 'of' hinzu — das ist ein deutsches Muster.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Geh geradeaus und dann biege rechts ab.
items.c: Go straight and then turn right.
a: Go straight and then turn right.
Go straight and then turn right
Go straight on and then turn right.
Go straight on and then turn right
Go straight ahead and then turn right.
Go straight ahead and then turn right
items.h: 'Geradeaus' = 'straight'. 'Biege rechts ab' = 'turn right'.
items.hd: 'Geradeaus' = 'straight'. 'Biege rechts ab' = 'turn right'.
items.e: 'Geh geradeaus' = 'Go straight'. 'Biege rechts ab' = 'turn right'. No extra prepositions needed.
items.ed: 'Geh geradeaus' = 'Go straight'. 'Biege rechts ab' = 'turn right'. Keine zusätzlichen Präpositionen nötig.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: the / turn / at / left / traffic lights
items.c: Turn left at the traffic lights.
a: Turn left at the traffic lights.
Turn left at the traffic lights
items.h: Start with the direction command, then say where.
items.hd: Beginne mit der Richtungsanweisung, dann sage wo.
items.e: Direction instructions follow: verb + direction + at + place: Turn left at the traffic lights.
items.ed: Richtungsanweisungen folgen: Verb + Richtung + at + Ort: Turn left at the traffic lights.

items.p: Match the English direction with its meaning: 1) go straight  2) turn left  3) go past  4) opposite  5) beside
items.h: Think about what each direction phrase means in German.
items.hd: Überlege, was jede Richtungsangabe auf Deutsch bedeutet.
items.e: go straight = geradeaus gehen (c), turn left = links abbiegen (e), go past = vorbeigehen (a), opposite = gegenüber (d), beside = neben (b).
items.ed: go straight = geradeaus gehen (c), turn left = links abbiegen (e), go past = vorbeigehen (a), opposite = gegenüber (d), beside = neben (b).

items.p: You are lost and want to find the train station. Ask for directions politely.
items.c: Excuse me, how do I get to the train station?
a: Excuse me, how do I get to the train station?
Excuse me, how do I get to the train station
How do I get to the train station?
How do I get to the train station
Excuse me, where is the train station?
Could you tell me the way to the train station?
Can you tell me the way to the train station?
items.h: Start with 'Excuse me' and ask 'How do I get to...?'
items.hd: Beginne mit 'Excuse me' und frage 'How do I get to...?'
items.e: To ask for directions politely: 'Excuse me, how do I get to the train station?'
items.ed: Um höflich nach dem Weg zu fragen: 'Excuse me, how do I get to the train station?'

items.p: I ___ do my homework before I can play.
items.h: 'I' uses 'have to', not 'has to'.
items.hd: 'I' verwendet 'have to', nicht 'has to'.
items.e: With 'I/you/we/they', use 'have to'. Only 'he/she/it' uses 'has to'.
items.ed: Mit 'I/you/we/they' verwendet man 'have to'. Nur 'he/she/it' verwendet 'has to'.

items.p: She ___ wear a school uniform every day.
items.h: 'She' is 3rd person — which form of 'have to' do we need?
items.hd: 'She' ist 3. Person — welche Form von 'have to' brauchen wir?
items.e: 'She/he/it' uses 'has to': She has to wear a uniform. Don't forget the 'to'!
items.ed: 'She/he/it' verwendet 'has to': She has to wear a uniform. Vergiss das 'to' nicht!

items.p: You ___ bring your own lunch. The school gives you food.
items.h: 'Don't have to' means it's not necessary. 'Mustn't' means it's forbidden.
items.hd: 'Don't have to' bedeutet, es ist nicht nötig. 'Mustn't' bedeutet, es ist verboten.
items.e: 'Don't have to' = not necessary. 'Mustn't' = forbidden. The school provides food, so bringing lunch is NOT NECESSARY (not forbidden).
items.ed: 'Don't have to' = nicht nötig. 'Mustn't' = verboten. Die Schule gibt Essen, also ist es NICHT NÖTIG, Mittagessen mitzubringen (nicht verboten).

items.p: Tom ___ get up early tomorrow. It's a school day.
items.h: 'Tom' = 'he'. What form does 'he' need?
items.hd: 'Tom' = 'he'. Welche Form braucht 'he'?
items.e: 'Tom' (= he) uses 'has to': Tom has to get up early. Remember: 'have to' changes to 'has to' for 3rd person.
items.ed: 'Tom' (= he) verwendet 'has to': Tom has to get up early. Denk daran: 'have to' wird zu 'has to' in der 3. Person.

items.p: She ___ clean her room. Her mum already did it.
items.h: Is it NOT NECESSARY or FORBIDDEN? Also: 'she' = 3rd person.
items.hd: Ist es NICHT NÖTIG oder VERBOTEN? Auch: 'she' = 3. Person.
items.e: 'She' uses 'doesn't have to' (not necessary). Her mum already cleaned, so it's not necessary — not forbidden.
items.ed: 'She' verwendet 'doesn't have to' (nicht nötig). Die Mutter hat schon geputzt, also ist es nicht nötig — nicht verboten.

items.p: We ___ run in the corridors. It's a school rule.
items.h: Is running in the corridors NOT NECESSARY or FORBIDDEN?
items.hd: Ist Laufen im Gang NICHT NÖTIG oder VERBOTEN?
items.e: 'Mustn't' = forbidden (it's a rule!). 'Don't have to' = not necessary. Running in corridors is FORBIDDEN, so we use 'mustn't'.
items.ed: 'Mustn't' = verboten (es ist eine Regel!). 'Don't have to' = nicht nötig. Im Gang laufen ist VERBOTEN, also verwenden wir 'mustn't'.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: She has to finish her project by Friday.
a: She has to finish her project by Friday.
ds: She have to finish her project by Friday.
She has finish her project by Friday.
She haves to finish her project by Friday.
items.h: 'She' is 3rd person. What form of 'have to' is correct?
items.hd: 'She' ist 3. Person. Welche Form von 'have to' ist richtig?
items.e: 'She/he/it' uses 'has to' (not 'have to'). 'Have to' always needs 'to' before the main verb.
items.ed: 'She/he/it' verwendet 'has to' (nicht 'have to'). 'Have to' braucht immer 'to' vor dem Hauptverb.

items.p: Which sentence means 'It is not necessary'?
items.c: You don't have to wear a tie.
a: You don't have to wear a tie.
ds: You hasn't to wear a tie.
You haven't to wear a tie.
items.h: 'Don't have to' = not necessary. 'Mustn't' = forbidden. Which one fits?
items.hd: 'Don't have to' = nicht nötig. 'Mustn't' = verboten. Welches passt?
items.e: 'Don't have to' means 'it is not necessary'. 'Mustn't' means 'it is forbidden'. These are NOT the same!
items.ed: 'Don't have to' bedeutet 'es ist nicht nötig'. 'Mustn't' bedeutet 'es ist verboten'. Das ist NICHT dasselbe!

items.p: Choose the correct sentence: The sign says 'No swimming'. You ___.
items.h: The sign says 'No swimming' — is it forbidden or just not necessary?
items.hd: Das Schild sagt 'No swimming' — ist es verboten oder nur nicht nötig?
items.e: 'No swimming' = it's forbidden. We use 'mustn't'. Be careful: German 'musst nicht' = English 'don't have to' (not necessary), NOT 'mustn't' (forbidden)!
items.ed: 'No swimming' = es ist verboten. Wir verwenden 'mustn't'. Achtung: Deutsch 'musst nicht' = Englisch 'don't have to' (nicht nötig), NICHT 'mustn't' (verboten)!

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She have to study for the test.
items.c: She has to study for the test.
a: She has to study for the test.
She has to study for the test
items.h: 'She' is 3rd person. What's the correct form?
items.hd: 'She' ist 3. Person. Was ist die richtige Form?
items.e: 'She' is 3rd person singular, so we use 'has to', not 'have to'.
items.ed: 'She' ist 3. Person Einzahl, deshalb verwenden wir 'has to', nicht 'have to'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: He hasn't to go to school on Saturday.
items.c: He doesn't have to go to school on Saturday.
a: He doesn't have to go to school on Saturday.
He doesn't have to go to school on Saturday
He does not have to go to school on Saturday.
items.h: How do you make 'have to' negative? Think about 'do/does'.
items.hd: Wie verneint man 'have to'? Denke an 'do/does'.
items.e: 'Have to' is negated with 'don't/doesn't have to', not 'hasn't to'. It is not a modal verb!
items.ed: 'Have to' wird mit 'don't/doesn't have to' verneint, nicht mit 'hasn't to'. Es ist kein Modalverb!

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: You must not forget your homework. It's not necessary.
items.c: You don't have to forget your homework. It's not necessary.
a: You don't have to forget your homework.
You don't have to forget your homework. It's not necessary.
You don't have to forget your homework. It's not necessary
items.h: If it's 'not necessary', is 'must not' the right choice? Think about the difference.
items.hd: Wenn es 'nicht nötig' ist, ist 'must not' die richtige Wahl? Denke über den Unterschied nach.
items.e: 'Must not' means 'forbidden', but the context says 'not necessary'. Use 'don't have to' for 'not necessary'.
items.ed: 'Must not' bedeutet 'verboten', aber der Kontext sagt 'nicht nötig'. Verwende 'don't have to' für 'nicht nötig'.

items.p: Your friend is new at school and asks about the rules. Explain: 'We ___ (be quiet) in the library.'
items.c: have to be quiet in the library
a: have to be quiet in the library
items.h: 'Have to' expresses necessity or obligation. It's a rule.
items.hd: 'Have to' drückt Notwendigkeit oder Verpflichtung aus. Es ist eine Regel.
items.e: 'We have to be quiet in the library' — 'have to' shows it's necessary/a rule.
items.ed: 'We have to be quiet in the library' — 'have to' zeigt, dass es notwendig/eine Regel ist.

items.p: Your little brother is worried about getting up early on Saturday. Reassure him: 'He ___ (not / come early) — it's the weekend!'
a: does not have to come early
items.h: 'Doesn't have to' means it's NOT necessary (he can choose).
items.hd: 'Doesn't have to' bedeutet, es ist NICHT notwendig (er kann wählen).
items.e: 'He doesn't have to come early' — it's not necessary. He CAN sleep in on Saturday.
items.ed: 'He doesn't have to come early' — es ist nicht notwendig. Er KANN am Samstag ausschlafen.

items.p: You see a 'NO PHONES' sign in class. Tell the new student the rule: 'You ___ (use) phones in class.'
a: must not use phones in class
items.h: A 'NO PHONES' sign means it's forbidden. Which modal means 'it's forbidden'?
items.hd: Ein 'NO PHONES'-Schild bedeutet, es ist verboten. Welches Modalverb bedeutet 'es ist verboten'?
items.e: 'Mustn't' means it's forbidden/not allowed. You mustn't use phones = phones are not allowed.
items.ed: 'Mustn't' bedeutet, es ist verboten/nicht erlaubt. You mustn't use phones = Handys sind nicht erlaubt.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Wir müssen um 8 Uhr in der Schule sein.
items.c: We have to be at school at 8 o'clock.
a: We have to be at school at 8 o'clock.
We have to be at school at 8 o'clock
We have to be at school at eight o'clock.
We have to be at school at 8.
We have to be at school by 8 o'clock.
We have to be at school by 8.
items.h: 'Müssen' = 'have to' (not 'must to'!). Remember: no 'to' after 'must', but 'have TO' needs 'to'.
items.hd: 'Müssen' = 'have to' (nicht 'must to'!). Denk daran: kein 'to' nach 'must', aber 'have TO' braucht 'to'.
items.e: 'Müssen' = 'have to': We have to be at school at 8 o'clock. 'Have to' always includes 'to'.
items.ed: 'Müssen' = 'have to': We have to be at school at 8 o'clock. 'Have to' enthält immer 'to'.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Du musst dein Zimmer nicht aufräumen. Mama hat es schon gemacht.
items.c: You don't have to tidy your room. Mum has already done it.
a: You don't have to tidy your room. Mum has already done it.
You don't have to tidy your room. Mum has already done it
You don't have to tidy your room.
You don't have to tidy up your room.
You don't have to clean your room.
You do not have to tidy your room.
You don't have to tidy your room. Mum already did it.
items.h: Careful! German 'musst nicht' = English 'don't have to' (not necessary), NOT 'mustn't' (forbidden)!
items.hd: Achtung! Deutsch 'musst nicht' = Englisch 'don't have to' (nicht nötig), NICHT 'mustn't' (verboten)!
items.e: German 'musst nicht' = 'don't have to' (not necessary). This is a classic L1 trap! 'Mustn't' means 'forbidden' in English.
items.ed: Deutsch 'musst nicht' = 'don't have to' (nicht nötig). Das ist eine klassische L1-Falle! 'Mustn't' bedeutet 'verboten' im Englischen.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: to / have / we / early / get up / tomorrow
items.c: We have to get up early tomorrow.
a: We have to get up early tomorrow.
We have to get up early tomorrow
Tomorrow we have to get up early.
Tomorrow we have to get up early
items.h: Start with 'We have to' and then add the rest.
items.hd: Beginne mit 'We have to' und füge dann den Rest hinzu.
items.e: The structure is: Subject + have to + base verb + rest: We have to get up early tomorrow.
items.ed: Die Struktur ist: Subjekt + have to + Grundform des Verbs + Rest: We have to get up early tomorrow.

items.p: Match each situation with the correct expression: 1) It's a school rule — no phones.  2) The bus is free for children.  3) He's the team captain.  4) Saturday is not a school day.
ds: a: You don't have to pay.
b: You don't have to go to school.
c: You have to turn off your phone.
d: He has to lead the team.
items.h: Think: is it necessary, not necessary, or forbidden?
items.hd: Denke: ist es notwendig, nicht nötig, oder verboten?
items.e: 1) forbidden → You mustn't use phones (c). 2) not necessary → You don't have to pay (a). 3) necessary → He has to lead the team (d). 4) not necessary → You don't have to go to school (b).
items.ed: 1) verboten → You mustn't use phones (c). 2) nicht nötig → You don't have to pay (a). 3) notwendig → He has to lead the team (d). 4) nicht nötig → You don't have to go to school (b).

items.p: She has to wear a uniform. Ask about: the uniform rule.
items.c: Does she have to wear a uniform?
a: Does she have to wear a uniform?
Does she have to wear a uniform
items.h: Questions with 'have to' use 'Do/Does + subject + have to'.
items.hd: Fragen mit 'have to' verwenden 'Do/Does + Subjekt + have to'.
items.e: To ask questions with 'have to': Does she have to wear a uniform? (not 'Has she to...').
items.ed: Um Fragen mit 'have to' zu stellen: Does she have to wear a uniform? (nicht 'Has she to...').

items.p: It ___ rain later, so take an umbrella.
items.h: After 'might', use the base form of the verb directly.
items.hd: Nach 'might' kommt direkt die Grundform des Verbs.
items.e: 'Might' is followed directly by the base form: might rain. No 'to', no -s, no extra modal.
items.ed: Nach 'might' kommt direkt die Grundform: might rain. Kein 'to', kein -s, kein zusätzliches Modalverb.

items.p: We ___ go to the swimming pool on Saturday.
items.h: Which word expresses possibility? It never changes form.
items.hd: Welches Wort drückt Möglichkeit aus? Es ändert sich nie.
items.e: 'Might' stays the same for all persons. It is followed by the base form of the verb.
items.ed: 'Might' bleibt für alle Personen gleich. Danach kommt die Grundform des Verbs.

items.p: She ___ come to the party. She isn't sure yet.
items.h: Modal verbs like 'might' never change, even with he/she/it.
items.hd: Modalverben wie 'might' ändern sich nie, auch nicht bei he/she/it.
items.e: 'Might' never takes -s, even with 'she'. She might come — not 'mights' or 'might comes'.
items.ed: 'Might' bekommt nie ein -s, auch nicht bei 'she'. She might come — nicht 'mights' oder 'might comes'.

items.p: Tom ___ not play football tomorrow. His leg hurts.
items.h: To make 'might' negative, just add 'not' after it.
items.hd: Um 'might' zu verneinen, füge einfach 'not' nach 'might' hinzu.
items.e: 'Might not' is the negative form. No auxiliary needed: He might not play.
items.ed: 'Might not' ist die verneinte Form. Kein Hilfsverb nötig: He might not play.

items.p: I'm not sure about the weather. It ___ be sunny or it ___ rain.
items.h: Both possibilities are uncertain. Use the same modal for both.
items.hd: Beide Möglichkeiten sind unsicher. Verwende für beide dasselbe Modalverb.
items.e: When both outcomes are uncertain, use 'might' for each: It might be sunny or it might rain.
items.ed: Wenn beide Ergebnisse unsicher sind, verwende 'might' für beide: It might be sunny or it might rain.

items.p: He ___ not ___ us. He looks very busy.
items.h: The structure is: might + not + base form.
items.hd: Die Struktur ist: might + not + Grundform.
items.e: 'Might not' is followed by the base form: might not help. No 'to', no -s, no extra auxiliary.
items.ed: Nach 'might not' kommt die Grundform: might not help. Kein 'to', kein -s, kein Hilfsverb.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: She might visit us next week.
a: She might visit us next week.
ds: She mights visit us next week.
She might to visit us next week.
She might visited us next week.
items.h: Remember: modal verbs never change form and are followed by the base form.
items.hd: Modalverben ändern sich nie und danach kommt die Grundform.
items.e: 'Might' + base form: She might visit. No -s on might, no 'to', no past form.
items.ed: 'Might' + Grundform: She might visit. Kein -s, kein 'to', keine Vergangenheitsform.

items.p: Which sentence means 'Maybe it won't snow'?
items.h: To say something possibly won't happen, use: might + not + base form.
items.hd: Um zu sagen, dass etwas möglicherweise nicht passiert: might + not + Grundform.
items.e: The negative of 'might' is 'might not' + base form. The word order is always: might not + verb.
items.ed: Die Verneinung von 'might' ist 'might not' + Grundform. Die Wortstellung ist immer: might not + Verb.

items.p: Choose the correct option: 'I'm not sure, but we ___ have a test on Friday.'
items.h: The speaker is uncertain. Which modal shows uncertainty?
items.hd: Der Sprecher ist unsicher. Welches Modalverb drückt Unsicherheit aus?
items.e: 'Might' expresses possibility and uncertainty. 'Will' and 'going to' are more certain. 'I'm not sure' signals uncertainty, so 'might' is correct.
items.ed: 'Might' drückt Möglichkeit und Unsicherheit aus. 'Will' und 'going to' sind sicherer. 'I'm not sure' zeigt Unsicherheit, also ist 'might' richtig.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: She / might / come / to / the / party
items.c: She might come to the party.
a: She might come to the party.
She might come to the party
items.h: Look at what comes right after 'might'. Is 'to' needed?
items.hd: Schau, was direkt nach 'might' kommt. Braucht man 'to'?
items.e: 'Might' is followed directly by the base form — no 'to': She might come.
items.ed: Nach 'might' kommt direkt die Grundform — kein 'to': She might come.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: He mights play basketball after school.
items.c: He might play basketball after school.
a: He might play basketball after school.
He might play basketball after school
items.h: Does 'might' change for he/she/it?
items.hd: Verändert sich 'might' bei he/she/it?
items.e: Modal verbs never add -s, even for 3rd person: He might play, not 'mights'.
items.ed: Modalverben bekommen nie ein -s, auch nicht in der 3. Person: He might play, nicht 'mights'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I might went to the cinema last weekend.
items.c: I might go to the cinema next weekend.
a: I might go to the cinema next weekend.
I might go to the cinema next weekend
items.h: 'Might' is always followed by the base form, not the past form.
items.hd: Nach 'might' kommt immer die Grundform, nicht die Vergangenheitsform.
items.e: 'Might' expresses future possibility and takes the base form: might go, not 'might went'. Also, 'might' doesn't work with 'last weekend' (past) — it's about future possibilities.
items.ed: 'Might' drückt zukünftige Möglichkeit aus und braucht die Grundform: might go, nicht 'might went'. 'Might' passt nicht zu 'last weekend' (Vergangenheit).

items.p: You're not sure about your weekend plans. Rewrite using 'might': 'Maybe I will buy a new game.' → 'I ___.'
items.c: I might buy a new game.
a: I might buy a new game.
I might buy a new game
items.h: 'Might' replaces 'maybe...will'. Might + base form (no 'to').
items.hd: 'Might' ersetzt 'maybe...will'. Might + Grundform (kein 'to').
items.e: 'Maybe I will' = 'I might'. Might shows something is possible but not certain.
items.ed: 'Maybe I will' = 'I might'. Might zeigt, dass etwas möglich aber nicht sicher ist.

items.p: Your classmate asks if Emma is coming to school. You're not sure. Say: 'She ___ (not come) to school tomorrow.'
items.c: might not come to school tomorrow
a: might not come to school tomorrow
might not come to school tomorrow.
She might not come to school tomorrow.
items.h: Negative: might not + base form. Don't use 'mightn't' (it's very rare).
items.hd: Verneinung: might not + Grundform.
items.e: 'She might not come' = 'maybe she won't come'. Might not shows uncertain negation.
items.ed: 'She might not come' = 'vielleicht kommt sie nicht'. Might not zeigt eine unsichere Verneinung.

items.p: Your family is thinking about a day trip. Rewrite using 'might': 'Perhaps we will visit the new museum.' → 'We ___.'
items.c: We might visit the new museum.
a: We might visit the new museum.
We might visit the new museum
items.h: 'Perhaps...will' = 'might'. Might + base form.
items.hd: 'Perhaps...will' = 'might'. Might + Grundform.
items.e: 'Perhaps we will visit' = 'We might visit'. Both express possibility.
items.ed: 'Perhaps we will visit' = 'We might visit'. Beide drücken Möglichkeit aus.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Wir gehen vielleicht morgen ins Kino.
items.c: We might go to the cinema tomorrow.
a: We might go to the cinema tomorrow.
We might go to the cinema tomorrow
We might go to the movies tomorrow.
We might go to the movies tomorrow.
items.h: 'Vielleicht' is expressed with 'might' here.
items.hd: 'Vielleicht' wird hier mit 'might' ausgedrückt.
items.e: German 'vielleicht' (maybe) with a verb is expressed using 'might' + base form in English.
items.ed: Deutsches 'vielleicht' mit einem Verb wird im Englischen mit 'might' + Grundform ausgedrückt.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Er kommt vielleicht nicht zur Schule. Er ist krank.
items.c: He might not come to school. He is sick.
a: He might not come to school. He is sick.
He might not come to school. He is sick
He might not come to school. He's sick.
He might not come to school. He's sick
He might not go to school. He is sick.
He might not go to school. He's sick.
He might not go to school. He is ill.
He might not come to school. He is ill.
items.h: 'Vielleicht nicht' = might not.
items.hd: 'Vielleicht nicht' = might not.
items.e: German 'vielleicht nicht' becomes 'might not' + base form: He might not come to school.
items.ed: Deutsches 'vielleicht nicht' wird zu 'might not' + Grundform: He might not come to school.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: might / we / park / to / go / the
items.c: We might go to the park.
a: We might go to the park.
We might go to the park
items.h: Start with the subject, then 'might', then the base form verb.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Subjekt, dann 'might', dann die Grundform des Verbs.
items.e: The word order is: Subject + might + base form + rest: We might go to the park.
items.ed: Die Wortstellung ist: Subjekt + might + Grundform + Rest: We might go to the park.

items.p:
Match each sentence beginning with its correct ending:
1. She might
2. They might not
3. It might
4. We might not
a. be sunny later.
b. have time for the trip.
c. come to the party late.
d. join the basketball team.
items.h: Check which ending makes the most sense with each beginning.
items.hd: Überprüfe, welches Ende am besten zu jedem Anfang passt.
items.e: 1-d: She might join the basketball team. 2-b: They might not have time. 3-a: It might be sunny. 4-c: We might not come late.
items.ed: 1-d: She might join... 2-b: They might not have time... 3-a: It might be sunny... 4-c: We might not come late...

items.p: You ask your friend about the weekend. She says: 'I might play tennis this afternoon.' What does she mean?
items.c: She's not sure yet — it's possible but not certain.
a: She's not sure yet — it's possible but not certain.
ds: She doesn't want to play tennis.
items.h: 'Might' shows something is possible but not sure. Is she certain or uncertain?
items.hd: 'Might' zeigt, dass etwas möglich aber nicht sicher ist. Ist sie sicher oder unsicher?
items.e: 'Might' expresses possibility — she's not sure yet. It's less certain than 'will'.
items.ed: 'Might' drückt Möglichkeit aus — sie ist sich noch nicht sicher. Es ist weniger sicher als 'will'.

items.p: ___, we went to the beach. Then, we had ice cream.
items.h: Which word starts a sequence of events?
items.hd: Welches Wort beginnt eine Abfolge von Ereignissen?
items.e: 'First' starts a narrative sequence: First, ... Then, ... After that, ... Finally, ...
items.ed: 'First' beginnt eine Erzählabfolge: First, ... Then, ... After that, ... Finally, ...

items.p: First, we visited the museum. ___, we had lunch at a nice restaurant.
items.h: What comes after 'first' in a story?
items.hd: Was kommt nach 'first' in einer Erzählung?
items.e: 'Then' or 'After that' comes after 'First' to show the next event in order.
items.ed: 'Then' oder 'After that' kommt nach 'First', um das nauchste Ereignis zu zeigen.

items.p: We were walking in the forest. ___, a deer jumped out in front of us!
items.h: The deer was unexpected. Which word shows surprise?
items.hd: Das Reh war unerwartet. Welches Wort zeigt Überraschung?
items.e: 'Suddenly' marks an unexpected event in a narrative.
items.ed: 'Suddenly' markiert ein unerwartetes Ereignis in einer Erzählung.

items.p: I saw that film two days ___.
items.h: Which word means 'in the past' and goes AFTER a time expression?
items.hd: Welches Wort bedeutet 'in der Vergangenheit' und steht NACH einer Zeitangabe?
items.e: 'Ago' follows a time expression: two days ago, three weeks ago, five minutes ago.
items.ed: 'Ago' steht nach einer Zeitangabe: two days ago, three weeks ago, five minutes ago.

items.p: First, we packed our bags. Then, we drove to the airport. ___, we got on the plane. ___, we arrived in London!
items.h: Put the events in logical order: First → Then → ___ → ___
items.hd: Ordne die Ereignisse logisch: First → Then → ___ → ___
items.e: The sequence order is: First → Then → After that → Finally.
items.ed: Die Reihenfolge ist: First → Then → After that → Finally.

items.p: ___ week, we went on a school trip to Salzburg.
items.h: Which word goes before 'week' to mean 'the week before this one'?
items.hd: Welches Wort steht vor 'week' und bedeutet 'die Woche vor dieser'?
items.e: 'Last' + time period (last week, last Monday, last summer) refers to the most recent past period. No 'the' is needed.
items.ed: 'Last' + Zeitraum (last week, last Monday, last summer) bezieht sich auf den letzten vergangenen Zeitraum. Kein 'the' nötig.

items.p: Which sentence has the correct word order?
items.c: Then she ran to the door.
a: Then she ran to the door.
ds: Then ran she to the door.
Then to the door she ran.
She then to the door ran.
items.h: In English, the subject comes before the verb, even after time markers.
items.hd: Im Englischen kommt das Subjekt vor dem Verb, auch nach Zeitangaben.
items.e: In English, the word order after a time marker is: Time marker + subject + verb. Not like German V2 where the verb comes second.
items.ed: Im Englischen ist die Wortstellung nach einer Zeitangabe: Zeitmarker + Subjekt + Verb. Anders als die deutsche V2-Regel.

items.p: Which story is in the correct order?
items.c: First, I woke up. Then, I had breakfast. After that, I went to school. Finally, I came home.
a: First, I woke up. Then, I had breakfast. After that, I went to school. Finally, I came home.
ds: Finally, I woke up. Then, I had breakfast. First, I went to school. After that, I came home.
Then, I woke up. After that, I had breakfast. Finally, I went to school. First, I came home.
After that, I woke up. First, I had breakfast. Finally, I went to school. Then, I came home.
items.h: Think carefully about the grammar rule and choose the best answer.
items.hd: Überlege dir die Grammatikregel und wähle die beste Antwort.
items.e: In a narrative, the standard sequence is: First → Then → After that → Finally.
items.ed: In einer Erzählung ist die Standardreihenfolge: First → Then → After that → Finally.

items.p: Choose the correct sentence:
ds: Suddenly, the lights are going out.
items.h: The story is in the past. Check the verb tense and word order.
items.hd: Die Geschichte ist in der Vergangenheit. Überprüfe Zeitform und Wortstellung.
items.e: 'Suddenly' is followed by subject + past simple verb: Suddenly, the lights went out. Keep the past tense consistent.
items.ed: Nach 'Suddenly' kommt Subjekt + Verb im Simple Past: Suddenly, the lights went out. Die Vergangenheitsform bleibt gleich.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: Then / she / went / to / the / shop
items.c: Then she went to the shop.
a: Then she went to the shop.
Then she went to the shop
items.h: In English, the subject comes before the verb, even after a time word.
items.hd: Im Englischen kommt das Subjekt vor dem Verb, auch nach einem Zeitwort.
items.e: In English: Then + subject + verb. Not German V2 order (Dann ging sie...).
items.ed: Im Englischen: Then + Subjekt + Verb. Nicht wie die deutsche V2-Regel (Dann ging sie...).

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: First, we ate pizza. Then we play football in the park.
items.c: First, we ate pizza. Then we played football in the park.
a: First, we ate pizza. Then we played football in the park.
First, we ate pizza. Then we played football in the park
items.h: Both events happened in the past. Check if all verbs are in the past tense.
items.hd: Beide Ereignisse fanden in der Vergangenheit statt. Sind alle Verben in der Vergangenheit?
items.e: Both events are in the past, so use past simple for both: ate and played. Mixing tenses in a narrative is a common error.
items.ed: Beide Ereignisse sind in der Vergangenheit, also verwende Simple Past für beide: ate und played. Zeitenmischung in Erzählungen ist ein häufiger Fehler.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: After / that, / he / watched / a / film / at / home
items.c: After that, he watched a film at home.
a: After that, he watched a film at home.
After that, he watched a film at home
items.h: Check the word order after 'After that'. Where should the subject go?
items.hd: Überprüfe die Wortstellung nach 'After that'. Wo sollte das Subjekt stehen?
items.e: In English: After that + subject + verb. Not German V2 order: 'Danach sah er einen Film.'
items.ed: Im Englischen: After that + Subjekt + Verb. Nicht wie im Deutschen: 'Danach sah er einen Film.'

items.p: Put the events in order: 'We arrived at the hotel. We went to the beach.' → '___, we arrived at the hotel. ___, we went to the beach.'
items.c: First, we arrived at the hotel. Then, we went to the beach.
a: First, we arrived at the hotel. Then, we went to the beach.
items.h: Use 'First' for the first event and 'Then' for the next event.
items.hd: Verwende 'First' für das erste Ereignis und 'Then' für das nächste.
items.e: 'First' and 'Then' are time markers that show the order of events in a narrative.
items.ed: 'First' und 'Then' sind Zeitmarker, die die Reihenfolge der Ereignisse zeigen.

items.p: Write it in English: 'Dann spielte er mit seinen Freunden.' →
items.c: Then he played with his friends.
a: Then he played with his friends.
Then he played with his friends
items.h: In English, 'then' comes first, then subject + verb. Don't put the verb before the subject.
items.hd: Im Englischen kommt 'then' zuerst, dann Subjekt + Verb. Stelle das Verb nicht vor das Subjekt.
items.e: English word order after 'then': Then + subject + verb. Not like German where the verb comes second.
items.ed: Englische Wortstellung nach 'then': Then + Subjekt + Verb. Nicht wie im Deutschen, wo das Verb an zweiter Stelle steht.

items.p: You're writing a story about a zoo trip. Fix the mistake and connect: 'We went to the zoo. We see the elephants.' →
items.c: First, we went to the zoo. Then, we saw the elephants.
a: First, we went to the zoo. Then, we saw the elephants.
First we went to the zoo. Then we saw the elephants.
items.h: Check both verbs — are they in the same tense? And add time markers to connect the events.
items.hd: Überprüfe beide Verben — sind sie in derselben Zeitform? Und füge Zeitmarker hinzu.
items.e: 'See' must be 'saw' (past simple) to match 'went'. Both events are in the past.
items.ed: 'See' muss 'saw' (Simple Past) sein, passend zu 'went'. Beide Ereignisse sind in der Vergangenheit.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Zuerst gingen wir ins Museum. Dann aßen wir ein Eis.
items.c: First, we went to the museum. Then, we ate an ice cream.
a: First, we went to the museum. Then, we ate an ice cream.
First, we went to the museum. Then, we ate an ice cream
First we went to the museum. Then we ate an ice cream.
First, we went to the museum. Then we ate ice cream.
First, we went to the museum. Then, we had an ice cream.
First, we went to the museum. Then, we had ice cream.
items.h: 'Zuerst' = First, 'Dann' = Then.
items.hd: 'Zuerst' = First, 'Dann' = Then.
items.e: 'Zuerst' translates to 'First' and 'Dann' to 'Then'. Remember English word order: First, we went (not 'First went we').
items.ed: 'Zuerst' wird zu 'First' und 'Dann' zu 'Then'. Beachte die englische Wortstellung: First, we went (nicht 'First went we').

items.p: 🇩🇪 Plötzlich öffnete sich die Tür und ein Hund rannte herein.
items.c: Suddenly, the door opened and a dog ran in.
a: Suddenly, the door opened and a dog ran in.
Suddenly, the door opened and a dog ran in
Suddenly the door opened and a dog ran in.
Suddenly, the door opened and a dog ran inside.
Suddenly the door opened and a dog ran inside.
items.h: 'Plötzlich' = Suddenly. Remember English word order after the time marker.
items.hd: 'Plötzlich' = Suddenly. Beachte die englische Wortstellung nach dem Zeitmarker.
items.e: 'Plötzlich' = Suddenly. English word order: Suddenly, the door opened (subject before verb), not 'Suddenly opened the door'.
items.ed: 'Plötzlich' = Suddenly. Englische Wortstellung: Suddenly, the door opened (Subjekt vor Verb), nicht 'Suddenly opened the door'.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: played / Then / we / football / ,
items.h: Start with the time marker, then subject, then verb.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Zeitmarker, dann Subjekt, dann Verb.
items.e: English word order after a time marker: Then + subject + verb: Then, we played football.
items.ed: Englische Wortstellung nach einem Zeitmarker: Then + Subjekt + Verb: Then, we played football.

items.p:
Match each time marker with its function:
1. First
2. Then / After that
3. Finally
4. Suddenly
5. Yesterday
a. shows an unexpected event
b. marks the last event in a sequence
c. a specific past time (the day before today)
d. starts a sequence
e. shows the next event in order
items.h: Think about when you would use each word in a story.
items.hd: Überlege, wann du jedes Wort in einer Geschichte verwenden würdest.
items.e: First = starts a sequence, Then/After that = next event, Finally = last event, Suddenly = unexpected, Yesterday = specific past day.
items.ed: First = beginnt die Reihenfolge, Then/After that = nauchstes Ereignis, Finally = letztes Ereignis, Suddenly = unerwartet, Yesterday = gestern.

items.p: You're reading a classmate's diary about a school trip. Which sentence correctly uses past time markers?
items.c: First, we walked to the park. Then, we played football.
a: First, we walked to the park. Then, we played football.
ds: First, we walk to the park. Then, we play football.
First, we have walked to the park. Then, we have played football.
First, we walked to the park. Then, we play football.
items.h: A diary about a past trip needs past simple in all sentences. Check the verbs!
items.hd: Ein Tagebuch über einen vergangenen Ausflug braucht Simple Past in allen Sätzen. Überprüfe die Verben!
items.e: Past narratives use past simple consistently: walked, played. Time markers like 'First' and 'Then' show the order.
items.ed: Vergangene Erzählungen verwenden durchgängig Simple Past: walked, played. Zeitmarker wie 'First' und 'Then' zeigen die Reihenfolge.

items.p: There are ___ bananas in the fruit bowl.
items.h: Is this a positive, negative, or question sentence?
items.hd: Ist das ein positiver, negativer oder Fragesatz?
items.e: In positive sentences, use 'some': There are some bananas.
items.ed: In positiven Sätzen verwende 'some': There are some bananas.

items.p: We haven't got ___ milk in the fridge.
items.h: This is a negative sentence (haven't got). Which word do we use?
items.hd: Das ist ein verneinter Satz (haven't got). Welches Wort verwenden wir?
items.e: In negative sentences, use 'any': We haven't got any milk.
items.ed: In verneinten Sätzen verwende 'any': We haven't got any milk.

items.p: Are there ___ eggs in the cupboard?
items.h: This is a question. Which word do we normally use in questions?
items.hd: Das ist eine Frage. Welches Wort verwenden wir normalerweise in Fragen?
items.e: In questions, use 'any': Are there any eggs?
items.ed: In Fragen verwende 'any': Are there any eggs?

items.p: Would you like ___ cake?
items.h: This is an offer (Would you like...?). Offers are a special case!
items.hd: Das ist ein Angebot (Would you like...?). Angebote sind ein Sonderfall!
items.e: EXCEPTION: In offers and requests, use 'some' even though it's a question: Would you like some cake?
items.ed: AUSNAHME: Bei Angeboten und Bitten verwende 'some', obwohl es eine Frage ist: Would you like some cake?

items.p: There isn't ___ bread left, but there are ___ biscuits.
items.h: The first part is negative (isn't), the second part is positive (there are).
items.hd: Der erste Teil ist verneinend (isn't), der zweite Teil ist positiv (there are).
items.e: Negative = any (There isn't any bread). Positive = some (There are some biscuits).
items.ed: Verneinung = any (There isn't any bread). Positiv = some (There are some biscuits).

items.p: Can I have ___ water, please? — Sorry, there isn't ___ water left.
items.h: The first sentence is a polite request. The second is a negative statement.
items.hd: Der erste Satz ist eine höfliche Bitte. Der zweite ist eine Verneinung.
items.e: Polite requests use 'some' (Can I have some water?). Negative sentences use 'any' (There isn't any water).
items.ed: Höfliche Bitten verwenden 'some' (Can I have some water?). Verneinte Sätze verwenden 'any' (There isn't any water).

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: I haven't got any homework today.
a: I haven't got any homework today.
ds: I haven't got some homework today.
I don't have got any homework today.
I haven't got no homework today.
items.h: Think carefully about the grammar rule and choose the best answer.
items.hd: Überlege dir die Grammatikregel und wähle die beste Antwort.
items.e: Negative sentences use 'any': I haven't got any homework. Not 'some' or double negatives.
items.ed: Verneinte Sätze verwenden 'any': I haven't got any homework. Nicht 'some' oder doppelte Verneinung.

items.p: Choose the correct sentence for offering food:
ds: Would you like the some pizza?
items.h: When you offer something, do you use 'some' or 'any'?
items.hd: Wenn du etwas anbietest, verwendest du 'some' oder 'any'?
items.e: Offers use 'some' even though they are questions: Would you like some pizza?
items.ed: Angebote verwenden 'some', obwohl es Fragen sind: Would you like some pizza?

items.p: Choose the correct option: 'There aren't ___ tomatoes, but there is ___ cheese.'
items.h: First clause is negative (aren't). Second clause is positive (there is).
items.hd: Der erste Satzteil ist verneinend (aren't). Der zweite ist positiv (there is).
items.e: Negative → any (aren't any tomatoes). Positive → some (there is some cheese).
items.ed: Verneinung → any (aren't any tomatoes). Positiv → some (there is some cheese).

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I haven't got some money.
items.h: Look at the negative word 'haven't'. What goes with negatives?
items.hd: Schau auf das Verneinungswort 'haven't'. Was gehört zu Verneinungen?
items.e: In negative sentences, use 'any' not 'some': I haven't got any money.
items.ed: In verneinten Sätzen verwende 'any', nicht 'some': I haven't got any money.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Would you like any tea?
items.h: This is an offer. Do offers use 'some' or 'any'?
items.hd: Das ist ein Angebot. Verwenden Angebote 'some' oder 'any'?
items.e: Offers use 'some' even though they are questions: Would you like some tea?
items.ed: Angebote verwenden 'some', obwohl es Fragen sind: Would you like some tea?

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I have any good books at home.
items.c: I have some good books at home.
a: I have some good books at home.
I have some good books at home
I've got some good books at home.
items.h: This is a positive sentence. Which word goes in positive sentences?
items.hd: Das ist ein positiver Satz. Welches Wort gehört in positive Sätze?
items.e: In positive sentences, use 'some' not 'any': I have some good books.
items.ed: In positiven Sätzen verwende 'some', nicht 'any': I have some good books.

items.p: You open the bag and it's empty. Tell your mum: 'There ___ oranges in the bag.' (Make it negative)
a: There aren't any oranges in the bag.
items.h: In negative sentences, use 'any' (not 'some').
items.hd: In verneinten Sätzen verwende 'any' (nicht 'some').
items.e: Negative: There aren't any oranges. Use 'any' in negative sentences.
items.ed: Verneinung: There aren't any oranges. Verwende 'any' in verneinten Sätzen.

items.p: You want to know about the biscuits. Ask your sister: '___ there ___ biscuits in the tin?'
a: Are there any biscuits in the tin?
items.h: In questions, use 'any' (not 'some').
items.hd: In Fragen verwende 'any' (nicht 'some').
items.e: Questions: Are there any biscuits? Use 'any' in questions.
items.ed: Fragen: Are there any biscuits? Verwende 'any' in Fragen.

items.p: Your friend is thirsty. Offer them a drink: 'Would you like ___ juice?'
items.h: When making an offer, use 'some' (even though it's a question).
items.hd: Bei Angeboten verwende 'some' (obwohl es eine Frage ist).
items.e: Offers use 'some' even in questions: 'Would you like some juice?' We expect the answer 'yes'.
items.ed: Angebote verwenden 'some' auch in Fragen: 'Would you like some juice?' Wir erwarten die Antwort 'ja'.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Gibt es noch Äpfel? — Nein, es gibt keine Äpfel mehr.
items.c: Are there any apples? — No, there aren't any apples left.
a: Are there any apples? — No, there aren't any apples left.
Are there any apples? - No, there aren't any apples left.
Are there any apples left? — No, there aren't any apples left.
Are there any apples? No, there aren't any apples left.
Are there any apples left? No, there aren't any.
Are there any apples? — No, there aren't any apples.
Are there any apples left? — No, there aren't any.
items.h: Questions use 'any'. Negative answers also use 'any'. German 'keine' = not ... any.
items.hd: Fragen verwenden 'any'. Verneinte Antworten auch 'any'. Deutsch 'keine' = not ... any.
items.e: 'Gibt es' in questions = 'Are there any...?' German 'keine' = 'not any' in English.
items.ed: 'Gibt es' in Fragen = 'Are there any...?' Deutsch 'keine' = 'not any' im Englischen.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Möchtest du etwas Kuchen? — Ja, bitte! Ich hätte gerne ein Stück.
items.c: Would you like some cake? — Yes, please! I'd like a piece.
a: Would you like some cake? — Yes, please! I'd like a piece.
Would you like some cake? - Yes, please! I'd like a piece.
Would you like some cake? Yes, please! I'd like a piece.
Would you like some cake? — Yes please! I would like a piece.
Would you like some cake? — Yes, please! I would like a piece.
items.h: 'Möchtest du' in offers = 'Would you like'. Offers use 'some'.
items.hd: 'Möchtest du' bei Angeboten = 'Would you like'. Angebote verwenden 'some'.
items.e: Offers use 'some': Would you like some cake? Not 'any cake'.
items.ed: Angebote verwenden 'some': Would you like some cake? Nicht 'any cake'.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: any / got / haven't / I / pencils
items.h: Start with the subject, then the negative verb, then 'any' + noun.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Subjekt, dann das verneinte Verb, dann 'any' + Nomen.
items.e: Negative sentence with 'any': Subject + haven't got + any + noun: I haven't got any pencils.
items.ed: Verneinender Satz mit 'any': Subjekt + haven't got + any + Nomen: I haven't got any pencils.

items.p:
Match each sentence type with the correct word:
1. Positive sentence: I need ___ help.
2. Negative sentence: She hasn't got ___ friends here.
3. Normal question: Have you got ___ brothers?
4. Offer: Would you like ___ juice?
a. some
b. any
c. any
d. some
items.h: Positive and offers = some. Negative and normal questions = any.
items.hd: Positiv und Angebote = some. Verneinung und normale Fragen = any.
items.e: 1. Positive → some. 2. Negative → any. 3. Question → any. 4. Offer → some (exception!).
items.ed: 1. Positiv → some. 2. Verneinung → any. 3. Frage → any. 4. Angebot → some (Ausnahme!).

items.p: You want to know if there is milk. Ask about: milk in the fridge.
items.c: Is there any milk in the fridge?
a: Is there any milk in the fridge?
Is there any milk in the fridge
items.h: Use 'Is there any...?' for uncountable nouns in questions.
items.hd: Verwende 'Is there any...?' für nicht zählbare Nomen in Fragen.
items.e: For questions about quantities, use 'any': Is there any milk in the fridge?
items.ed: Für Fragen über Mengen verwende 'any': Is there any milk in the fridge?

items.p: I don't like this T-shirt. I want the blue ___.
items.h: You're talking about one T-shirt. Is it singular or plural?
items.hd: Du sprichst über ein T-Shirt. Ist es Einzahl oder Mehrzahl?
items.e: Use 'one' to replace a singular countable noun. 'The blue one' = 'the blue T-shirt'.
items.ed: Verwende 'one', um ein zählbares Nomen in der Einzahl zu ersetzen. 'The blue one' = 'the blue T-shirt'.

items.p: These shoes are too small. I need bigger ___.
items.h: Shoes is plural. Which form do you need — one or ones?
items.hd: Shoes ist Mehrzahl. Welche Form brauchst du — one oder ones?
items.e: Use 'ones' to replace a plural noun. 'Bigger ones' = 'bigger shoes'.
items.ed: Verwende 'ones', um ein Nomen in der Mehrzahl zu ersetzen. 'Bigger ones' = 'bigger shoes'.

items.p: Which pencil do you want? — The red ___.
items.h: In English, you can't just say 'the red' — you need a word after it.
items.hd: Im Englischen kannst du nicht einfach 'the red' sagen — du brauchst ein Wort danach.
items.e: In English, we need 'one' after an adjective: 'the red one'. In German, 'den roten' works alone, but in English it doesn't.
items.ed: Im Englischen brauchen wir 'one' nach einem Adjektiv: 'the red one'. Im Deutschen funktioniert 'den roten' allein, im Englischen nicht.

items.p: My old trainers are broken. I got new ___ for my birthday.
items.h: Trainers is plural. What replaces a plural noun?
items.hd: Trainers ist Mehrzahl. Was ersetzt ein Nomen in der Mehrzahl?
items.e: 'New ones' replaces 'new trainers'. Use 'ones' for plural noun substitution.
items.ed: 'New ones' ersetzt 'new trainers'. Verwende 'ones' für die Mehrzahl-Ersetzung.

items.p: There are two cakes left. Do you want the chocolate ___ or the vanilla ___?
items.h: Each cake is singular. Use the same word for both gaps.
items.hd: Jeder Kuchen ist Einzahl. Verwende dasselbe Wort für beide Lücken.
items.e: Both gaps refer to one cake each, so use 'one' both times: 'the chocolate one or the vanilla one'.
items.ed: Beide Lücken beziehen sich auf je einen Kuchen, also verwende beide Male 'one': 'the chocolate one or the vanilla one'.

items.p: I like these stickers, but the ___ on the table are even cooler.
items.h: What word replaces a plural noun when you describe which group you mean?
items.hd: Welches Wort ersetzt ein Nomen in der Mehrzahl, wenn du beschreibst, welche Gruppe du meinst?
items.e: 'The ones on the table' = 'the stickers on the table'. Use 'ones' to replace a plural noun.
items.ed: 'The ones on the table' = 'the stickers on the table'. Verwende 'ones' um ein Nomen in der Mehrzahl zu ersetzen.

items.p: Which bag is yours? Choose the correct sentence.
ds: The small bag one is mine.
items.h: You need a word after 'small' that replaces 'bag'.
items.hd: Du brauchst ein Wort nach 'small', das 'bag' ersetzt.
items.e: 'One' replaces the singular noun 'bag'. In English, you can't say 'the small' alone like in German.
items.ed: 'One' ersetzt das Nomen 'bag'. Im Englischen kann man nicht 'the small' allein sagen wie im Deutschen.

items.p: I lost my coloured pencils. Choose the correct answer.
items.h: Pencils is plural. Think about what word replaces a plural noun.
items.hd: Pencils ist Mehrzahl. Überlege, welches Wort ein Nomen in der Mehrzahl ersetzt.
items.e: 'My ones' replaces 'my coloured pencils'. Use 'ones' because pencils is plural.
items.ed: 'My ones' ersetzt 'my coloured pencils'. Verwende 'ones', weil pencils Mehrzahl ist.

items.p: We have two dogs. Which sentence is correct?
items.c: The big one is older than the small one.
a: The big one is older than the small one.
ds: The big is older than the small.
The big ones is older than the small ones.
The big one is older than the small.
items.h: Each gap refers to one single dog.
items.hd: Jede Lücke bezieht sich auf einen einzelnen Hund.
items.e: When comparing two singular items, use 'one' for each: 'the big one' and 'the small one'.
items.ed: Wenn du zwei einzelne Dinge vergleichst, verwende 'one' für jedes: 'the big one' und 'the small one'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I don't want the green ones. I want the red ones.
a: I don't want the green one. I want the red one.
I don't want the green one. I want the red one
items.h: Look at the context — is the speaker talking about one thing or many?
items.hd: Schau dir den Kontext an — spricht die Person über ein Ding oder mehrere?
items.e: Without more context, if this refers to a single item, 'ones' should be 'one'. The sentence needs singular 'one' if referring to one item.
items.ed: Wenn es um einen einzelnen Gegenstand geht, sollte 'ones' 'one' sein. Der Satz braucht 'one' in der Einzahl.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Which hat do you like? — I like the big.
items.h: In English, you can't end a sentence with just an adjective after 'the'.
items.hd: Im Englischen kann man einen Satz nicht einfach mit einem Adjektiv nach 'the' beenden.
items.e: In English, you need 'one' after the adjective: 'the big one'. German allows 'den großen' alone, but English doesn't.
items.ed: Im Englischen braucht man 'one' nach dem Adjektiv: 'the big one'. Deutsch erlaubt 'den großen' allein, Englisch nicht.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: These books are boring. The one on that shelf are much better.
a: The ones on that shelf are much better.
The ones on that shelf are much better
items.h: Look at the verb 'are'. Does it match 'one' or 'ones'?
items.hd: Schau dir das Verb 'are' an. Passt es zu 'one' oder 'ones'?
items.e: 'Are' is plural, so we need 'ones': 'The ones on that shelf are much better.' 'One' is singular and would need 'is'.
items.ed: 'Are' ist Mehrzahl, also brauchen wir 'ones': 'The ones on that shelf are much better.' 'One' ist Einzahl und bräuchte 'is'.

items.p: You're at the fruit market with your mum. She picks up two apples. You prefer the green one: 'I like the green ___, not the red ___.'
a: I like the green one, not the red one.
items.h: 'One' replaces a singular noun to avoid repeating it.
items.hd: 'One' ersetzt ein Substantiv im Singular, um Wiederholungen zu vermeiden.
items.e: 'One' replaces 'apple' (singular). I like the green one = the green apple.
items.ed: 'One' ersetzt 'apple' (Singular). I like the green one = den grünen Apfel.

items.p: You're getting ready for PE but your socks are wrong. Tell your friend: 'These socks are too small. I need long ___.'
items.h: 'Ones' replaces a plural noun. You need more than one sock!
items.hd: 'Ones' ersetzt ein Substantiv im Plural. Du brauchst mehr als eine Socke!
items.e: 'Ones' replaces 'socks' (plural). I need long ones = long socks.
items.ed: 'Ones' ersetzt 'socks' (Plural). I need long ones = lange Socken.

items.p: You need a new bike. Tell your dad: 'My old bike is too small. I want a new ___.'
items.h: 'One' replaces a singular noun (bike).
items.hd: 'One' ersetzt ein Substantiv im Singular (bike).
items.e: 'One' replaces 'bike' (singular). I want a new one = a new bike.
items.ed: 'One' ersetzt 'bike' (Singular). I want a new one = ein neues Fahrrad.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ich mag die blauen nicht. Ich mag die roten.
items.c: I don't like the blue ones. I like the red ones.
a: I don't like the blue ones. I like the red ones.
I don't like the blue ones. I like the red ones
I do not like the blue ones. I like the red ones.
I do not like the blue ones. I like the red ones
items.h: In English, you need a word after the colour adjective. Is it plural?
items.hd: Im Englischen brauchst du ein Wort nach dem Farbadjektiv. Ist es Mehrzahl?
items.e: German allows 'die blauen' alone, but English needs 'the blue ones'. Use 'ones' because it refers to plural items.
items.ed: Deutsch erlaubt 'die blauen' allein, aber Englisch braucht 'the blue ones'. Verwende 'ones', weil es sich auf Mehrzahl bezieht.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Welches Buch willst du? — Das dicke.
items.c: Which book do you want? — The thick one.
a: Which book do you want? — The thick one.
Which book do you want? The thick one.
Which book do you want? - The thick one.
Which book do you want? — The thick one
items.h: In English, you can't just say 'the thick' — you need a pronoun substitute.
items.hd: Im Englischen kannst du nicht einfach 'the thick' sagen — du brauchst ein Ersatzpronomen.
items.e: German 'das dicke' works without a noun, but English requires 'the thick one'.
items.ed: Im Deutschen funktioniert 'das dicke' ohne Nomen, aber im Englischen braucht man 'the thick one'.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: like / the / I / small / one
items.h: Start with the subject, then the verb, then what you like.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Subjekt, dann das Verb, dann was du magst.
items.e: The correct order is: Subject + verb + article + adjective + one: 'I like the small one.'
items.ed: Die richtige Reihenfolge ist: Subjekt + Verb + Artikel + Adjektiv + one: 'I like the small one.'

items.p: Match each sentence beginning with the correct ending. 1: I like this book but the other 2: These gloves are nice but the black 3: My phone is old. I want a new 4: Those cookies look good, especially the big
items.h: Check if each noun is singular or plural to decide between 'one' and 'ones'.
items.hd: Prüfe, ob jedes Nomen Einzahl oder Mehrzahl ist, um zwischen 'one' und 'ones' zu entscheiden.
items.e: 1→c (book = singular → one is better), 2→d (gloves = plural → ones are warmer), 3→a (phone = singular → one), 4→b (cookies = plural → ones).
items.ed: 1→c (book = Einzahl → one is better), 2→d (gloves = Mehrzahl → ones are warmer), 3→a (phone = Einzahl → one), 4→b (cookies = Mehrzahl → ones).

items.p: Form a question to ask which jacket someone wants. Use 'one' instead of repeating 'jacket'. Start with 'Which'.
items.h: 'Which' + substitute pronoun + question word order.
items.hd: 'Which' + Ersatzpronomen + Fragestellung.
items.e: 'Which one do you want?' uses 'one' to avoid repeating 'jacket'. The question follows do-support word order.
items.ed: 'Which one do you want?' verwendet 'one', um 'jacket' nicht zu wiederholen. Die Frage folgt der do-Umschreibung.

items.p: I like ___ football after school.
items.h: After 'like', add -ing to the verb.
items.hd: Nach 'like' hänge -ing an das Verb an.
items.e: 'Like' is followed by the -ing form: 'like playing'. Not the base form or past form.
items.ed: Nach 'like' kommt die -ing-Form: 'like playing'. Nicht die Grundform oder Vergangenheitsform.

items.p: She likes ___ books before bedtime.
items.h: After 'likes', what form does the verb take?
items.hd: Welche Form hat das Verb nach 'likes'?
items.e: 'Likes' is followed by the -ing form: 'likes reading'. The -s is only on 'like', not on the next verb.
items.ed: Nach 'likes' kommt die -ing-Form: 'likes reading'. Das -s steht nur bei 'like', nicht beim nauchsten Verb.

items.p: My brother likes ___ in the lake in summer.
items.h: After 'likes', add -ing. Watch the spelling carefully!
items.hd: Nach 'likes' hänge -ing an. Achte genau auf die Schreibung!
items.e: 'Swim' doubles the final consonant before -ing: 'swimming'. After 'likes', always use the -ing form.
items.ed: 'Swim' verdoppelt den Endkonsonanten vor -ing: 'swimming'. Nach 'likes' verwende immer die -ing-Form.

items.p: We don't like ___ up early on Saturdays.
items.h: After 'don't like', the next verb needs -ing. Double the last consonant!
items.hd: Nach 'don't like' braucht das nauchste Verb -ing. Verdopple den letzten Konsonanten!
items.e: 'Get' doubles the final consonant before -ing: 'getting'. The negative 'don't like' is still followed by -ing.
items.ed: 'Get' verdoppelt den Endkonsonanten vor -ing: 'getting'. Die Verneinung 'don't like' wird trotzdem mit -ing fortgesetzt.

items.p: Does your cat like ___ with a ball?
items.h: Even in questions, 'like' is followed by the -ing form.
items.hd: Auch in Fragen folgt nach 'like' die -ing-Form.
items.e: 'Does your cat like playing...?' — in questions, 'like' still takes -ing. 'Does' handles the question form.
items.ed: 'Does your cat like playing...?' — in Fragen folgt auf 'like' trotzdem -ing. 'Does' bildet die Frage.

items.p: Tom and Lisa like ___ videos and ___ them online.
items.h: Both verbs after 'like' need the same form.
items.hd: Beide Verben nach 'like' brauchen dieselbe Form.
items.e: When two verbs follow 'like', both take the -ing form: 'like making ... and sharing'.
items.ed: Wenn zwei Verben nach 'like' kommen, nehmen beide die -ing-Form: 'like making ... and sharing'.

items.p: Choose the correct sentence about Anna's hobby.
items.h: Think carefully about the grammar rule and choose the best answer.
items.hd: Überlege dir die Grammatikregel und wähle die beste Antwort.
items.e: 'Anna likes dancing.' — 3rd person -s on 'like', then the -ing form of the activity verb.
items.ed: 'Anna likes dancing.' — 3. Person -s bei 'like', dann die -ing-Form des Tätigkeitsverbs.

items.p: Choose the correct question.
items.h: In questions with 'do', the main verb stays in base form, but the verb after 'like' still needs -ing.
items.hd: In Fragen mit 'do' bleibt das Hauptverb in der Grundform, aber das Verb nach 'like' braucht trotzdem -ing.
items.e: 'Do you like cooking?' — 'Do' for the question, 'like' stays base form, and 'cook' becomes 'cooking'.
items.ed: 'Do you like cooking?' — 'Do' für die Frage, 'like' bleibt in der Grundform, und 'cook' wird zu 'cooking'.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: My dad doesn't like running in the rain.
a: My dad doesn't like running in the rain.
ds: My dad doesn't like run in the rain.
My dad doesn't likes running in the rain.
My dad not like running in the rain.
items.h: Think carefully about the grammar rule and choose the best answer.
items.hd: Überlege dir die Grammatikregel und wähle die beste Antwort.
items.e: 'Doesn't like running' — 'doesn't' handles the negative and 3rd person, 'like' is base form, 'run' becomes 'running'.
items.ed: 'Doesn't like running' — 'doesn't' übernimmt die Verneinung und 3. Person, 'like' ist Grundform, 'run' wird zu 'running'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I like swim in the sea.
a: I like swimming in the sea.
I like swimming in the sea
items.h: What form does the verb need after 'like'?
items.hd: Welche Form braucht das Verb nach 'like'?
items.e: 'Like' is followed by the -ing form: 'I like swimming in the sea.' 'Swim' needs double 'm' + -ing.
items.ed: Nach 'like' kommt die -ing-Form: 'I like swimming in the sea.' Bei 'swim' wird das 'm' vor -ing verdoppelt.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: He like playing computer games.
items.h: Look at the subject. Does the verb 'like' have the right form for 'he'?
items.hd: Schau dir das Subjekt an. Hat das Verb 'like' die richtige Form für 'he'?
items.e: 'He' needs 3rd person -s: 'He likes playing...' The -ing on 'playing' is correct.
items.ed: 'He' braucht die 3. Person -s: 'He likes playing...' Das -ing bei 'playing' ist richtig.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She likes to singing in the shower.
a: She likes singing in the shower.
She likes singing in the shower
items.h: Is it 'likes to sing' or 'likes singing'? You can't mix both patterns.
items.hd: Heißt es 'likes to sing' oder 'likes singing'? Man kann nicht beide Muster mischen.
items.e: Either 'likes singing' or 'likes to sing' — but NOT 'likes to singing'. Remove 'to' to fix it.
items.ed: Entweder 'likes singing' oder 'likes to sing' — aber NICHT 'likes to singing'. Entferne 'to', um es zu korrigieren.

items.p: Your pen pal asks about your hobbies. Make a sentence: 'I / like / draw pictures' →
items.h: After 'like', use the -ing form of the verb.
items.hd: Nach 'like' verwende die -ing-Form des Verbs.
items.e: Like + verb-ing: I like drawing (not 'I like draw').
items.ed: Like + Verb-ing: I like drawing (nicht 'I like draw').

items.p: Your friend says Tom watches too much TV. Make it negative: 'He ___ (not like / watch) TV.'
items.h: Negative: doesn't like + verb-ing.
items.hd: Verneinung: doesn't like + Verb-ing.
items.e: Negative present simple with like + -ing: He doesn't like watching TV.
items.ed: Verneinetes Present Simple mit like + -ing: He doesn't like watching TV.

items.p: You want to know about your classmates' hobbies. Ask them: '___ (they / like / ride) bikes?'
items.h: Question: Do/Does + subject + like + verb-ing?
items.hd: Frage: Do/Does + Subjekt + like + Verb-ing?
items.e: Question form: Do they like riding bikes? (Do + subject + like + -ing)
items.ed: Frageform: Do they like riding bikes? (Do + Subjekt + like + -ing)

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ich spiele gern Gitarre.
items.h: German 'gern + Verb' becomes 'like + verb-ing' in English.
items.hd: 'Gern + Verb' im Deutschen wird zu 'like + Verb-ing' im Englischen.
items.e: German uses 'gern' + verb, but English uses 'like' + -ing: 'I like playing guitar.'
items.ed: Im Deutschen verwendet man 'gern' + Verb, im Englischen 'like' + -ing: 'I like playing guitar.'

items.p: 🇩🇪 Meine Schwester kocht nicht gern.
a: My sister does not like cooking.
My sister does not like cooking
items.h: 'Nicht gern' = 'doesn't like'. Then add the -ing form.
items.hd: 'Nicht gern' = 'doesn't like'. Dann füge die -ing-Form hinzu.
items.e: German 'kocht nicht gern' = 'doesn't like cooking'. Use 'doesn't' for 3rd person negative + -ing.
items.ed: 'Kocht nicht gern' = 'doesn't like cooking'. Verwende 'doesn't' für 3. Person Verneinung + -ing.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: likes / she / riding / her / bike
items.h: Start with the subject, then 'likes', then the -ing verb and object.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Subjekt, dann 'likes', dann das -ing-Verb und das Objekt.
items.e: Subject + likes + -ing + object: 'She likes riding her bike.'
items.ed: Subjekt + likes + -ing + Objekt: 'She likes riding her bike.'

items.p: Match each subject with the correct ending. 1: I like 2: She likes 3: We don't like 4: Does he like 5: They like
items.h: Every ending uses the -ing form. Match the subject-verb agreement and sentence type.
items.hd: Jede Ergänzung verwendet die -ing-Form. Achte auf Subjekt-Verb-Übereinstimmung und Satzart.
items.e: All activities use the -ing form after 'like/likes'. 'Does he like' needs a question mark ending (b). The rest are statements.
items.ed: Alle Aktivitäten verwenden die -ing-Form nach 'like/likes'. 'Does he like' braucht eine Frage-Ergänzung (b). Der Rest sind Aussagesätze.

items.p: Your friend asks about your hobbies. Which sentence is correct?
items.c: I like playing basketball after school.
a: I like playing basketball after school.
ds: I like play basketball after school.
I am like playing basketball after school.
I like to playing basketball after school.
items.h: Think carefully about the grammar rule and choose the best answer.
items.hd: Überlege dir die Grammatikregel und wähle die beste Antwort.
items.e: 'Like + verb-ing' describes things you enjoy: I like playing basketball.
items.ed: 'Like + Verb-ing' beschreibt Dinge, die dir Spaß machen: I like playing basketball.

items.p: You ___ do your homework before you play outside.
