items.h: Which modal verb means you have a strong obligation?
items.hd: Welches Modalverb drückt eine starke Pflicht aus?
items.e: 'Must' expresses strong obligation. It is followed directly by the base form: 'must do'.
items.ed: 'Must' drückt eine starke Pflicht aus. Danach kommt direkt die Grundform: 'must do'.

items.p: You ___ run in the corridors. It's dangerous!
items.h: Running in corridors is forbidden. Which word means 'you are not allowed to'?
items.hd: Im Korridor laufen ist verboten. Welches Wort bedeutet 'du darfst nicht'?
items.e: 'Mustn't' = you are not allowed to (prohibition). Don't confuse it with 'don't have to' (= it's not necessary).
items.ed: 'Mustn't' = du darfst nicht (Verbot). Verwechsle es nicht mit 'don't have to' (= du musst nicht, es ist nicht nötig).

items.p: She ___ wear a helmet when she rides her bike.
items.h: Modal verbs never change form, even with he/she/it.
items.hd: Modalverben ändern ihre Form nie, auch nicht bei he/she/it.
items.e: 'Must' stays the same for all persons: 'She must wear'. No -s, no 'to'.
items.ed: 'Must' bleibt für alle Personen gleich: 'She must wear'. Kein -s, kein 'to'.

items.p: You ___ touch the animals at the zoo. It's against the rules.
items.h: It's against the rules = it's forbidden. Which word means 'you are not allowed'?
items.hd: Es ist gegen die Regeln = es ist verboten. Welches Wort bedeutet 'du darfst nicht'?
items.e: 'Mustn't' means it's forbidden. 'Don't have to' means it's not necessary — a very different meaning!
items.ed: 'Mustn't' bedeutet, es ist verboten. 'Don't have to' bedeutet, es ist nicht nötig — eine ganz andere Bedeutung!

items.p: Tomorrow is a holiday. We ___ go to school.
items.h: Is school forbidden or just not necessary tomorrow?
items.hd: Ist Schule verboten oder einfach nicht nötig morgen?
items.e: 'Don't have to' = it's not necessary (nicht müssen). 'Mustn't' = it's forbidden (nicht dürfen). Big difference!
items.ed: 'Don't have to' = es ist nicht nötig (nicht müssen). 'Mustn't' = es ist verboten (nicht dürfen). Großer Unterschied!

items.p: You ___ use your phone during the test. But you ___ bring a pencil — a pen is also fine.
items.h: Phone use is forbidden. The pencil is optional. Use different expressions!
items.hd: Handybenutzung ist verboten. Der Bleistift ist optional. Verwende verschiedene Ausdrücke!
items.e: 'Mustn't' = forbidden (phone during test). 'Don't have to' = not necessary (pencil is optional, pen works too).
items.ed: 'Mustn't' = verboten (Handy beim Test). 'Don't have to' = nicht nötig (Bleistift ist optional, Kugelschreiber geht auch).

items.p: The sign says 'No swimming'. Choose the correct sentence.
ds: You don't have to swim here.
items.h: 'No swimming' = it's forbidden. Which sentence says that?
items.hd: 'No swimming' = es ist verboten. Welcher Satz drückt das aus?
items.e: 'Mustn't' expresses prohibition: 'You mustn't swim here.' 'Don't have to' would mean it's optional.
items.ed: 'Mustn't' drückt ein Verbot aus: 'You mustn't swim here.' 'Don't have to' würde bedeuten, es ist freiwillig.

items.p: It's Saturday. Choose the correct sentence about school.
items.c: We don't have to go to school today.
a: We don't have to go to school today.
ds: We mustn't go to school today.
We must go to school today.
We don't must go to school today.
items.h: Is school forbidden on Saturday, or just not necessary?
items.hd: Ist Schule am Samstag verboten, oder einfach nicht nötig?
items.e: No school on Saturday = it's not necessary → 'don't have to'. 'Mustn't' would mean going to school is forbidden!
items.ed: Keine Schule am Samstag = es ist nicht nötig → 'don't have to'. 'Mustn't' würde bedeuten, zur Schule gehen ist verboten!

items.p: In the library: Choose the correct sentence.
items.c: You mustn't talk loudly, but you don't have to whisper.
a: You mustn't talk loudly, but you don't have to whisper.
ds: You don't have to talk loudly, but you mustn't whisper.
You mustn't talk loudly, but you mustn't whisper.
You don't must talk loudly, but you don't must whisper.
items.h: Talking loudly is forbidden. Whispering is allowed but not required.
items.hd: Laut sprechen ist verboten. Flüstern ist erlaubt, aber nicht nötig.
items.e: 'Mustn't' = forbidden (loud talking). 'Don't have to' = not necessary (whispering is optional, quiet voice is enough).
items.ed: 'Mustn't' = verboten (laut sprechen). 'Don't have to' = nicht nötig (flüstern ist freiwillig, leise reden reicht).

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: You must to wear a seatbelt in the car.
a: You must wear a seatbelt in the car.
You must wear a seatbelt in the car
items.h: Does 'must' need 'to' before the next verb?
items.hd: Braucht 'must' ein 'to' vor dem nauchsten Verb?
items.e: 'Must' is followed directly by the base form — no 'to': 'You must wear', not 'must to wear'.
items.ed: Nach 'must' kommt direkt die Grundform — kein 'to': 'You must wear', nicht 'must to wear'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: You don't must eat in the classroom.
a: You mustn't eat in the classroom.
You mustn't eat in the classroom
You must not eat in the classroom.
You must not eat in the classroom
items.h: How do you negate 'must'? Can you use 'don't' with a modal verb?
items.hd: Wie verneint man 'must'? Kann man 'don't' mit einem Modalverb verwenden?
items.e: Modal verbs are negated directly: 'must not' / 'mustn't'. You cannot use 'don't' + 'must'.
items.ed: Modalverben werden direkt verneint: 'must not' / 'mustn't'. Man kann nicht 'don't' + 'must' verwenden.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: It's a holiday. We mustn't go to school.
a: We don't have to go to school.
We don't have to go to school
We do not have to go to school.
We do not have to go to school
items.h: Is going to school on a holiday forbidden or just not necessary?
items.hd: Ist der Schulbesuch am Feiertag verboten oder einfach nicht nötig?
items.e: On a holiday, school is not necessary (not forbidden). Use 'don't have to' (nicht müssen), not 'mustn't' (nicht dürfen).
items.ed: Am Feiertag ist Schule nicht nötig (nicht verboten). Verwende 'don't have to' (nicht müssen), nicht 'mustn't' (nicht dürfen).

items.p: Your little sister forgot to brush her teeth. Tell her: 'You ___ (brush) your teeth before bed!'
a: You must brush your teeth before bed!
items.h: 'Must' + base form (no 'to'). It's a strong rule!
items.hd: 'Must' + Grundform (kein 'to'). Es ist eine strenge Regel!
items.e: 'Must' expresses strong obligation. You must brush your teeth = it's very important.
items.ed: 'Must' drückt eine starke Verpflichtung aus. You must brush your teeth = es ist sehr wichtig.

items.p: You see a 'NO PHONES' sign at school. Tell the new student: 'You ___ (use) phones in class.'
a: You mustn't use phones in class.
items.h: 'Mustn't' means it's forbidden. The sign says 'NO'!
items.hd: 'Mustn't' bedeutet, es ist verboten. Das Schild sagt 'NO'!
items.e: 'Mustn't' = it's forbidden/not allowed. You mustn't use phones in class.
items.ed: 'Mustn't' = es ist verboten/nicht erlaubt. You mustn't use phones in class.

items.p: Your friend is packing too much food for the trip. Tell him it's not necessary: 'You ___ (bring) food. The school provides lunch.'
a: You don't have to bring food.
items.h: It's not necessary (but not forbidden). Which expression means 'not necessary'?
items.hd: Es ist nicht notwendig (aber nicht verboten). Welcher Ausdruck bedeutet 'nicht notwendig'?
items.e: 'Don't have to' means it's not necessary. 'Mustn't' means forbidden — different meaning!
items.ed: 'Don't have to' bedeutet, es ist nicht notwendig. 'Mustn't' bedeutet verboten — andere Bedeutung!

items.p: 🇩🇪 Du musst leise sein.
items.h: 'Musst' = strong obligation. Which English modal matches?
items.hd: 'Musst' = starke Pflicht. Welches englische Modalverb passt?
items.e: German 'musst' = English 'must' for obligation: 'You must be quiet.'
items.ed: Deutsches 'musst' = englisches 'must' für Pflicht: 'You must be quiet.'

items.p: 🇩🇪 Du musst nicht warten. (= Es ist nicht nötig.)
a: You do not have to wait.
You do not have to wait
items.h: Careful! 'Musst nicht' does NOT translate to 'mustn't'. Think about the meaning.
items.hd: Vorsicht! 'Musst nicht' ist NICHT 'mustn't'. Denke an die Bedeutung.
items.e: German 'musst nicht' = 'don't have to' (not necessary). NOT 'mustn't' (which means forbidden / 'darfst nicht')!
items.ed: Deutsches 'musst nicht' = 'don't have to' (nicht nötig). NICHT 'mustn't' (das bedeutet verboten / 'darfst nicht')!

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: mustn't / the / you / feed / animals
items.h: Start with the subject, then the modal + not, then the verb and object.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Subjekt, dann Modal + not, dann Verb und Objekt.
items.e: Subject + mustn't + base form + object: 'You mustn't feed the animals.'
items.ed: Subjekt + mustn't + Grundform + Objekt: 'You mustn't feed the animals.'

items.p: Match each situation with the correct rule. 1: There's a test tomorrow. 2: It's Saturday, no school. 3: The sign says 'No photos'. 4: You can wear trainers or school shoes. 5: The teacher is talking.
ds: a: You don't have to get up early.
b: You don't have to wear school shoes.
items.h: Decide: is each situation about obligation (must), prohibition (mustn't), or no necessity (don't have to)?
items.hd: Entscheide: Geht es um Pflicht (must), Verbot (mustn't) oder keine Notwendigkeit (don't have to)?
items.e: 1→d (test = must study), 2→a (no school = don't have to get up early), 3→c (no photos = mustn't), 4→b (choice = don't have to), 5→e (teacher talking = mustn't talk).
items.ed: 1→d (Test = must study), 2→a (keine Schule = don't have to), 3→c (keine Fotos = mustn't), 4→b (Wahl = don't have to), 5→e (Lehrer spricht = mustn't talk).

items.p: You see this sign at the swimming pool: 'NO RUNNING.' What does it mean?
items.c: You mustn't run at the swimming pool.
a: You mustn't run at the swimming pool.
ds: You don't have to run at the swimming pool.
You shouldn't run at the swimming pool.
You must run at the swimming pool.
items.h: 'NO RUNNING' is a rule. Which modal expresses that something is forbidden?
items.hd: 'NO RUNNING' ist eine Regel. Welches Modalverb drückt aus, dass etwas verboten ist?
items.e: 'Mustn't' means forbidden/not allowed. A 'NO RUNNING' sign = you mustn't run.
items.ed: 'Mustn't' bedeutet verboten/nicht erlaubt. Ein 'NO RUNNING'-Schild = you mustn't run.

items.p: This is ___ bag. (Tom)
items.h: To show that Tom owns the bag, add something to his name.
items.hd: Um zu zeigen, dass die Tasche Tom gehört, füge etwas an seinen Namen an.
items.e: Singular possessive: add 's (apostrophe + s) to the owner's name: Tom's bag.
items.ed: Besitz in der Einzahl: füge 's (Apostroph + s) an den Namen des Besitzers: Tom's bag.

items.p: My ___ car is blue. (mum)
items.h: Add the possessive marker to 'mum' to show it's her car.
items.hd: Füge die Besitzform an 'mum' an, um zu zeigen, dass es ihr Auto ist.
items.e: Singular possessive: mum's (apostrophe + s). One mum, one car = 's.
items.ed: Besitz in der Einzahl: mum's (Apostroph + s). Eine Mum, ein Auto = 's.

items.p: The ___ classroom is on the first floor. (girls)
items.h: More than one girl. Where does the apostrophe go for a regular plural?
items.hd: Mehr als ein Mädchen. Wo kommt der Apostroph bei einer regelmäßigen Mehrzahl hin?
items.e: For regular plurals ending in -s, add only an apostrophe: girls' classroom (= the classroom of the girls).
items.ed: Bei regelmäßiger Mehrzahl auf -s wird nur ein Apostroph angehängt: girls' classroom (= das Klassenzimmer der Mädchen).

items.p: The ___ toys are all over the floor. (children)
items.h: 'Children' is already plural but doesn't end in -s. What do you add?
items.hd: 'Children' ist schon Mehrzahl, endet aber nicht auf -s. Was fügst du an?
items.e: Irregular plurals (children, men, women) take 's — just like singular nouns: children's toys.
items.ed: Unregelmäßige Mehrzahlformen (children, men, women) bekommen 's — genau wie Einzahlnomen: children's toys.

items.p: Both ___ bikes are in the garage. (my parents)
items.h: 'Both' tells you it's plural. Regular plurals ending in -s just get an apostrophe.
items.hd: 'Both' zeigt, dass es Mehrzahl ist. Regelmäßige Mehrzahl auf -s bekommt nur einen Apostroph.
items.e: 'Parents' is a regular plural ending in -s, so add just the apostrophe: my parents' bikes.
items.ed: 'Parents' ist eine regelmäßige Mehrzahl auf -s, also wird nur der Apostroph angehängt: my parents' bikes.

items.p: The ___ office is on the second floor. (women)
items.h: 'Women' is an irregular plural — it doesn't end in -s. How do you form the possessive?
items.hd: 'Women' ist eine unregelmäßige Mehrzahl — sie endet nicht auf -s. Wie bildest du die Besitzform?
items.e: 'Women' is an irregular plural (like 'children', 'men'), so it takes 's: women's office.
items.ed: 'Women' ist eine unregelmäßige Mehrzahl (wie 'children', 'men'), also bekommt es 's: women's office.

items.p: Three brothers share a room. Choose the correct sentence.
items.c: The brothers' room is very big.
a: The brothers' room is very big.
ds: The brother's room is very big.
The brothers room is very big.
The brothers's room is very big.
items.h: Three brothers = plural. The plural of 'brother' already ends in -s.
items.hd: Drei Brüder = Mehrzahl. Die Mehrzahl von 'brother' endet schon auf -s.
items.e: Regular plural possessive: brothers' (apostrophe after the -s). Three brothers share the room.
items.ed: Regelmäßige Mehrzahl-Besitzform: brothers' (Apostroph nach dem -s). Drei Brüder teilen sich das Zimmer.

items.p: The people have a new park. Choose the correct sentence.
items.c: The people's new park is beautiful.
a: The people's new park is beautiful.
ds: The peoples' new park is beautiful.
The peoples new park is beautiful.
The people new park is beautiful.
items.h: 'People' is already plural but doesn't end in -s. It's like 'children'.
items.hd: 'People' ist schon Mehrzahl, endet aber nicht auf -s. Es ist wie 'children'.
items.e: 'People' is an irregular plural (like children, men). Add 's: people's park.
items.ed: 'People' ist eine unregelmäßige Mehrzahl (wie children, men). Füge 's an: people's park.

items.p: James has a dog. Which sentence is correct?
items.h: James is one person. Singular names ending in -s still usually get 's.
items.hd: James ist eine Person. Einzelne Namen auf -s bekommen trotzdem meist 's.
items.e: For singular names ending in -s, add 's: James's dog. This is the standard form taught at A2.
items.ed: Bei einzelnen Namen auf -s wird 's angehängt: James's dog. Das ist die Standardform, die auf A2-Niveau unterrichtet wird.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Lisas cat is black and white.
a: Lisa's cat is black and white.
Lisa's cat is black and white
items.h: How do you show that the cat belongs to Lisa?
items.hd: Wie zeigst du, dass die Katze Lisa gehört?
items.e: Singular possessive needs an apostrophe + s: Lisa's cat. German doesn't use an apostrophe, but English does.
items.ed: Die Einzahl-Besitzform braucht einen Apostroph + s: Lisa's cat. Deutsch verwendet keinen Apostroph, Englisch schon.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: The student's lockers are in the corridor. (many students)
a: The students' lockers are in the corridor.
The students' lockers are in the corridor
items.h: Many students — so it's a plural possessive. Where does the apostrophe go?
items.hd: Viele Schüler — also eine Mehrzahl-Besitzform. Wo kommt der Apostroph hin?
items.e: For plural nouns ending in -s, put the apostrophe after the -s: students' lockers (not student's).
items.ed: Bei Mehrzahlnomen auf -s kommt der Apostroph nach dem -s: students' lockers (nicht student's).

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: The mens' changing room is on the left.
a: The men's changing room is on the left.
The men's changing room is on the left
items.h: 'Men' is already plural. It doesn't end in -s. So the apostrophe goes...
items.hd: 'Men' ist schon Mehrzahl. Es endet nicht auf -s. Also kommt der Apostroph...
items.e: 'Men' is an irregular plural — it doesn't end in -s, so add 's: men's (not mens').
items.ed: 'Men' ist eine unregelmäßige Mehrzahl — endet nicht auf -s, also wird 's angehängt: men's (nicht mens').

items.p: You're introducing your family at school. Describe the bike: 'the bike of my sister' → 'my ___'
items.h: Use 's after the owner: my sister's bike (not 'the bike of my sister').
items.hd: Verwende 's nach dem Besitzer: my sister's bike (nicht 'the bike of my sister').
items.e: Possessive 's: owner + 's + thing. My sister's bike = the bike that belongs to my sister.
items.ed: Possessiv-'s: Besitzer + 's + Sache. My sister's bike = das Fahrrad meiner Schwester.

items.p: You're labelling things in the classroom. The boys share a room: 'the room of the boys' → 'the ___'
items.h: For plural nouns ending in -s, just add an apostrophe: boys' (not boys's).
items.hd: Bei Pluralnomen auf -s füge nur einen Apostroph hinzu: boys' (nicht boys's).
items.e: Plural possessive: boys' room. The apostrophe goes after the -s for regular plurals.
items.ed: Plural-Possessiv: boys' room. Der Apostroph steht nach dem -s bei regelmäßigem Plural.

items.p: You're describing the school library. The children have special books: 'the books of the children' → 'the ___'
items.h: Irregular plural (children) doesn't end in -s, so add 's: children's.
items.hd: Unregelmäßiger Plural (children) endet nicht auf -s, also füge 's hinzu: children's.
items.e: Irregular plural possessive: children's books. Add 's because 'children' doesn't already end in -s.
items.ed: Unregelmäßiger Plural-Possessiv: children's books. Füge 's hinzu, da 'children' nicht auf -s endet.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Annas Bruder spielt Fußball.
items.h: German 'Annas' has no apostrophe, but English needs one!
items.hd: Deutsch 'Annas' hat keinen Apostroph, aber Englisch braucht einen!
items.e: German: 'Annas Bruder' (no apostrophe). English: 'Anna's brother' (apostrophe + s required).
items.ed: Deutsch: 'Annas Bruder' (kein Apostroph). Englisch: 'Anna's brother' (Apostroph + s nötig).

items.p: 🇩🇪 Das Spielzeug der Kinder liegt überall herum.
a: The children's toys are all over the place.
The children's toys are all over the place
items.h: 'Kinder' = 'children' — an irregular plural. How do you form the possessive?
items.hd: 'Kinder' = 'children' — eine unregelmäßige Mehrzahl. Wie bildest du die Besitzform?
items.e: 'Children' is irregular plural → children's toys (apostrophe + s, like singular nouns).
items.ed: 'Children' ist unregelmäßige Mehrzahl → children's toys (Apostroph + s, wie bei Einzahlnomen).

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: is / my / phone / where / dad's / ?
items.h: Start with the question word, then the verb, then the possessive phrase.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Fragewort, dann das Verb, dann die Besitzform.
items.e: Question word + verb + possessive phrase: 'Where is my dad's phone?'
items.ed: Fragewort + Verb + Besitzform: 'Where is my dad's phone?'

items.p: Match each owner with the correct possessive form. 1: the teacher (singular) 2: the teachers (plural) 3: the children 4: my friend 5: the men
items.h: Singular and irregular plurals get 's. Regular plurals ending in -s get just an apostrophe.
items.hd: Einzahl und unregelmäßige Mehrzahl bekommen 's. Regelmäßige Mehrzahl auf -s bekommt nur einen Apostroph.
items.e: 1→c (singular: teacher's), 2→a (regular plural: teachers'), 3→e (irregular plural: children's), 4→d (singular: friend's), 5→b (irregular plural: men's).
items.ed: 1→c (Einzahl: teacher's), 2→a (regelmäßige Mehrzahl: teachers'), 3→e (unregelm. Mehrzahl: children's), 4→d (Einzahl: friend's), 5→b (unregelm. Mehrzahl: men's).

items.p: Form a question using 'whose': the book / it / whose / is → ...
items.h: Start with 'Whose' + the thing, then the verb.
items.hd: Beginne mit 'Whose' + das Ding, dann das Verb.
items.e: 'Whose' asks about possession: 'Whose book is it?' = 'Wessen Buch ist das?'
items.ed: 'Whose' fragt nach dem Besitz: 'Whose book is it?' = 'Wessen Buch ist das?'

items.p: Is this your pen? — Yes, it's ___.
items.h: You need a word that means 'my pen' but stands alone.
items.hd: Du brauchst ein Wort, das 'mein Stift' bedeutet, aber allein steht.
items.e: After 'it's', we need a possessive pronoun that replaces 'my pen'. The possessive pronoun for 'my' is 'mine'.
items.ed: Nach 'it's' brauchen wir ein besitzanzeigendes Fürwort, das 'my pen' ersetzt. Das besitzanzeigende Fürwort für 'my' ist 'mine'.

items.p: That's not my bag. It's ___.
items.h: You need a word that means 'your bag' without repeating the noun.
items.hd: Du brauchst ein Wort, das 'deine Tasche' bedeutet, ohne das Nomen zu wiederholen.
items.e: 'Yours' replaces 'your bag'. Possessive pronouns stand alone — no noun after them, no apostrophe.
items.ed: 'Yours' ersetzt 'your bag'. Besitzanzeigende Fürwörter stehen allein — kein Nomen danach, kein Apostroph.

items.p: My phone is broken. Can I use ___?
items.h: Which word means 'your phone' without saying 'phone'?
items.hd: Welches Wort bedeutet 'dein Telefon', ohne 'Telefon' zu sagen?
items.e: 'Yours' means 'your phone' — it replaces the noun. 'Your' always needs a noun after it.
items.ed: 'Yours' bedeutet 'dein Telefon' — es ersetzt das Nomen. 'Your' braucht immer ein Nomen danach.

items.p: We forgot our football. Can we borrow ___?
items.h: Which word means 'their football' without repeating 'football'?
items.hd: Welches Wort bedeutet 'ihren Fußball', ohne 'Fußball' zu wiederholen?
items.e: 'Theirs' replaces 'their football'. No apostrophe — possessive pronouns never have one.
items.ed: 'Theirs' ersetzt 'their football'. Kein Apostroph — besitzanzeigende Fürwörter haben nie eines.

items.p: That bike isn't his. It's ___.
items.h: Which word means 'her bike' and stands alone?
items.hd: Welches Wort bedeutet 'ihr Fahrrad' und steht allein?
items.e: 'Hers' means 'her bike'. No apostrophe needed — possessive pronouns never take one.
items.ed: 'Hers' bedeutet 'ihr Fahrrad'. Kein Apostroph nötig — besitzanzeigende Fürwörter bekommen nie eines.

items.p: I like your painting, but I think ___ is better.
items.h: Which word means 'our painting' without saying the noun?
items.hd: Welches Wort bedeutet 'unser Bild', ohne das Nomen zu sagen?
items.e: 'Ours' replaces 'our painting'. Possessive pronouns replace the adjective + noun combination.
items.ed: 'Ours' ersetzt 'our painting'. Besitzanzeigende Fürwörter ersetzen die Kombination aus Adjektiv + Nomen.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.h: Which word stands alone after 'is' to show ownership?
items.hd: Welches Wort steht nach 'is' allein, um Besitz zu zeigen?
items.e: 'Mine' is a possessive pronoun — it replaces 'my book'. You cannot say 'my' alone or 'mine book'.
items.ed: 'Mine' ist ein besitzanzeigendes Fürwort — es ersetzt 'my book'. Man kann nicht 'my' allein oder 'mine book' sagen.

items.p: Choose the correct option:
items.c: Is that pencil case yours or hers?
a: Is that pencil case yours or hers?
ds: Is that pencil case your or her?
Is that pencil case yours or her's?
Is that pencil case your's or hers?
items.h: When no noun follows, you need the pronoun forms.
items.hd: Wenn kein Nomen folgt, brauchst du die Fürwort-Formen.
items.e: Both 'yours' and 'hers' are possessive pronouns — they stand alone without a noun after them. No apostrophe needed.
items.ed: Sowohl 'yours' als auch 'hers' sind besitzanzeigende Fürwörter — sie stehen allein ohne Nomen danach. Kein Apostroph nötig.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: This skateboard is her's.
items.h: Do possessive pronouns need an apostrophe?
items.hd: Brauchen besitzanzeigende Fürwörter ein Apostroph?
items.e: Possessive pronouns never have an apostrophe: hers, yours, ours, theirs — not her's, your's, our's, their's.
items.ed: Besitzanzeigende Fürwörter haben nie ein Apostroph: hers, yours, ours, theirs — nicht her's, your's, our's, their's.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Is this mine jacket?
items.h: Can you put 'mine' before a noun?
items.hd: Kann man 'mine' vor ein Nomen stellen?
items.e: 'Mine' is a possessive pronoun — it stands alone. Before a noun, use 'my': my jacket (not mine jacket).
items.ed: 'Mine' ist ein besitzanzeigendes Fürwort — es steht allein. Vor einem Nomen verwendet man 'my': my jacket (nicht mine jacket).

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: That tablet is my, not your.
items.c: That tablet is mine, not yours.
a: That tablet is mine, not yours.
That tablet is mine, not yours
items.h: After 'is', can you use 'my' and 'your' without a noun?
items.hd: Kann man nach 'is' die Wörter 'my' und 'your' ohne Nomen verwenden?
items.e: 'My' and 'your' need a noun after them. Alone after 'is', use the pronouns: mine, yours.
items.ed: 'My' und 'your' brauchen ein Nomen danach. Allein nach 'is' verwendet man die Fürwörter: mine, yours.

items.p: Someone found a book in the classroom. It's your book. Say: 'This book is ___.'
items.h: Which possessive pronoun replaces 'my book'? My → mine.
items.hd: Welches besitzanzeigende Pronomen ersetzt 'my book'? My → mine.
items.e: Possessive pronoun: my → mine. 'This book is mine' = 'This is my book.'
items.ed: Possessivpronomen: my → mine. 'This book is mine' = 'This is my book.'

items.p: Your friends left their sandwiches on the table. Point to them: 'Those sandwiches are ___.'
items.h: Their → theirs. No apostrophe needed!
items.hd: Their → theirs. Kein Apostroph nötig!
items.e: Possessive pronoun: their → theirs. 'Those sandwiches are theirs' = 'those are their sandwiches.'
items.ed: Possessivpronomen: their → theirs. 'Those sandwiches are theirs' = 'das sind ihre Sandwiches.'

items.p: Tom and Emma both have guitars. You want to know whose this one is. Ask: 'Is this his or ___?'
items.h: Her → hers. The possessive pronoun stands alone (no noun after it).
items.hd: Her → hers. Das Possessivpronomen steht allein (kein Nomen danach).
items.e: Possessive pronoun: her → hers. Is this his or hers? = his guitar or her guitar?
items.ed: Possessivpronomen: her → hers. Is this his or hers? = seine Gitarre oder ihre Gitarre?

items.p: 🇩🇪 Dieses Fahrrad gehört mir.
items.h: Use a possessive pronoun after 'is'.
items.hd: Verwende ein besitzanzeigendes Fürwort nach 'is'.
items.e: 'Gehört mir' → 'is mine'. In English we say 'This bike is mine', not 'This bike belongs me'.
items.ed: 'Gehört mir' → 'is mine'. Im Englischen sagt man 'This bike is mine', nicht 'This bike belongs me'.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ist das dein Rucksack oder ihrer?
items.c: Is that your backpack or hers?
a: Is that your backpack or hers?
Is that your backpack or hers
Is this your backpack or hers?
Is this your backpack or hers
Is that your rucksack or hers?
Is that your rucksack or hers
items.h: Use 'your' before the noun and a pronoun after 'or'.
items.hd: Verwende 'your' vor dem Nomen und ein Fürwort nach 'or'.
items.e: 'Your' + noun (your backpack), but 'hers' alone (no noun after). German 'ihrer' = English 'hers'.
items.ed: 'Your' + Nomen (your backpack), aber 'hers' allein (kein Nomen danach). Deutsch 'ihrer' = Englisch 'hers'.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: yours / is / or / this / mine / ?
items.h: Start with the verb for a yes/no question.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Verb für eine Ja/Nein-Frage.
items.e: Yes/no questions: Is + subject + rest. Both 'yours or mine' and 'mine or yours' are correct.
items.ed: Ja/Nein-Fragen: Is + Subjekt + Rest. Sowohl 'yours or mine' als auch 'mine or yours' sind richtig.

items.p:
Match the possessive adjective to its possessive pronoun:
1. my
2. your
3. his
4. her
5. our
6. their

a. theirs
b. mine
c. hers
d. his
e. yours
f. ours
items.h: One pair has the same form for both adjective and pronoun.
items.hd: Ein Paar hat dieselbe Form für Adjektiv und Fürwort.
items.e: my→mine, your→yours, his→his, her→hers, our→ours, their→theirs. Note: 'his' stays the same!
items.ed: my→mine, your→yours, his→his, her→hers, our→ours, their→theirs. Beachte: 'his' bleibt gleich!

items.p: This is Lisa's pencil case. → Ask about ownership. Start with 'Whose'.
items.h: Whose + noun + is + this?
items.hd: Whose + Nomen + is + this?
items.e: To ask about ownership: Whose + noun + is this? or Whose is this + noun?
items.ed: Um nach Besitz zu fragen: Whose + Nomen + is this? oder Whose is this + Nomen?

items.p: ___ is your best friend?
items.h: You are asking about a person, not about possession.
items.hd: Du fragst nach einer Person, nicht nach Besitz.
items.e: 'Who' asks about a person: Who is your best friend? 'Whose' asks about possession.
items.ed: 'Who' fragt nach einer Person: Who is your best friend? 'Whose' fragt nach Besitz.

items.p: ___ bag is on the floor?
items.h: You are asking who the bag belongs to.
items.hd: Du fragst, wem die Tasche gehört.
items.e: 'Whose' asks about possession: Whose bag? = Who does the bag belong to?
items.ed: 'Whose' fragt nach Besitz: Whose bag? = Wem gehört die Tasche?

items.p: ___ plays the guitar in your class?
items.h: 'Who' is the subject here — does it need an extra auxiliary?
items.hd: 'Who' ist hier das Subjekt — braucht es ein zusätzliches Hilfsverb?
items.e: When 'who' is the subject, there is NO auxiliary: Who plays? (not Who does play?)
items.ed: Wenn 'who' das Subjekt ist, gibt es KEIN Hilfsverb: Who plays? (nicht Who does play?)

items.p: ___ phone keeps ringing?
items.h: The question asks about the owner of the phone.
items.hd: Die Frage fragt nach dem Besitzer des Telefons.
items.e: 'Whose' + noun asks about ownership: Whose phone? Don't confuse with 'who's' (= who is/has).
items.ed: 'Whose' + Nomen fragt nach Besitz: Whose phone? Nicht verwechseln mit 'who's' (= who is/has).

items.p: ___ won the race yesterday?
items.h: 'Who' is the subject — do you need an auxiliary for past tense?
items.hd: 'Who' ist das Subjekt — brauchst du ein Hilfsverb für die Vergangenheit?
items.e: Subject questions in past simple: Who won? (not Who did win?). No auxiliary needed when 'who' is the subject.
items.ed: Subjektfragen im Simple Past: Who won? (nicht Who did win?). Kein Hilfsverb nötig, wenn 'who' das Subjekt ist.

items.p: ___ idea was it to go to the park?
items.h: The question asks about who the idea belonged to.
items.hd: Die Frage fragt, wessen Idee es war.
items.e: 'Whose' before a noun shows possession: Whose idea? 'Who's' = who is/has — completely different!
items.ed: 'Whose' vor einem Nomen zeigt Besitz: Whose idea? 'Who's' = who is/has — etwas ganz anderes!

items.p: Which question is correct?
items.h: When 'who' is the subject, do you need 'does'?
items.hd: Wenn 'who' das Subjekt ist, brauchst du 'does'?
items.e: Subject questions with 'who': Who + verb (3rd person form). No auxiliary needed: Who lives? (not Who does live?)
items.ed: Subjektfragen mit 'who': Who + Verb (3. Person-Form). Kein Hilfsverb nötig: Who lives? (nicht Who does live?)

items.p: Choose the correct option:
items.h: Which word shows possession and comes before a noun?
items.hd: Welches Wort zeigt Besitz und steht vor einem Nomen?
items.e: 'Whose' shows possession before a noun: Whose turn? 'Who's' = 'who is' — a contraction, not a possessive.
items.ed: 'Whose' zeigt Besitz vor einem Nomen: Whose turn? 'Who's' = 'who is' — eine Kurzform, kein Possessiv.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Who does want ice cream?
items.h: Does 'who' as a subject need an auxiliary?
items.hd: Braucht 'who' als Subjekt ein Hilfsverb?
items.e: When 'who' is the subject, no auxiliary is needed: Who wants? (not Who does want?)
items.ed: Wenn 'who' das Subjekt ist, braucht man kein Hilfsverb: Who wants? (nicht Who does want?)

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Who's jacket is that?
items.h: 'Who's' and 'whose' are different words!
items.hd: 'Who's' und 'whose' sind verschiedene Wörter!
items.e: 'Who's' = who is/has (contraction). 'Whose' = belonging to whom (possession). Before a noun showing ownership, use 'whose'.
items.ed: 'Who's' = who is/has (Kurzform). 'Whose' = wem gehörend (Besitz). Vor einem Nomen, das Besitz zeigt, verwende 'whose'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Who did break the window?
items.h: Is 'who' the subject here? Does a subject question need 'did'?
items.hd: Ist 'who' hier das Subjekt? Braucht eine Subjektfrage 'did'?
items.e: 'Who' is the subject (someone broke the window), so use the past form directly: Who broke? No 'did' needed.
items.ed: 'Who' ist das Subjekt (jemand hat das Fenster zerbrochen), also verwende direkt die Vergangenheitsform: Who broke? Kein 'did' nötig.

items.p: Someone ate your sandwich from the fridge! You're annoyed. Ask: 'Who ___?'
items.h: 'Who' replaces the subject. Who + verb (no auxiliary 'did' needed).
items.hd: 'Who' ersetzt das Subjekt. Who + Verb (kein Hilfsverb 'did' nötig).
items.e: Who-subject questions: Who ate my sandwich? (No 'did' — 'who' IS the subject.)
items.ed: Who-Subjektfragen: Who ate my sandwich? (Kein 'did' — 'who' IST das Subjekt.)

items.p: You find a cute cat in the school yard. You want to know who owns it. Ask: 'Whose ___?'
items.h: 'Whose' asks about the owner. Whose + noun + is this?
items.hd: 'Whose' fragt nach dem Besitzer. Whose + Nomen + is this?
items.e: 'Whose cat is this?' asks about ownership. Whose + noun + verb.
items.ed: 'Whose cat is this?' fragt nach dem Besitz. Whose + Nomen + Verb.

items.p: A new student in your class speaks Japanese. You're curious. Ask: 'Who ___ in your class?'
a: Who speaks Japanese in your class?
items.h: 'Who' is the subject, so the verb takes third person -s: speaks.
items.hd: 'Who' ist das Subjekt, daher bekommt das Verb die dritte Person -s: speaks.
items.e: Who-subject questions: Who speaks Japanese? (third person -s because 'who' = one person.)
items.ed: Who-Subjektfragen: Who speaks Japanese? (Dritte Person -s, weil 'who' = eine Person.)

items.p: 🇩🇪 Wer hat das Spiel gewonnen?
items.h: Subject question in past — no 'did' needed.
items.hd: Subjektfrage in der Vergangenheit — kein 'did' nötig.
items.e: German uses Perfekt (hat gewonnen), but English uses past simple: Who won? No auxiliary in subject questions.
items.ed: Deutsch verwendet das Perfekt (hat gewonnen), aber Englisch verwendet das Simple Past: Who won? Kein Hilfsverb bei Subjektfragen.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Wessen Hund ist das?
items.h: 'Wessen' = which English question word?
items.hd: 'Wessen' = welches englische Fragewort?
items.e: German 'wessen' = English 'whose'. Whose + noun: Whose dog is that?
items.ed: Deutsch 'wessen' = Englisch 'whose'. Whose + Nomen: Whose dog is that?

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: the / won / who / competition / ?
items.h: Subject question: Who + verb + object.
items.hd: Subjektfrage: Who + Verb + Objekt.
items.e: Subject questions follow normal sentence order after 'who': Who won the competition?
items.ed: Subjektfragen folgen nach 'who' der normalen Satzstellung: Who won the competition?

items.p:
Match the question words with the correct endings:
1. Who
2. Whose
3. Who
4. Whose

a. car is parked outside?
b. made this cake?
c. books are these?
d. wants to come along?
items.h: 'Who' asks about a person. 'Whose' asks about ownership (noun follows).
items.hd: 'Who' fragt nach einer Person. 'Whose' fragt nach Besitz (Nomen folgt).
items.e: Who + verb (made, wants) asks about a person. Whose + noun (car, books) asks about ownership.
items.ed: Who + Verb (made, wants) fragt nach einer Person. Whose + Nomen (car, books) fragt nach Besitz.

items.p: Emma taught us the dance. → Ask about the person. Start with 'Who'.
items.h: 'Who' replaces 'Emma'. Subject question — no 'did'!
items.hd: 'Who' ersetzt 'Emma'. Subjektfrage — kein 'did'!
items.e: Who taught? (subject question, past tense). No 'did' needed because 'who' is the subject.
items.ed: Who taught? (Subjektfrage, Vergangenheit). Kein 'did' nötig, weil 'who' das Subjekt ist.

items.p: I ___ just finished my homework.
items.h: Which auxiliary goes with 'I' in the present perfect?
items.hd: Welches Hilfsverb passt zu 'I' im Present Perfect?
items.e: Present perfect: I/you/we/they have + past participle. He/she/it has + past participle.
items.ed: Present Perfect: I/you/we/they have + Past Participle. He/she/it has + Past Participle.

items.p: She ___ just arrived at school.
items.h: Which auxiliary goes with 'she' in the present perfect?
items.hd: Welches Hilfsverb passt zu 'she' im Present Perfect?
items.e: He/she/it + has + past participle. 'She has arrived' (not 'have' or 'is').
items.ed: He/she/it + has + Past Participle. 'She has arrived' (nicht 'have' oder 'is').

items.p: He has ___ his lunch already.
items.h: After 'has', you need the past participle form.
items.hd: Nach 'has' brauchst du die Past-Participle-Form.
items.e: Present perfect: has + past participle. eat → ate (past) → eaten (past participle).
items.ed: Present Perfect: has + Past Participle. eat → ate (Vergangenheit) → eaten (Past Participle).

items.p: We have just ___ a great film.
items.h: What is the past participle of 'see'? (see – saw – ???)
items.hd: Was ist das Past Participle von 'see'? (see – saw – ???)
items.e: see → saw → seen. After 'have/has', always use the past participle: have seen (not saw).
items.ed: see → saw → seen. Nach 'have/has' immer das Past Participle verwenden: have seen (nicht saw).

items.p: My brother ___ broken his arm.
items.h: 'My brother' = he. Which auxiliary for he/she/it?
items.hd: 'My brother' = he. Welches Hilfsverb für he/she/it?
items.e: 'My brother' = 3rd person singular (he), so we use 'has': He has broken his arm.
items.ed: 'My brother' = 3. Person Einzahl (he), also verwenden wir 'has': He has broken his arm.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: She has just made a cake.
a: She has just made a cake.
ds: She has just maked a cake.
She have just made a cake.
She just has made a cake.
items.h: Check: has or have? Regular or irregular past participle? Word order with 'just'?
items.hd: Prüfe: has oder have? Regelmäßiges oder unregelmäßiges Past Participle? Wortstellung mit 'just'?
items.e: She has (3rd person) + just (between auxiliary and participle) + made (irregular: make-made-made).
items.ed: She has (3. Person) + just (zwischen Hilfsverb und Partizip) + made (unregelmäßig: make-made-made).

items.p: Choose the correct option:
items.h: Check: have or has with 'I'? What is the past participle of 'lose'?
items.hd: Prüfe: have oder has mit 'I'? Was ist das Past Participle von 'lose'?
items.e: I + have + past participle: I have lost. lose → lost → lost (irregular verb).
items.ed: I + have + Past Participle: I have lost. lose → lost → lost (unregelmäßiges Verb).

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She have just called you.
items.h: Which auxiliary does 'she' need in the present perfect?
items.hd: Welches Hilfsverb braucht 'she' im Present Perfect?
items.e: 'She' is 3rd person singular → has (not have). She has just called you.
items.ed: 'She' ist 3. Person Einzahl → has (nicht have). She has just called you.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I have broke my new headphones.
items.c: I have broken my new headphones.
a: I have broken my new headphones.
I have broken my new headphones
items.h: What is the past participle of 'break'? (break – broke – ???)
items.hd: Was ist das Past Participle von 'break'? (break – broke – ???)
items.e: break → broke (past) → broken (past participle). After 'have', use the participle: have broken.
items.ed: break → broke (Vergangenheit) → broken (Past Participle). Nach 'have' verwendet man das Partizip: have broken.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: He has writed a letter to his grandma.
items.c: He has written a letter to his grandma.
a: He has written a letter to his grandma.
He has written a letter to his grandma
items.h: Write is irregular. The past participle is not 'writed'.
items.hd: Write ist unregelmäßig. Das Past Participle ist nicht 'writed'.
items.e: Write is irregular: write-wrote-written. Use 'written' after has/have.
items.ed: Write ist unregelmäßig: write-wrote-written. Verwende 'written' nach has/have.

items.p: You just finished your homework. Tell your mum: 'I have just ___ my homework.'
items.h: Present perfect: have/has + past participle. Finish → finished.
items.hd: Present Perfect: have/has + Past Participle. Finish → finished.
items.e: 'I have just finished my homework.' 'Just' goes between 'have' and the past participle.
items.ed: 'I have just finished my homework.' 'Just' steht zwischen 'have' und dem Past Participle.

items.p: Tom bought a new skateboard a moment ago. Tell your friend: 'Tom ___ (just / buy) a new skateboard.'
items.c: has just bought a new skateboard
a: has just bought a new skateboard
items.h: The past simple 'bought' happened a moment ago → rewrite with present perfect + just. Tom = he → 'has'. Buy → bought (past participle).
items.hd: Das Simple Past 'bought' ist gerade passiert → schreibe es mit Present Perfect + just um. Tom = he → 'has'. Buy → bought (Past Participle).
items.e: Third person: Tom has just bought. 'Has' for he/she/it + past participle.
items.ed: Dritte Person: Tom has just bought. 'Has' für he/she/it + Past Participle.

items.p: The children found a kitten in the garden a moment ago. Say: 'The children ___ (just / find) a kitten.'
items.h: The past simple 'found' happened a moment ago → rewrite with present perfect + just. The children = they → 'have'. Find → found (past participle).
items.hd: Das Simple Past 'found' ist gerade passiert → schreibe es mit Present Perfect + just um. The children = they → 'have'. Find → found (Past Participle).
items.e: Plural subject: The children have just found. 'Have' for I/you/we/they + past participle.
items.ed: Pluralsubjekt: The children have just found. 'Have' für I/you/we/they + Past Participle.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ich habe gerade mein Frühstück gegessen.
items.c: I have just eaten my breakfast.
a: I have just eaten my breakfast.
I have just eaten my breakfast
items.h: 'Gerade' = 'just'. 'Gegessen' is the past participle of 'eat' → 'eaten'.
items.hd: 'Gerade' = 'just'. 'Gegessen' ist das Past Participle von 'eat' → 'eaten'.
items.e: German 'habe gerade gegessen' → English present perfect with 'just': I have just eaten. Eat is irregular: eat-ate-eaten.
items.ed: Deutsch 'habe gerade gegessen' → Englisch Present Perfect mit 'just': I have just eaten. Eat ist unregelmäßig: eat-ate-eaten.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Er hat gerade sein Fahrrad repariert.
items.c: He has just fixed his bike.
a: He has just fixed his bike.
He has just fixed his bike
He has just repaired his bike.
He has just repaired his bike
He has just fixed his bicycle.
He has just fixed his bicycle
items.h: 'Er hat gerade repariert' → he has just fixed/repaired.
items.hd: 'Er hat gerade repariert' → he has just fixed/repaired.
items.e: 'Er' = 3rd person → 'has'. German Perfekt with 'gerade' → English present perfect with 'just'.
items.ed: 'Er' = 3. Person → 'has'. Deutsches Perfekt mit 'gerade' → Englisches Present Perfect mit 'just'.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: just / has / a / she / letter / written / .
items.c: She has just written a letter.
a: She has just written a letter.
She has just written a letter
items.h: Subject + has/have + just + past participle + object.
items.hd: Subjekt + has/have + just + Past Participle + Objekt.
items.e: Present perfect word order: She + has + just + written + a letter. 'Just' goes between the auxiliary and participle.
items.ed: Present-Perfect-Wortstellung: She + has + just + written + a letter. 'Just' steht zwischen Hilfsverb und Partizip.

items.p:
Match the verb with its past participle:
1. go
2. buy
3. fall
4. break
5. find
6. lose
7. cut

a. cut
b. fallen
c. gone
d. lost
e. found
f. bought
g. broken
items.h: go-went-gone, buy-bought-bought, fall-fell-fallen. Think three forms.
items.hd: go-went-gone, buy-bought-bought, fall-fell-fallen. Denke an die drei Formen.
items.e: go→gone, buy→bought, fall→fallen, break→broken, find→found, lose→lost, cut→cut. Learn all three forms!
items.ed: go→gone, buy→bought, fall→fallen, break→broken, find→found, lose→lost, cut→cut. Lerne alle drei Formen!

items.p: You walk into the kitchen. Your mum is smiling and there's a fresh cake on the table. Which sentence fits?
items.c: She has just finished baking the cake.
a: She has just finished baking the cake.
ds: She just finished baking the cake.
She finished baking the cake yesterday.
items.h: The cake is fresh and she just did it. Which tense shows a recently completed action?
items.hd: Der Kuchen ist frisch und sie hat es gerade gemacht. Welche Zeitform zeigt eine kürzlich abgeschlossene Handlung?
items.e: Present perfect with 'just' shows something happened very recently: She has just finished.
items.ed: Present Perfect mit 'just' zeigt, dass etwas gerade erst passiert ist: She has just finished.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: an / has / eaten / he / apple / just / .
items.c: He has just eaten an apple.
a: He has just eaten an apple.
He has just eaten an apple
items.h: Subject + has/have + just + past participle + object.
items.hd: Subjekt + has/have + just + Past Participle + Objekt.
items.e: Order: He has just eaten an apple. 'Just' goes between has and the past participle.
items.ed: Reihenfolge: He has just eaten an apple. 'Just' steht zwischen has und Past Participle.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: the / have / film / they / seen / already / .
items.c: They have already seen the film.
a: They have already seen the film.
They have already seen the film
items.h: Subject + have/has + already + past participle + object.
items.hd: Subjekt + have/has + already + Past Participle + Objekt.
items.e: Order: They have already seen the film. Like 'just', 'already' sits between have and the past participle.
items.ed: Reihenfolge: They have already seen the film. Wie 'just' steht auch 'already' zwischen have und Past Participle.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I have see the new Spider-Man movie.
items.c: I have seen the new Spider-Man movie.
a: I have seen the new Spider-Man movie.
I have seen the new Spider-Man movie
items.h: See is irregular. Which form comes after 'have'?
items.hd: See ist unregelmäßig. Welche Form kommt nach 'have'?
items.e: See is irregular: see-saw-seen. After have/has use the past participle: seen.
items.ed: See ist unregelmäßig: see-saw-seen. Nach have/has das Past Participle: seen.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She has buyed a new phone.
items.c: She has bought a new phone.
a: She has bought a new phone.
She has bought a new phone
items.h: Buy is irregular. The past participle is not 'buyed'.
items.hd: Buy ist unregelmäßig. Das Past Participle ist nicht 'buyed'.
items.e: Buy is irregular: buy-bought-bought. Past participle = bought.
items.ed: Buy ist unregelmäßig: buy-bought-bought. Past Participle = bought.

items.p:
Match the verb with its past participle:
1. hurt
2. win
3. see
4. be
5. meet
6. put
7. write

a. met
b. been
c. put
d. written
e. hurt
f. seen
g. won
items.h: hurt-hurt-hurt, win-won-won, see-saw-seen, be-was-been.
items.hd: hurt-hurt-hurt, win-won-won, see-saw-seen, be-was-been.
items.e: hurt→hurt (same!), win→won, see→seen, be→been, meet→met, put→put (same!), write→written.
items.ed: hurt→hurt (gleich!), win→won, see→seen, be→been, meet→met, put→put (gleich!), write→written.

items.p:
Match the verb with its past participle:
1. eat
2. think
3. hit
4. ring
5. read
6. know
7. tell

a. rung
b. thought
c. known
d. hit
e. eaten
f. told
g. read
items.h: eat-ate-eaten, think-thought-thought, ring-rang-rung.
items.hd: eat-ate-eaten, think-thought-thought, ring-rang-rung.
items.e: eat→eaten, think→thought, hit→hit (same!), ring→rung, read→read (spelled same, said 'red'!), know→known, tell→told.
items.ed: eat→eaten, think→thought, hit→hit (gleich!), ring→rung, read→read (gleich geschrieben, 'red' ausgesprochen!), know→known, tell→told.

items.p: Fill in the missing forms. Check your answers when you're done.
items.h: These are the first 11 irregular verbs from page 98. Practise the three forms!
items.hd: Das sind die ersten 11 unregelmäßigen Verben von Seite 98. Übe die drei Formen!
items.e: Three forms: base – past simple – past participle. Irregular verbs don't follow a rule — you just have to learn them.
items.ed: Drei Formen: Grundform – Simple Past – Past Participle. Unregelmäßige Verben folgen keiner Regel — du musst sie auswendig lernen.

items.p: Fill in the missing forms. Check your answers when you're done.
items.h: These are the remaining 10 irregular verbs from page 98. Watch out: 'read' is spelled the same for all three forms but pronounced differently!
items.hd: Das sind die restlichen 10 unregelmäßigen Verben von Seite 98. Achtung: 'read' schreibt man in allen drei Formen gleich, spricht es aber anders aus!
items.e: Three forms: base – past simple – past participle. Some verbs (put, hit, cut, hurt, read) look the same in all three forms.
items.ed: Drei Formen: Grundform – Simple Past – Past Participle. Einige Verben (put, hit, cut, hurt, read) sehen in allen drei Formen gleich aus.

items.p: It's cold! I ___ close the window.
items.h: This is a spontaneous decision — made right now.
items.hd: Das ist eine spontane Entscheidung — gerade jetzt getroffen.
items.e: Spontaneous decisions use 'will': I'll close the window. (decided right now, not planned before).
items.ed: Spontane Entscheidungen verwenden 'will': I'll close the window. (gerade jetzt entschieden, nicht vorher geplant).

items.p: Don't worry, I ___ help you with your homework.
items.h: This is an offer to help — decided at this moment.
items.hd: Das ist ein Hilfsangebot — in diesem Moment entschieden.
items.e: Offers use 'will': I'll help you. Will + base form (never 'wills').
items.ed: Angebote verwenden 'will': I'll help you. Will + Grundform (nie 'wills').

items.p: I am not sure, but I think it ___ rain tomorrow.
items.h: 'I think' shows it's an opinion or prediction about the future.
items.hd: 'I think' zeigt, dass es eine Meinung oder Vorhersage über die Zukunft ist.
items.e: Predictions based on opinion use 'will': I think it will rain. (no evidence, just a guess).
items.ed: Vorhersagen basierend auf Meinung verwenden 'will': I think it will rain. (kein Beweis, nur eine Vermutung).

items.p: She ___ be 13 next month. Her birthday is on the 14th.
items.h: 'Will' never changes form — not even for he/she/it.
items.hd: 'Will' ändert seine Form nie — auch nicht für he/she/it.
items.e: 'Will' is the same for all subjects: I will, she will, they will. Never 'wills'. Facts about the future use 'will'.
items.ed: 'Will' ist für alle Subjekte gleich: I will, she will, they will. Nie 'wills'. Fakten über die Zukunft verwenden 'will'.

items.p: I just decided: he ___ come to the party. He's feeling sick.
items.h: The negative of 'will' is 'won't' (not 'willn't').
items.hd: Die Verneinung von 'will' ist 'won't' (nicht 'willn't').
items.e: will not → won't. This is irregular! It's not 'willn't'. He won't come = He will not come.
items.ed: will not → won't. Das ist unregelmäßig! Es ist nicht 'willn't'. He won't come = He will not come.

items.p: The phone is ringing. I ___ answer it!
items.h: You just heard the phone — this is a decision made right now.
items.hd: Du hast gerade das Telefon gehört — das ist eine Entscheidung, die gerade getroffen wird.
items.e: Spontaneous decisions and offers at the moment of speaking use 'will': I'll answer it!
items.ed: Spontane Entscheidungen und Angebote im Moment des Sprechens verwenden 'will': I'll answer it!

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: I think she will like the present.
a: I think she will like the present.
ds: I think she wills like the present.
I think she will likes the present.
I think she will to like the present.
items.h: After 'will', what form does the main verb take?
items.hd: Welche Form nimmt das Hauptverb nach 'will' an?
items.e: will + base form (no -s, no 'to'): she will like. 'Will' never changes: not 'wills', not 'will likes'.
items.ed: will + Grundform (kein -s, kein 'to'): she will like. 'Will' ändert sich nie: nicht 'wills', nicht 'will likes'.

items.p: Choose the correct option:
items.c: We won't be late, I promise.
a: We won't be late, I promise.
ds: We willn't be late, I promise.
We don't will be late, I promise.
We won't to be late, I promise.
items.h: What is the correct short form of 'will not'?
items.hd: Was ist die richtige Kurzform von 'will not'?
items.e: will not → won't (irregular!). Then base form: won't be. Not 'willn't', not 'don't will'.
items.ed: will not → won't (unregelmäßig!). Dann Grundform: won't be. Nicht 'willn't', nicht 'don't will'.

items.p: Which sentence uses the CORRECT future form?
items.c: Look at those clouds! It's going to rain.
a: Look at those clouds! It's going to rain.
ds: Look at those clouds! It will rain.
Look at those clouds! It rains.
Look at those clouds! It's raining.
items.h: There is visible evidence (the clouds). Which future form is for evidence-based predictions?
items.hd: Es gibt sichtbare Beweise (die Wolken). Welche Zukunftsform ist für beweisbasierte Vorhersagen?
items.e: Evidence-based predictions use 'going to': Look at those clouds — it's going to rain. 'Will' is for opinions without evidence.
items.ed: Beweisbasierte Vorhersagen verwenden 'going to': Schau dir die Wolken an — it's going to rain. 'Will' ist für Meinungen ohne Beweis.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She wills come to the party.
items.c: She will come to the party.
a: She will come to the party.
She will come to the party
items.h: Does 'will' change for he/she/it?
items.hd: Ändert sich 'will' für he/she/it?
items.e: 'Will' never changes form — no -s for 3rd person: she will (not wills).
items.ed: 'Will' ändert seine Form nie — kein -s für die 3. Person: she will (nicht wills).

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: They willn't forget your birthday.
a: They will not forget your birthday.
They will not forget your birthday
items.h: What is the correct short form of 'will not'?
items.hd: Was ist die richtige Kurzform von 'will not'?
items.e: will not → won't (not 'willn't'!). This is an irregular contraction you need to learn.
items.ed: will not → won't (nicht 'willn't'!). Das ist eine unregelmäßige Kurzform, die man lernen muss.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I will to help you carry the bags.
items.c: I will help you carry the bags.
a: I will help you carry the bags.
I will help you carry the bags
I'll help you carry the bags.
I'll help you carry the bags
items.h: Does 'will' need 'to' before the main verb?
items.hd: Braucht 'will' ein 'to' vor dem Hauptverb?
items.e: will + base form (no 'to'): I will help (not I will to help). Unlike 'want to' or 'need to', 'will' takes the bare infinitive.
items.ed: will + Grundform (kein 'to'): I will help (nicht I will to help). Anders als 'want to' oder 'need to' nimmt 'will' den bloßen Infinitiv.

items.p: Your friend is worried you won't phone tonight. Make a promise: 'I ___ (call) you tonight.'
items.h: Will + base form for promises and spontaneous decisions.
items.hd: Will + Grundform für Versprechen und spontane Entscheidungen.
items.e: 'Will' is used for promises: I will call you tonight. (I promise.)
items.ed: 'Will' wird für Versprechen verwendet: I will call you tonight. (Ich verspreche es.)

items.p: Your teacher asks if Ben will be ready. You don't think so. Say: 'He ___ (not finish) on time.'
items.h: Negative: will not = won't. Won't + base form.
items.hd: Verneinung: will not = won't. Won't + Grundform.
items.e: Negative will future: He won't finish. Won't = will not.
items.ed: Verneinetes Will Future: He won't finish. Won't = will not.

items.p: You want to know when your cousins arrive. Ask your dad: '___ they ___ (arrive) at six?'
items.h: Question: Will + subject + base form?
items.hd: Frage: Will + Subjekt + Grundform?
items.e: Will-future questions: Will + they + arrive? Put 'will' before the subject.
items.ed: Will-Future-Fragen: Will + they + arrive? Stelle 'will' vor das Subjekt.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ich werde dir helfen.
items.h: 'Werde helfen' → will + base form.
items.hd: 'Werde helfen' → will + Grundform.
items.e: German 'werde ... helfen' → English 'will help'. will + base form, no 'to'.
items.ed: Deutsch 'werde ... helfen' → Englisch 'will help'. will + Grundform, kein 'to'.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Er wird morgen nicht kommen.
items.h: 'Wird nicht' → won't / will not.
items.hd: 'Wird nicht' → won't / will not.
items.e: German 'wird nicht kommen' → English 'won't come'. Negative: will not = won't.
items.ed: Deutsch 'wird nicht kommen' → Englisch 'won't come'. Verneinung: will not = won't.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: will / you / I / later / call / .
items.h: Subject + will + verb + object + time.
items.hd: Subjekt + will + Verb + Objekt + Zeit.
items.e: Future with 'will': Subject + will + base form: I will call you later.
items.ed: Zukunft mit 'will': Subjekt + will + Grundform: I will call you later.

items.p:
Match the situation with the correct future form:
1. I've already bought the tickets. We ... (go) to the cinema.
2. The doorbell is ringing. I ... (open) the door.
3. I think robots ... (do) all housework in 2050.
4. Look at the sky! It ... (snow).

a. will open
b. are going to go
c. will do
d. is going to snow
items.h: Will = spontaneous/opinion. Going to = planned/evidence.
items.hd: Will = spontan/Meinung. Going to = geplant/Beweis.
items.e: 1. Planned (tickets bought) → going to. 2. Spontaneous → will. 3. Opinion (I think) → will. 4. Evidence (sky) → going to.
items.ed: 1. Geplant (Tickets gekauft) → going to. 2. Spontan → will. 3. Meinung (I think) → will. 4. Beweis (Himmel) → going to.

items.p: Your friend drops her heavy bag. You want to help immediately. Which sentence fits this moment?
items.c: I'll carry that bag for you.
a: I'll carry that bag for you.
ds: I carry that bag for you.
I'm going to carry that bag for you.
I carried that bag for you.
items.h: You decided to help right now, in this moment. Which future form is for spontaneous decisions?
items.hd: Du hast dich gerade jetzt entschieden zu helfen. Welche Zukunftsform ist für spontane Entscheidungen?
items.e: 'Will' is used for spontaneous decisions made at the moment of speaking: 'I'll carry that bag for you.'
items.ed: 'Will' wird für spontane Entscheidungen verwendet, die im Moment des Sprechens getroffen werden: 'I'll carry that bag for you.'

items.p: She sings very ___. Everyone loves her voice.
items.h: We need an adverb here (how does she sing?), not an adjective.
items.hd: Wir brauchen hier ein Adverb (wie singt sie?), kein Adjektiv.
items.e: We need an adverb to describe how she sings. 'Beautiful' is an adjective; add -ly → beautifully.
items.ed: Wir brauchen ein Adverb, um zu beschreiben, wie sie singt. 'Beautiful' ist ein Adjektiv; füge -ly hinzu → beautifully.

items.p: Please walk ___. The baby is sleeping.
items.h: How should you walk? You need an adverb after the verb.
items.hd: Wie sollst du gehen? Du brauchst ein Adverb nach dem Verb.
items.e: 'Quiet' is an adjective. To describe how someone walks, add -ly: quietly.
items.ed: 'Quiet' ist ein Adjektiv. Um zu beschreiben, wie jemand geht, füge -ly hinzu: quietly.

items.p: He plays the guitar really ___.
items.h: 'Good' has a special adverb form. It does NOT follow the -ly rule.
items.hd: 'Good' hat eine besondere Adverbform. Es folgt NICHT der -ly Regel.
items.e: 'Good' is an adjective. The adverb is 'well' (irregular): He plays well.
items.ed: 'Good' ist ein Adjektiv. Das Adverb ist 'well' (unregelmäßig): He plays well.

items.p: The children played ___ in the garden all afternoon.
items.h: When the adjective ends in -y, change -y to -ily.
items.hd: Wenn das Adjektiv auf -y endet, ändere -y zu -ily.
items.e: 'Happy' ends in -y, so we change it to -ily: happily. This describes how they played.
items.ed: 'Happy' endet auf -y, also ändern wir es zu -ily: happily. Das beschreibt, wie sie gespielt haben.

items.p: The cat can run very ___.
items.h: Some adverbs look exactly the same as the adjective. 'Fast' is one of them.
items.hd: Manche Adverbien sehen genauso aus wie das Adjektiv. 'Fast' ist eines davon.
items.e: 'Fast' is both an adjective and an adverb. We never say 'fastly'!
items.ed: 'Fast' ist sowohl Adjektiv als auch Adverb. Wir sagen nie 'fastly'!

items.p: She did her homework ___ because she wanted to watch TV.
items.h: How did she do her homework? Use the adverb form of 'quick'.
items.hd: Wie hat sie ihre Hausaufgaben gemacht? Verwende die Adverbform von 'quick'.
items.e: 'Quick' + -ly = quickly. The adverb describes how she did her homework.
items.ed: 'Quick' + -ly = quickly. Das Adverb beschreibt, wie sie ihre Hausaufgaben gemacht hat.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: He spoke to the teacher politely.
a: He spoke to the teacher politely.
ds: He spoke to the teacher polite.
He politely spoke to the teacher politely.
He spoke polite to the teacher.
items.h: The adverb usually goes after the verb or after the object.
items.hd: Das Adverb steht meistens nach dem Verb oder nach dem Objekt.
items.e: 'Politely' is the adverb form. It goes after the object: spoke to the teacher politely.
items.ed: 'Politely' ist die Adverbform. Es steht nach dem Objekt: spoke to the teacher politely.

items.p: Which is the correct adverb form of 'good'?
items.h: 'Good' does not follow the normal -ly rule for adverbs.
items.hd: 'Good' folgt nicht der normalen -ly Regel für Adverbien.
items.e: 'Good' → 'well' is irregular. We say 'She dances well', not 'She dances goodly'.
items.ed: 'Good' → 'well' ist unregelmäßig. Wir sagen 'She dances well', nicht 'She dances goodly'.

items.p: Which sentence uses the adverb correctly?
items.h: 'Late' as an adverb means 'not on time'. Be careful: 'lately' means 'recently'!
items.hd: 'Late' als Adverb bedeutet 'nicht pünktlich'. Achtung: 'lately' bedeutet 'kürzlich'!
items.e: 'Late' is both adjective and adverb (= not on time). 'Lately' exists but means 'recently' — a different meaning!
items.ed: 'Late' ist sowohl Adjektiv als auch Adverb (= nicht pünktlich). 'Lately' gibt es, aber es bedeutet 'kürzlich' — eine andere Bedeutung!

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: He runs very fastly in the morning.
items.c: He runs very fast in the morning.
a: He runs very fast in the morning.
He runs very fast in the morning
items.h: 'Fast' is a special adverb. Does it need -ly?
items.hd: 'Fast' ist ein besonderes Adverb. Braucht es -ly?
items.e: 'Fast' is already an adverb — we never add -ly. The correct form is 'fast', not 'fastly'.
items.ed: 'Fast' ist bereits ein Adverb — wir fügen nie -ly hinzu. Die richtige Form ist 'fast', nicht 'fastly'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She speaks English very good.
items.h: 'Good' is an adjective. What is the adverb form?
items.hd: 'Good' ist ein Adjektiv. Wie heißt die Adverbform?
items.e: 'Good' is an adjective, not an adverb. The adverb form is 'well': She speaks English well.
items.ed: 'Good' ist ein Adjektiv, kein Adverb. Die Adverbform ist 'well': She speaks English well.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: The dog sat patient and waited for food.
items.c: The dog sat patiently and waited for food.
a: The dog sat patiently and waited for food.
The dog sat patiently and waited for food
items.h: How did the dog sit? You need an adverb, not an adjective.
items.hd: Wie hat der Hund gesessen? Du brauchst ein Adverb, kein Adjektiv.
items.e: 'Patient' is an adjective. The adverb is 'patiently' (add -ly): The dog sat patiently.
items.ed: 'Patient' ist ein Adjektiv. Das Adverb ist 'patiently' (füge -ly hinzu): The dog sat patiently.

items.p: Your mum is a very safe driver. Describe how she drives: 'She is a careful driver. → She drives ___.'
items.h: Adjective → adverb: careful → carefully (add -ly).
items.hd: Adjektiv → Adverb: careful → carefully (-ly anhängen).
items.e: Adverbs of manner: careful → carefully. Add -ly to make an adverb from an adjective.
items.ed: Adverbien der Art und Weise: careful → carefully. Hänge -ly an, um aus einem Adjektiv ein Adverb zu machen.

items.p: Your brother doesn't try hard at work. Describe how he works: 'He is a lazy worker. → He works ___.'
items.h: Adjectives ending in -y: change y to i, then add -ly. Lazy → lazily.
items.hd: Adjektive auf -y: ändere y zu i, dann hänge -ly an. Lazy → lazily.
items.e: Adverb formation: lazy → lazily. Change -y to -ily.
items.ed: Adverbbildung: lazy → lazily. Ändere -y zu -ily.

items.p: Your sister has an amazing voice. Describe how she sings: 'She is a good singer. → She sings ___.'
items.h: 'Good' is irregular. The adverb of 'good' is NOT 'goodly'!
items.hd: 'Good' ist unregelmäßig. Das Adverb von 'good' ist NICHT 'goodly'!
items.e: Irregular adverb: good → well. She sings well (not 'goodly').
items.ed: Unregelmäßiges Adverb: good → well. She sings well (nicht 'goodly').

items.p: 🇩🇪 Er spricht sehr leise.
items.h: 'Leise' describes how he speaks — you need an adverb.
items.hd: 'Leise' beschreibt, wie er spricht — du brauchst ein Adverb.
items.e: German 'leise' = quietly (adverb). In English we must use the adverb form after the verb: speaks quietly.
items.ed: Deutsch 'leise' = quietly (Adverb). Im Englischen müssen wir die Adverbform nach dem Verb verwenden: speaks quietly.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Sie schwimmt gut.
items.h: 'Gut' as an adverb is NOT 'good' in English. Think about the irregular form.
items.hd: 'Gut' als Adverb ist im Englischen NICHT 'good'. Denke an die unregelmäßige Form.
items.e: German 'gut' (adverb) = 'well' in English. 'Good' is an adjective, 'well' is the adverb.
items.ed: Deutsch 'gut' (Adverb) = 'well' im Englischen. 'Good' ist ein Adjektiv, 'well' ist das Adverb.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: slowly / the / walked / old / man / very
items.c: The old man walked very slowly.
a: The old man walked very slowly.
The old man walked very slowly
items.h: Start with the subject, then the verb, then the adverb at the end.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Subjekt, dann das Verb, dann das Adverb am Ende.
items.e: The adverb 'slowly' goes after the verb: The old man walked very slowly.
items.ed: Das Adverb 'slowly' steht nach dem Verb: The old man walked very slowly.

items.p:
Match each adjective with its correct adverb form:
1. happy
2. good
3. fast
4. careful
5. easy
a. well
b. easily
c. carefully
d. fast
e. happily
items.h: Watch out for the irregular forms and the ones that stay the same.
items.hd: Achte auf die unregelmäßigen Formen und die, die gleich bleiben.
items.e: happy → happily (-y to -ily), good → well (irregular), fast → fast (no change), careful → carefully (+ly), easy → easily (-y to -ily).
items.ed: happy → happily (-y zu -ily), good → well (unregelmäßig), fast → fast (keine Änderung), careful → carefully (+ly), easy → easily (-y zu -ily).

items.p: You want to know how your friend dances. Ask a question using 'how'.
items.h: Use 'How' + does + subject + base form verb.
items.hd: Verwende 'How' + does + Subjekt + Grundform des Verbs.
items.e: To ask about manner, use 'How' + auxiliary + subject + verb: How does your friend dance?
items.ed: Um nach der Art und Weise zu fragen, verwende 'How' + Hilfsverb + Subjekt + Verb: How does your friend dance?

items.p: I have ___ finished my homework. I can play now.
items.h: The sentence is positive and means the homework is done. Which signal word fits?
items.hd: Der Satz ist positiv und bedeutet, dass die Hausaufgabe fertig ist. Welches Signalwort passt?
items.e: 'Already' is used in positive sentences to say something happened sooner than expected: I have already finished.
items.ed: 'Already' wird in positiven Sätzen verwendet, um zu sagen, dass etwas früher als erwartet passiert ist: I have already finished.

items.p: Have you ___ been to London?
items.h: This question asks about life experience (at any time in your life).
items.hd: Diese Frage fragt nach Lebenserfahrung (irgendwann in deinem Leben).
items.e: 'Ever' is used between have/has and the past participle in questions about life experience: Have you ever been...?
items.ed: 'Ever' steht zwischen have/has und dem Partizip in Fragen über Lebenserfahrung: Have you ever been...?

items.p: She hasn't called me ___.
items.h: This is a negative sentence. The signal word goes at the end.
items.hd: Das ist ein negativer Satz. Das Signalwort steht am Ende.
items.e: 'Yet' is used at the end of negative sentences and questions: She hasn't called me yet.
items.ed: 'Yet' steht am Ende von negativen Sätzen und Fragen: She hasn't called me yet.

items.p: I have ___ eaten sushi. I don't know what it tastes like.
items.h: The speaker has zero experience with sushi. No 'not' in the sentence!
items.hd: Der Sprecher hat keine Erfahrung mit Sushi. Kein 'not' im Satz!
items.e: 'Never' means 'at no time'. It goes between have and the past participle. Note: no 'not' needed — 'never' is already negative.
items.ed: 'Never' bedeutet 'nie'. Es steht zwischen have und dem Partizip. Beachte: kein 'not' nötig — 'never' ist bereits negativ.

items.p: Has Tom finished the test ___? — No, he's still writing.
items.h: This question asks whether something is finished now. The word goes at the end.
items.hd: Diese Frage fragt, ob etwas jetzt fertig ist. Das Wort steht am Ende.
items.e: 'Yet' is used at the end of questions to ask if something has happened up to now: Has he finished yet?
items.ed: 'Yet' steht am Ende von Fragen, um zu fragen, ob etwas bis jetzt passiert ist: Has he finished yet?

items.p: We have ___ visited three museums today. Let's go to another one!
items.h: The sentence is positive and shows surprise at how much they have done.
items.hd: Der Satz ist positiv und zeigt Überraschung darüber, wie viel sie gemacht haben.
items.e: 'Already' in positive present perfect sentences shows something happened sooner than expected.
items.ed: 'Already' in positiven Present-Perfect-Sätzen zeigt, dass etwas früher als erwartet passiert ist.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.h: 'Never' goes between have/has and the past participle. It already makes the sentence negative.
items.hd: 'Never' steht zwischen have/has und dem Partizip. Es macht den Satz bereits negativ.
items.e: 'Never' goes between have and the past participle: I have never tried. No 'not' or 'n't' is needed!
items.ed: 'Never' steht zwischen have und dem Partizip: I have never tried. Kein 'not' oder 'n't' nötig!

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I haven't never been to Paris.
items.c: I have never been to Paris.
a: I have never been to Paris.
I have never been to Paris
items.h: Can you use 'haven't' and 'never' together? That would be two negatives!
items.hd: Kann man 'haven't' und 'never' zusammen verwenden? Das wären zwei Verneinungen!
items.e: Double negative: 'haven't' + 'never' = two negatives. Use 'have never' (no 'not'): I have never been to Paris.
items.ed: Doppelte Verneinung: 'haven't' + 'never' = zwei Verneinungen. Verwende 'have never' (ohne 'not'): I have never been to Paris.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: We / have / already / visited / the / museum
items.c: We have already visited the museum.
a: We have already visited the museum.
We have already visited the museum
items.h: Where should 'already' go — before or after the past participle?
items.hd: Wo soll 'already' stehen — vor oder nach dem Partizip?
items.e: 'Already' goes between have and the past participle: We have already visited, not 'visited already'.
items.ed: 'Already' steht zwischen have und dem Partizip: We have already visited, nicht 'visited already'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Have you ever been yet to New York?
items.c: Have you ever been to New York?
a: Have you ever been to New York?
Have you ever been to New York
Have you been to New York yet?
Have you been to New York yet
items.h: Can 'ever' and 'yet' be in the same question? Choose one!
items.hd: Können 'ever' und 'yet' in derselben Frage stehen? Wähle eines!
items.e: Don't mix 'ever' and 'yet' in the same question. Use either 'Have you ever been...?' or 'Have you been... yet?'
items.ed: Mische nicht 'ever' und 'yet' in derselben Frage. Verwende entweder 'Have you ever been...?' oder 'Have you been... yet?'

items.p: Your teacher asks if you've done the project. You haven't! Say: 'I ___ (not do) my project ___.'
a: have not done my project yet
I haven't done my project yet.
items.h: Negative present perfect + 'yet' at the end: haven't + past participle + yet.
items.hd: Verneinetes Present Perfect + 'yet' am Ende: haven't + Past Participle + yet.
items.e: 'Yet' goes at the end in negative present perfect: I haven't done my project yet.
items.ed: 'Yet' steht am Ende beim verneinten Present Perfect: I haven't done my project yet.

items.p: Your friend asks if you've tried octopus. You haven't, ever. Say: 'I ___ (never / eat) octopus.'
items.h: 'Never' goes between 'have' and the past participle.
items.hd: 'Never' steht zwischen 'have' und dem Past Participle.
items.e: Present perfect + never: I have never eaten. 'Never' replaces 'not...ever'.
items.ed: Present Perfect + never: I have never eaten. 'Never' ersetzt 'not...ever'.

items.p: You want to know about your pen pal's experiences. Ask: '___ you ___ (ever / ride) a horse?'
items.c: Have you ever ridden a horse?
a: Have you ever ridden a horse?
Have you ever ridden a horse
items.h: Question: Have/Has + subject + ever + past participle?
items.hd: Frage: Have/Has + Subjekt + ever + Past Participle?
items.e: Present perfect question with 'ever': Have you ever ridden? 'Ever' goes before the past participle.
items.ed: Present-Perfect-Frage mit 'ever': Have you ever ridden? 'Ever' steht vor dem Past Participle.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ich habe noch nie einen Delfin gesehen.
items.c: I have never seen a dolphin.
a: I have never seen a dolphin.
I have never seen a dolphin
items.h: 'Noch nie' = 'never'. Remember: no 'not' with 'never'!
items.hd: 'Noch nie' = 'never'. Denke daran: kein 'not' zusammen mit 'never'!
items.e: German 'noch nie' = English 'never'. Position: have + never + past participle. No double negative!
items.ed: Deutsch 'noch nie' = Englisch 'never'. Position: have + never + Partizip. Keine doppelte Verneinung!

items.p: 🇩🇪 Hast du schon das neue Buch gelesen?
items.c: Have you already read the new book?
a: Have you already read the new book?
Have you already read the new book
Have you read the new book yet?
Have you read the new book yet
items.h: 'Schon' in a question can be 'already' (between have and participle) or 'yet' (at the end).
items.hd: 'Schon' in einer Frage kann 'already' (zwischen have und Partizip) oder 'yet' (am Ende) sein.
items.e: German 'schon' can be translated as 'already' or 'yet' in questions. Both positions are correct.
items.ed: Deutsch 'schon' kann in Fragen als 'already' oder 'yet' übersetzt werden. Beide Positionen sind korrekt.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: already / has / eaten / she / lunch
items.h: Subject + has + already + past participle + object.
items.hd: Subjekt + has + already + Partizip + Objekt.
items.e: Word order with 'already': Subject + has + already + past participle: She has already eaten lunch.
items.ed: Wortstellung mit 'already': Subjekt + has + already + Partizip: She has already eaten lunch.

items.p:
Match each signal word with its correct use:
1. already
2. yet
3. ever
4. never
a. questions about life experience (between have and pp)
b. positive sentences (between have and pp, = sooner than expected)
c. negative sentences and questions (at the end)
d. negative meaning without 'not' (between have and pp)
items.h: Think about which sentence types each word appears in: positive, negative, or questions.
items.hd: Überlege, in welchen Satztypen jedes Wort vorkommt: positiv, negativ oder Fragen.
items.e: already = positive (sooner than expected), yet = end of negatives/questions, ever = questions about experience, never = negative meaning (no 'not').
items.ed: already = positiv (früher als erwartet), yet = Ende von Verneinungen/Fragen, ever = Fragen über Erfahrung, never = negative Bedeutung (kein 'not').

items.p: Your friend asks: 'Do you want to see this film?' You saw it last week. Which answer uses present perfect correctly?
items.c: No thanks, I have already seen it.
a: No thanks, I have already seen it.
ds: No thanks, I already saw it.
No thanks, I have already saw it.
No thanks, I seen it already.
items.h: 'Already' goes between 'have' and the past participle. What's the past participle of 'see'?
items.hd: 'Already' steht zwischen 'have' und dem Past Participle. Was ist das Past Participle von 'see'?
items.e: Present perfect with 'already': I have already seen. Have + already + past participle (seen, not saw).
items.ed: Present Perfect mit 'already': I have already seen. Have + already + Past Participle (seen, nicht saw).

items.p: "I like pizza." — "So ___ I."
items.h: The original sentence uses present simple ('like'). What auxiliary does present simple use?
items.hd: Der Originalsatz verwendet Simple Present ('like'). Welches Hilfsverb benutzt Simple Present?
items.e: 'I like' is present simple, so the auxiliary is 'do': So do I.
items.ed: 'I like' ist Simple Present, also ist das Hilfsverb 'do': So do I.

items.p: "I can't swim." — "Neither ___ I."
items.h: Match the auxiliary to the original sentence. The original uses 'can't'.
items.hd: Passe das Hilfsverb an den Originalsatz an. Der Originalsatz verwendet 'can't'.
items.e: The original sentence uses 'can', so we use 'can' in the response: Neither can I. (No 'not' — 'neither' is already negative.)
items.ed: Der Originalsatz verwendet 'can', also verwenden wir 'can' in der Antwort: Neither can I. (Kein 'not' — 'neither' ist bereits negativ.)

items.p: "She has been to Rome." — "So ___ I."
items.h: The original uses 'has'. What form of 'have' goes with 'I'?
items.hd: Der Originalsatz verwendet 'has'. Welche Form von 'have' passt zu 'I'?
items.e: The original uses 'has' (present perfect). With 'I', we need 'have': So have I.
items.ed: Der Originalsatz verwendet 'has' (Present Perfect). Mit 'I' brauchen wir 'have': So have I.

items.p: "Tom doesn't like spiders." — "___ do I."
items.h: The original sentence is negative ('doesn't'). Use 'Neither' for negative agreement.
items.hd: Der Originalsatz ist negativ ('doesn't'). Verwende 'Neither' für negative Übereinstimmung.
items.e: For negative agreement, use 'Neither + auxiliary + I': Neither do I. ('So' is only for positive.)
items.ed: Für negative Übereinstimmung verwende 'Neither + Hilfsverb + I': Neither do I. ('So' ist nur für positiv.)

items.p: "My sister will travel to Spain." — "So ___ my brother."
items.h: The original sentence uses 'will'. The auxiliary must match!
items.hd: Der Originalsatz verwendet 'will'. Das Hilfsverb muss übereinstimmen!
items.e: The original uses 'will', so we match it: So will my brother.
items.ed: Der Originalsatz verwendet 'will', also passen wir es an: So will my brother.

items.p: "She doesn't have a pet." — "Neither ___ he."
items.h: 'He' needs the 3rd person form. And remember: 'neither' is already negative!
items.hd: 'He' braucht die 3. Person Form. Und denke daran: 'neither' ist bereits negativ!
items.e: The original uses 'doesn't' (= does + not). With 'neither' (already negative), use 'does': Neither does he.
items.ed: Der Originalsatz verwendet 'doesn't' (= does + not). Mit 'neither' (bereits negativ) verwende 'does': Neither does he.

items.p: "I love chocolate." — Which response means 'Me too'?
items.h: The structure is: So + auxiliary + subject. Make sure the auxiliary matches the original verb.
items.hd: Die Struktur ist: So + Hilfsverb + Subjekt. Das Hilfsverb muss zum Originalverb passen.
items.e: 'I love' = present simple → auxiliary is 'do'. So + do + I: So do I.
items.ed: 'I love' = Simple Present → Hilfsverb ist 'do'. So + do + I: So do I.

items.p: "I haven't finished the book." — Which is the correct agreement?
items.h: The original is negative. Use 'Neither' + auxiliary + subject. No extra 'not'!
items.hd: Der Originalsatz ist negativ. Verwende 'Neither' + Hilfsverb + Subjekt. Kein zusätzliches 'not'!
